author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Break, break, break,
line2: On thy cold gray stones, O Sea!

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: And I would that my tongue could utter
line2: The thoughts that arise in me.

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: O, well for the fisherman’s boy,
line2: That he shouts with his sister at play!

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: O, well for the sailor lad,
line2: That he sings in his boat on the bay!

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: And the stately ships go on
line2: To their haven under the hill;

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: But O for the touch of a vanished hand,
line2: And the sound of a voice that is still!

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Break, break, break,
line2: At the foot of thy crags, O Sea!

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: But the tender grace of a day that is dead
line2: Will never come back to me.

author: Robert Burns
line1: Farewell to the Highlands, farewell to the North,
line2: The birth-place of Valour, the country of Worth;

author: Robert Burns
line1: Wherever I wander, wherever I rove,
line2: The hills of the Highlands for ever I love.

author: Robert Burns
line1: My heart’s in the Highlands, my heart is not here;
line2: My heart’s in the Highlands a-chasing the deer;

author: Robert Burns
line1: A-chasing the wild-deer, and following the roe,
line2: My heart’s in the Highlands wherever I go.

author: Robert Burns
line1: Farewell to the mountains high covered with snow;
line2: Farewell to the straths and green valleys below;

author: Robert Burns
line1: Farewell to the forests and wild-hanging woods;
line2: Farewell to the torrents and loud-pouring floods.

author: Robert Burns
line1: My heart’s in the Highlands, my heart is not here;
line2: My heart’s in the Highlands a-chasing the deer;

author: Robert Burns
line1: A-chasing the wild-deer, and following the roe,
line2: My heart’s in the Highlands wherever I go.

poemTitle: I Wandered Lonely as a Cloud [Daffodils]
author: William Wordsworth
line1: I wandered lonely as a cloud
line2: That floats on high o’er vales and hills,

poemTitle: I Wandered Lonely as a Cloud [Daffodils]
author: William Wordsworth
line1: When all at once I saw a crowd,
line2: A host, of golden daffodils;

poemTitle: I Wandered Lonely as a Cloud [Daffodils]
author: William Wordsworth
line1: Beside the lake, beneath the trees,
line2: Fluttering and dancing in the breeze.

poemTitle: I Wandered Lonely as a Cloud [Daffodils]
author: William Wordsworth
line1: Continuous as the stars that shine
line2: And twinkle on the milky way,

poemTitle: I Wandered Lonely as a Cloud [Daffodils]
author: William Wordsworth
line1: They stretched in never-ending line
line2: Along the margin of a bay:

poemTitle: I Wandered Lonely as a Cloud [Daffodils]
author: William Wordsworth
line1: Ten thousand saw I at a glance,
line2: Tossing their heads in sprightly dance.

poemTitle: I Wandered Lonely as a Cloud [Daffodils]
author: William Wordsworth
line1: The waves beside them danced; but they
line2: Out-did the sparkling waves in glee:

poemTitle: I Wandered Lonely as a Cloud [Daffodils]
author: William Wordsworth
line1: A poet could not but be gay,
line2: In such a jocund company:

poemTitle: I Wandered Lonely as a Cloud [Daffodils]
author: William Wordsworth
line1: I gazed — and gazed — but little thought
line2: What wealth the show to me had brought:

poemTitle: I Wandered Lonely as a Cloud [Daffodils]
author: William Wordsworth
line1: For oft, when on my couch I lie
line2: In vacant or in pensive mood,

poemTitle: I Wandered Lonely as a Cloud [Daffodils]
author: William Wordsworth
line1: They flash upon that inward eye
line2: Which is the bliss of solitude;

poemTitle: I Wandered Lonely as a Cloud [Daffodils]
author: William Wordsworth
line1: And then my heart with pleasure fills,
line2: And dances with the daffodils.

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: In Xanadu did Kubla Khan
line2: A stately pleasure-dome decree:

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Where Alph, the sacred river, ran
line2: Through caverns measureless to man

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Down to a sunless sea.
line2: So twice five miles of fertile ground

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: With walls and towers were girdled round;
line2: And there were gardens bright with sinuous rills,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Where blossomed many an incense-bearing tree;
line2: And here were forests ancient as the hills,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Enfolding sunny spots of greenery.
line2: But oh! that deep romantic chasm which slanted

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Down the green hill athwart a cedarn cover!
line2: A savage place! as holy and enchanted

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: As e’er beneath a waning moon was haunted
line2: By woman wailing for her demon-lover!

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: And from this chasm, with ceaseless turmoil seething,
line2: As if this earth in fast thick pants were breathing,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: A mighty fountain momently was forced:
line2: Amid whose swift half-intermitted burst

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Huge fragments vaulted like rebounding hail,
line2: Or chaffy grain beneath the thresher’s flail:

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: And mid these dancing rocks at once and ever
line2: It flung up momently the sacred river.

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Five miles meandering with a mazy motion
line2: Through wood and dale the sacred river ran,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Then reached the caverns measureless to man,
line2: And sank in tumult to a lifeless ocean;

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: And ’mid this tumult Kubla heard from far
line2: Ancestral voices prophesying war!

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: The shadow of the dome of pleasure
line2: Floated midway on the waves;

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Where was heard the mingled measure
line2: From the fountain and the caves.

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: It was a miracle of rare device,
line2: A sunny pleasure-dome with caves of ice!

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: A damsel with a dulcimer
line2: In a vision once I saw:

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: It was an Abyssinian maid
line2: And on her dulcimer she played,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Singing of Mount Abora.
line2: Could I revive within me

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Her symphony and song,
line2: To such a deep delight ’twould win me,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: That with music loud and long,
line2: I would build that dome in air,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: That sunny dome! those caves of ice!
line2: And all who heard should see them there,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: And all should cry, Beware! Beware!
line2: His flashing eyes, his floating hair!

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Weave a circle round him thrice,
line2: And close your eyes with holy dread

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: For he on honey-dew hath fed,
line2: And drunk the milk of Paradise.

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: PART I
line2: It is an ancient Mariner,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: And he stoppeth one of three.
line2: ‘By thy long grey beard and glittering eye,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Now wherefore stopp’st thou me?
line2: The Bridegroom’s doors are opened wide,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: And I am next of kin;
line2: The guests are met, the feast is set:

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: May’st hear the merry din.’
line2: He holds him with his skinny hand,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: ‘There was a ship,’ quoth he.
line2: ‘Hold off! unhand me, grey-beard loon!’

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Eftsoons his hand dropt he.
line2: He holds him with his glittering eye—

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: The Wedding-Guest stood still,
line2: And listens like a three years’ child:

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: The Mariner hath his will.
line2: The Wedding-Guest sat on a stone:

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: He cannot choose but hear;
line2: And thus spake on that ancient man,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: The bright-eyed Mariner.
line2: ‘The ship was cheered, the harbour cleared,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Merrily did we drop
line2: Below the kirk, below the hill,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Below the lighthouse top.
line2: The Sun came up upon the left,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Out of the sea came he!
line2: And he shone bright, and on the right

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Went down into the sea.
line2: Higher and higher every day,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Till over the mast at noon—’
line2: The Wedding-Guest here beat his breast,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: For he heard the loud bassoon.
line2: The bride hath paced into the hall,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Red as a rose is she;
line2: Nodding their heads before her goes

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: The merry minstrelsy.
line2: The Wedding-Guest he beat his breast,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Yet he cannot choose but hear;
line2: And thus spake on that ancient man,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: The bright-eyed Mariner.
line2: And now the STORM-BLAST came, and he

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Was tyrannous and strong:
line2: He struck with his o’ertaking wings,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: And chased us south along.
line2: With sloping masts and dipping prow,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: As who pursued with yell and blow
line2: Still treads the shadow of his foe,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: And forward bends his head,
line2: The ship drove fast, loud roared the blast,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: And southward aye we fled.
line2: And now there came both mist and snow,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: And it grew wondrous cold:
line2: And ice, mast-high, came floating by,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: As green as emerald.
line2: And through the drifts the snowy clifts

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Did send a dismal sheen:
line2: Nor shapes of men nor beasts we ken—

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: The ice was all between.
line2: The ice was here, the ice was there,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: The ice was all around:
line2: It cracked and growled, and roared and howled,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Like noises in a swound!
line2: At length did cross an Albatross,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Thorough the fog it came;
line2: As if it had been a Christian soul,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: We hailed it in God’s name.
line2: It ate the food it ne’er had eat,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: And round and round it flew.
line2: The ice did split with a thunder-fit;

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: The helmsman steered us through!
line2: And a good south wind sprung up behind;

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: The Albatross did follow,
line2: And every day, for food or play,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Came to the mariner’s hollo!
line2: In mist or cloud, on mast or shroud,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: It perched for vespers nine;
line2: Whiles all the night, through fog-smoke white,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Glimmered the white Moon-shine.’
line2: ‘God save thee, ancient Mariner!

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: From the fiends, that plague thee thus!—
line2: Why look’st thou so?’—With my cross-bow

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: I shot the ALBATROSS.
line2: PART II

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: The Sun now rose upon the right:
line2: Out of the sea came he,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Still hid in mist, and on the left
line2: Went down into the sea.

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: And the good south wind still blew behind,
line2: But no sweet bird did follow,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Nor any day for food or play
line2: Came to the mariner’s hollo!

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: And I had done a hellish thing,
line2: And it would work ‘em woe:

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: For all averred, I had killed the bird
line2: That made the breeze to blow.

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Ah wretch! said they, the bird to slay,
line2: That made the breeze to blow!

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Nor dim nor red, like God’s own head,
line2: The glorious Sun uprist:

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Then all averred, I had killed the bird
line2: That brought the fog and mist.

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: ‘Twas right, said they, such birds to slay,
line2: That bring the fog and mist.

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: The fair breeze blew, the white foam flew,
line2: The furrow followed free;

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: We were the first that ever burst
line2: Into that silent sea.

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Down dropt the breeze, the sails dropt down,
line2: ‘Twas sad as sad could be;

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: And we did speak only to break
line2: The silence of the sea!

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: All in a hot and copper sky,
line2: The bloody Sun, at noon,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Right up above the mast did stand,
line2: No bigger than the Moon.

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Day after day, day after day,
line2: We stuck, nor breath nor motion;

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: As idle as a painted ship
line2: Upon a painted ocean.

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Water, water, every where,
line2: And all the boards did shrink;

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Water, water, every where,
line2: Nor any drop to drink.

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: The very deep did rot: O Christ!
line2: That ever this should be!

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Yea, slimy things did crawl with legs
line2: Upon the slimy sea.

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: About, about, in reel and rout
line2: The death-fires danced at night;

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: The water, like a witch’s oils,
line2: Burnt green, and blue and white.

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: And some in dreams assurèd were
line2: Of the Spirit that plagued us so;

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Nine fathom deep he had followed us
line2: From the land of mist and snow.

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: And every tongue, through utter drought,
line2: Was withered at the root;

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: We could not speak, no more than if
line2: We had been choked with soot.

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Ah! well a-day! what evil looks
line2: Had I from old and young!

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Instead of the cross, the Albatross
line2: About my neck was hung.

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: PART III
line2: There passed a weary time. Each throat

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Was parched, and glazed each eye.
line2: A weary time! a weary time!

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: How glazed each weary eye,
line2: When looking westward, I beheld

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: A something in the sky.
line2: At first it seemed a little speck,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: And then it seemed a mist;
line2: It moved and moved, and took at last

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: A certain shape, I wist.
line2: A speck, a mist, a shape, I wist!

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: And still it neared and neared:
line2: As if it dodged a water-sprite,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: It plunged and tacked and veered.
line2: With throats unslaked, with black lips baked,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: We could nor laugh nor wail;
line2: Through utter drought all dumb we stood!

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: I bit my arm, I sucked the blood,
line2: And cried, A sail! a sail!

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: With throats unslaked, with black lips baked,
line2: Agape they heard me call:

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Gramercy! they for joy did grin,
line2: And all at once their breath drew in.

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: As they were drinking all.
line2: See! see! (I cried) she tacks no more!

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Hither to work us weal;
line2: Without a breeze, without a tide,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: She steadies with upright keel!
line2: The western wave was all a-flame.

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: The day was well nigh done!
line2: Almost upon the western wave

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Rested the broad bright Sun;
line2: When that strange shape drove suddenly

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Betwixt us and the Sun.
line2: And straight the Sun was flecked with bars,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: (Heaven’s Mother send us grace!)
line2: As if through a dungeon-grate he peered

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: With broad and burning face.
line2: Alas! (thought I, and my heart beat loud)

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: How fast she nears and nears!
line2: Are those her sails that glance in the Sun,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Like restless gossameres?
line2: Are those her ribs through which the Sun

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Did peer, as through a grate?
line2: And is that Woman all her crew?

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Is that a DEATH? and are there two?
line2: Is DEATH that woman’s mate?

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Her lips were red, her looks were free,
line2: Her locks were yellow as gold:

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Her skin was as white as leprosy,
line2: The Night-mare LIFE-IN-DEATH was she,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Who thicks man’s blood with cold.
line2: The naked hulk alongside came,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: And the twain were casting dice;
line2: ‘The game is done! I’ve won! I’ve won!’

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Quoth she, and whistles thrice.
line2: The Sun’s rim dips; the stars rush out;

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: At one stride comes the dark;
line2: With far-heard whisper, o’er the sea,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Off shot the spectre-bark.
line2: We listened and looked sideways up!

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Fear at my heart, as at a cup,
line2: My life-blood seemed to sip!

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: The stars were dim, and thick the night,
line2: The steersman’s face by his lamp gleamed white;

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: From the sails the dew did drip—
line2: Till clomb above the eastern bar

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: The hornèd Moon, with one bright star
line2: Within the nether tip.

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: One after one, by the star-dogged Moon,
line2: Too quick for groan or sigh,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Each turned his face with a ghastly pang,
line2: And cursed me with his eye.

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Four times fifty living men,
line2: (And I heard nor sigh nor groan)

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: With heavy thump, a lifeless lump,
line2: They dropped down one by one.

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: The souls did from their bodies fly,—
line2: They fled to bliss or woe!

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: And every soul, it passed me by,
line2: Like the whizz of my cross-bow!

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: PART IV
line2: ‘I fear thee, ancient Mariner!

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: I fear thy skinny hand!
line2: And thou art long, and lank, and brown,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: As is the ribbed sea-sand.
line2: I fear thee and thy glittering eye,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: And thy skinny hand, so brown.’—
line2: Fear not, fear not, thou Wedding-Guest!

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: This body dropt not down.
line2: Alone, alone, all, all alone,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Alone on a wide wide sea!
line2: And never a saint took pity on

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: My soul in agony.
line2: The many men, so beautiful!

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: And they all dead did lie:
line2: And a thousand thousand slimy things

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Lived on; and so did I.
line2: I looked upon the rotting sea,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: And drew my eyes away;
line2: I looked upon the rotting deck,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: And there the dead men lay.
line2: I looked to heaven, and tried to pray;

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: But or ever a prayer had gusht,
line2: A wicked whisper came, and made

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: My heart as dry as dust.
line2: I closed my lids, and kept them close,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: And the balls like pulses beat;
line2: For the sky and the sea, and the sea and the sky

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Lay dead like a load on my weary eye,
line2: And the dead were at my feet.

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: The cold sweat melted from their limbs,
line2: Nor rot nor reek did they:

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: The look with which they looked on me
line2: Had never passed away.

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: An orphan’s curse would drag to hell
line2: A spirit from on high;

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: But oh! more horrible than that
line2: Is the curse in a dead man’s eye!

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Seven days, seven nights, I saw that curse,
line2: And yet I could not die.

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: The moving Moon went up the sky,
line2: And no where did abide:

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Softly she was going up,
line2: And a star or two beside—

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Her beams bemocked the sultry main,
line2: Like April hoar-frost spread;

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: But where the ship’s huge shadow lay,
line2: The charmèd water burnt alway

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: A still and awful red.
line2: Beyond the shadow of the ship,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: I watched the water-snakes:
line2: They moved in tracks of shining white,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: And when they reared, the elfish light
line2: Fell off in hoary flakes.

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Within the shadow of the ship
line2: I watched their rich attire:

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Blue, glossy green, and velvet black,
line2: They coiled and swam; and every track

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Was a flash of golden fire.
line2: O happy living things! no tongue

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Their beauty might declare:
line2: A spring of love gushed from my heart,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: And I blessed them unaware:
line2: Sure my kind saint took pity on me,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: And I blessed them unaware.
line2: The self-same moment I could pray;

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: And from my neck so free
line2: The Albatross fell off, and sank

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Like lead into the sea.
line2: PART V

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Oh sleep! it is a gentle thing,
line2: Beloved from pole to pole!

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: To Mary Queen the praise be given!
line2: She sent the gentle sleep from Heaven,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: That slid into my soul.
line2: The silly buckets on the deck,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: That had so long remained,
line2: I dreamt that they were filled with dew;

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: And when I awoke, it rained.
line2: My lips were wet, my throat was cold,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: My garments all were dank;
line2: Sure I had drunken in my dreams,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: And still my body drank.
line2: I moved, and could not feel my limbs:

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: I was so light—almost
line2: I thought that I had died in sleep,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: And was a blessed ghost.
line2: And soon I heard a roaring wind:

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: It did not come anear;
line2: But with its sound it shook the sails,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: That were so thin and sere.
line2: The upper air burst into life!

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: And a hundred fire-flags sheen,
line2: To and fro they were hurried about!

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: And to and fro, and in and out,
line2: The wan stars danced between.

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: And the coming wind did roar more loud,
line2: And the sails did sigh like sedge,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: And the rain poured down from one black cloud;
line2: The Moon was at its edge.

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: The thick black cloud was cleft, and still
line2: The Moon was at its side:

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Like waters shot from some high crag,
line2: The lightning fell with never a jag,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: A river steep and wide.
line2: The loud wind never reached the ship,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Yet now the ship moved on!
line2: Beneath the lightning and the Moon

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: The dead men gave a groan.
line2: They groaned, they stirred, they all uprose,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Nor spake, nor moved their eyes;
line2: It had been strange, even in a dream,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: To have seen those dead men rise.
line2: The helmsman steered, the ship moved on;

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Yet never a breeze up-blew;
line2: The mariners all ‘gan work the ropes,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Where they were wont to do;
line2: They raised their limbs like lifeless tools—

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: We were a ghastly crew.
line2: The body of my brother’s son

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Stood by me, knee to knee:
line2: The body and I pulled at one rope,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: But he said nought to me.
line2: ‘I fear thee, ancient Mariner!’

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Be calm, thou Wedding-Guest!
line2: ‘Twas not those souls that fled in pain,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Which to their corses came again,
line2: But a troop of spirits blest:

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: For when it dawned—they dropped their arms,
line2: And clustered round the mast;

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Sweet sounds rose slowly through their mouths,
line2: And from their bodies passed.

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Around, around, flew each sweet sound,
line2: Then darted to the Sun;

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Slowly the sounds came back again,
line2: Now mixed, now one by one.

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Sometimes a-dropping from the sky
line2: I heard the sky-lark sing;

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Sometimes all little birds that are,
line2: How they seemed to fill the sea and air

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: With their sweet jargoning!
line2: And now ‘twas like all instruments,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Now like a lonely flute;
line2: And now it is an angel’s song,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: That makes the heavens be mute.
line2: It ceased; yet still the sails made on

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: A pleasant noise till noon,
line2: A noise like of a hidden brook

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: In the leafy month of June,
line2: That to the sleeping woods all night

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Singeth a quiet tune.
line2: Till noon we quietly sailed on,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Yet never a breeze did breathe:
line2: Slowly and smoothly went the ship,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Moved onward from beneath.
line2: Under the keel nine fathom deep,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: From the land of mist and snow,
line2: The spirit slid: and it was he

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: That made the ship to go.
line2: The sails at noon left off their tune,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: And the ship stood still also.
line2: The Sun, right up above the mast,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Had fixed her to the ocean:
line2: But in a minute she ‘gan stir,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: With a short uneasy motion—
line2: Backwards and forwards half her length

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: With a short uneasy motion.
line2: Then like a pawing horse let go,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: She made a sudden bound:
line2: It flung the blood into my head,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: And I fell down in a swound.
line2: How long in that same fit I lay,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: I have not to declare;
line2: But ere my living life returned,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: I heard and in my soul discerned
line2: Two voices in the air.

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: ‘Is it he?’ quoth one, ‘Is this the man?
line2: By him who died on cross,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: With his cruel bow he laid full low
line2: The harmless Albatross.

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: The spirit who bideth by himself
line2: In the land of mist and snow,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: He loved the bird that loved the man
line2: Who shot him with his bow.’

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: The other was a softer voice,
line2: As soft as honey-dew:

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Quoth he, ‘The man hath penance done,
line2: And penance more will do.’

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: PART VI
line2: First Voice

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: ‘But tell me, tell me! speak again,
line2: Thy soft response renewing—

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: What makes that ship drive on so fast?
line2: What is the ocean doing?’

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Second Voice
line2: Still as a slave before his lord,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: The ocean hath no blast;
line2: His great bright eye most silently

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Up to the Moon is cast—
line2: If he may know which way to go;

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: For she guides him smooth or grim.
line2: See, brother, see! how graciously

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: She looketh down on him.’
line2: First Voice

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: ‘But why drives on that ship so fast,
line2: Without or wave or wind?’

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Second Voice
line2: ‘The air is cut away before,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: And closes from behind.
line2: Fly, brother, fly! more high, more high!

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Or we shall be belated:
line2: For slow and slow that ship will go,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: When the Mariner’s trance is abated.’
line2: I woke, and we were sailing on

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: As in a gentle weather:
line2: ‘Twas night, calm night, the moon was high;

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: The dead men stood together.
line2: All stood together on the deck,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: For a charnel-dungeon fitter:
line2: All fixed on me their stony eyes,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: That in the Moon did glitter.
line2: The pang, the curse, with which they died,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Had never passed away:
line2: I could not draw my eyes from theirs,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Nor turn them up to pray.
line2: And now this spell was snapt: once more

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: I viewed the ocean green,
line2: And looked far forth, yet little saw

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Of what had else been seen—
line2: Like one, that on a lonesome road

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Doth walk in fear and dread,
line2: And having once turned round walks on,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: And turns no more his head;
line2: Because he knows, a frightful fiend

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Doth close behind him tread.
line2: But soon there breathed a wind on me,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Nor sound nor motion made:
line2: Its path was not upon the sea,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: In ripple or in shade.
line2: It raised my hair, it fanned my cheek

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Like a meadow-gale of spring—
line2: It mingled strangely with my fears,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Yet it felt like a welcoming.
line2: Swiftly, swiftly flew the ship,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Yet she sailed softly too:
line2: Sweetly, sweetly blew the breeze—

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: On me alone it blew.
line2: Oh! dream of joy! is this indeed

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: The light-house top I see?
line2: Is this the hill? is this the kirk?

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Is this mine own countree?
line2: We drifted o’er the harbour-bar,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: And I with sobs did pray—
line2: O let me be awake, my God!

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Or let me sleep alway.
line2: The harbour-bay was clear as glass,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: So smoothly it was strewn!
line2: And on the bay the moonlight lay,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: And the shadow of the Moon.
line2: The rock shone bright, the kirk no less,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: That stands above the rock:
line2: The moonlight steeped in silentness

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: The steady weathercock.
line2: And the bay was white with silent light,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Till rising from the same,
line2: Full many shapes, that shadows were,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: In crimson colours came.
line2: A little distance from the prow

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Those crimson shadows were:
line2: I turned my eyes upon the deck—

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Oh, Christ! what saw I there!
line2: Each corse lay flat, lifeless and flat,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: And, by the holy rood!
line2: A man all light, a seraph-man,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: On every corse there stood.
line2: This seraph-band, each waved his hand:

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: It was a heavenly sight!
line2: They stood as signals to the land,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Each one a lovely light;
line2: This seraph-band, each waved his hand,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: No voice did they impart—
line2: No voice; but oh! the silence sank

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Like music on my heart.
line2: But soon I heard the dash of oars,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: I heard the Pilot’s cheer;
line2: My head was turned perforce away

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: And I saw a boat appear.
line2: The Pilot and the Pilot’s boy,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: I heard them coming fast:
line2: Dear Lord in Heaven! it was a joy

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: The dead men could not blast.
line2: I saw a third—I heard his voice:

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: It is the Hermit good!
line2: He singeth loud his godly hymns

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: That he makes in the wood.
line2: He’ll shrieve my soul, he’ll wash away

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: The Albatross’s blood.
line2: PART VII

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: This Hermit good lives in that wood
line2: Which slopes down to the sea.

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: How loudly his sweet voice he rears!
line2: He loves to talk with marineres

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: That come from a far countree.
line2: He kneels at morn, and noon, and eve—

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: He hath a cushion plump:
line2: It is the moss that wholly hides

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: The rotted old oak-stump.
line2: The skiff-boat neared: I heard them talk,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: ‘Why, this is strange, I trow!
line2: Where are those lights so many and fair,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: That signal made but now?’
line2: ‘Strange, by my faith!’ the Hermit said—

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: ‘And they answered not our cheer!
line2: The planks looked warped! and see those sails,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: How thin they are and sere!
line2: I never saw aught like to them,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Unless perchance it were
line2: Brown skeletons of leaves that lag

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: My forest-brook along;
line2: When the ivy-tod is heavy with snow,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: And the owlet whoops to the wolf below,
line2: That eats the she-wolf’s young.’

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: ‘Dear Lord! it hath a fiendish look—
line2: (The Pilot made reply)

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: I am a-feared’—’Push on, push on!’
line2: Said the Hermit cheerily.

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: The boat came closer to the ship,
line2: But I nor spake nor stirred;

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: The boat came close beneath the ship,
line2: And straight a sound was heard.

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Under the water it rumbled on,
line2: Still louder and more dread:

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: It reached the ship, it split the bay;
line2: The ship went down like lead.

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Stunned by that loud and dreadful sound,
line2: Which sky and ocean smote,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Like one that hath been seven days drowned
line2: My body lay afloat;

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: But swift as dreams, myself I found
line2: Within the Pilot’s boat.

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Upon the whirl, where sank the ship,
line2: The boat spun round and round;

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: And all was still, save that the hill
line2: Was telling of the sound.

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: I moved my lips—the Pilot shrieked
line2: And fell down in a fit;

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: The holy Hermit raised his eyes,
line2: And prayed where he did sit.

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: I took the oars: the Pilot’s boy,
line2: Who now doth crazy go,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Laughed loud and long, and all the while
line2: His eyes went to and fro.

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: ‘Ha! ha!’ quoth he, ‘full plain I see,
line2: The Devil knows how to row.’

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: And now, all in my own countree,
line2: I stood on the firm land!

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: The Hermit stepped forth from the boat,
line2: And scarcely he could stand.

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: ‘O shrieve me, shrieve me, holy man!’
line2: The Hermit crossed his brow.

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: ‘Say quick,’ quoth he, ‘I bid thee say—
line2: What manner of man art thou?’

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Forthwith this frame of mine was wrenched
line2: With a woful agony,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Which forced me to begin my tale;
line2: And then it left me free.

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Since then, at an uncertain hour,
line2: That agony returns:

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: And till my ghastly tale is told,
line2: This heart within me burns.

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: I pass, like night, from land to land;
line2: I have strange power of speech;

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: That moment that his face I see,
line2: I know the man that must hear me:

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: To him my tale I teach.
line2: What loud uproar bursts from that door!

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: The wedding-guests are there:
line2: But in the garden-bower the bride

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: And bride-maids singing are:
line2: And hark the little vesper bell,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Which biddeth me to prayer!
line2: O Wedding-Guest! this soul hath been

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Alone on a wide wide sea:
line2: So lonely ‘twas, that God himself

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Scarce seemèd there to be.
line2: O sweeter than the marriage-feast,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: ‘Tis sweeter far to me,
line2: To walk together to the kirk

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: With a goodly company!—
line2: To walk together to the kirk,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: And all together pray,
line2: While each to his great Father bends,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Old men, and babes, and loving friends
line2: And youths and maidens gay!

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Farewell, farewell! but this I tell
line2: To thee, thou Wedding-Guest!

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: He prayeth well, who loveth well
line2: Both man and bird and beast.

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: He prayeth best, who loveth best
line2: All things both great and small;

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: For the dear God who loveth us,
line2: He made and loveth all.

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: The Mariner, whose eye is bright,
line2: Whose beard with age is hoar,

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: Is gone: and now the Wedding-Guest
line2: Turned from the bridegroom’s door.

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: He went like one that hath been stunned,
line2: And is of sense forlorn:

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
line1: A sadder and a wiser man,
line2: He rose the morrow morn.

author: Patrick Barrington
line1: I had a Hippopotamus, I kept him in a shed
line2: And fed him upon vitamins and vegetable bread

author: Patrick Barrington
line1: I made him my companion on many cheery walks
line2: And had his portrait done by a celebrity in chalk

author: Patrick Barrington
line1: His charming eccentricities were known on every side
line2: The creatures’ popularity was wonderfully wide

author: Patrick Barrington
line1: He frolocked with the Rector in a dozen friendly tussles
line2: Who could not but remark on his hippopotamuscles

author: Patrick Barrington
line1: If he should be affected by depression or the dumps
line2: By hippopotameasles or the hippopotamumps

author: Patrick Barrington
line1: I never knew a particle of peace ‘till it was plain
line2: He was hippopotamasticating properly again

author: Patrick Barrington
line1: I had a Hippopotamus, I loved him as a friend
line2: But beautiful relationships are bound to have an end

author: Patrick Barrington
line1: Time takes alas! our joys from us and rids us of our blisses
line2: My hippopotamus turned out to be a hippopotamisses

author: Patrick Barrington
line1: My house keeper regarded him with jaundice in her eye
line2: She did not want a colony of hippotami

author: Patrick Barrington
line1: She borrowed a machine gun from from her soldier nephew, Percy
line2: And showed my hippopotamus no hippopotamercy

author: Patrick Barrington
line1: My house now lacks that glamour that the charming creature gave
line2: The garage where I kept him is now as silent as the grave

author: Patrick Barrington
line1: No longer he displays among the motor tyres and spanners
line2: His hippopomastery of hippopotamanners

author: Patrick Barrington
line1: No longer now he gambols in the orchards in the spring
line2: No longer do I lead him through the village on a string

author: Patrick Barrington
line1: No longer in the morning does the neighbourhood rejoice
line2: To his hippopotamusically-modulated voice.

author: Patrick Barrington
line1: I had a hippopotamus but nothing upon earth
line2: Is constant in its happines or lasting in its mirth

author: Patrick Barrington
line1: No joy that life can give me can be strong enough to smother
line2: My sorrow for that might-have-been-a-hippopota-mother

poemTitle: Stopping By Woods On A Snowy Evening
author: Robert Frost
line1: Whose woods these are I think I know.
line2: His house is in the village though;

poemTitle: Stopping By Woods On A Snowy Evening
author: Robert Frost
line1: He will not see me stopping here
line2: To watch his woods fill up with snow.

poemTitle: Stopping By Woods On A Snowy Evening
author: Robert Frost
line1: My little horse must think it queer
line2: To stop without a farmhouse near

poemTitle: Stopping By Woods On A Snowy Evening
author: Robert Frost
line1: Between the woods and frozen lake
line2: The darkest evening of the year.

poemTitle: Stopping By Woods On A Snowy Evening
author: Robert Frost
line1: He gives his harness bells a shake
line2: To ask if there is some mistake.

poemTitle: Stopping By Woods On A Snowy Evening
author: Robert Frost
line1: The only other sound’s the sweep
line2: Of easy wind and downy flake.

poemTitle: Stopping By Woods On A Snowy Evening
author: Robert Frost
line1: The woods are lovely, dark and deep.
line2: But I have promises to keep,

poemTitle: Stopping By Woods On A Snowy Evening
author: Robert Frost
line1: And miles to go before I sleep,
line2: And miles to go before I sleep.

author: Nissim Ezekiel
line1: Remember me? I am Professor Sheth.
line2: Once I taught you geography. Now

author: Nissim Ezekiel
line1: I am retired, though my health is good. My wife died some years
line2: back.

author: Nissim Ezekiel
line1: By God’s grace, all my children
line2: Are well settled in life.

author: Nissim Ezekiel
line1: One is Sales Manager,
line2: One is Bank Manager,

author: Nissim Ezekiel
line1: Both have cars.
line2: Other also doing well, though not so well.

author: Nissim Ezekiel
line1: Every family must have black sheep.
line2: Sarala and Tarala are married,

author: Nissim Ezekiel
line1: Their husbands are very nice boys.
line2: You won’t believe but I have eleven grandchildren.

author: Nissim Ezekiel
line1: How many issues you have? Three?
line2: That is good. These are days of family planning.

author: Nissim Ezekiel
line1: I am not against. We have to change with times.
line2: Whole world is changing. In India also

author: Nissim Ezekiel
line1: We are keeping up. Our progress is progressing.
line2: Old values are going, new values are coming.

author: Nissim Ezekiel
line1: Everything is happening with leaps and bounds.
line2: I am going out rarely, now and then

author: Nissim Ezekiel
line1: Only, this is price of old age
line2: But my health is O.K. Usual aches and pains.

author: Nissim Ezekiel
line1: No diabetes, no blood pressure, no heart attack.
line2: This is because of sound habits in youth.

author: Nissim Ezekiel
line1: How is your health keeping?
line2: Nicely? I am happy for that.

author: Nissim Ezekiel
line1: This year I am sixty-nine
line2: and hope to score a century.

author: Nissim Ezekiel
line1: You were so thin, like stick,
line2: Now you are man of weight and consequence.

author: Nissim Ezekiel
line1: That is good joke.
line2: If you are coming again this side by chance,

author: Nissim Ezekiel
line1: Visit please my humble residence also.
line2: I am living just on opposite house’s backside.

author: W.H. Auden
line1: Stop all the clocks, cut off the telephone,
line2: Prevent the dog from barking with a juicy bone,

author: W.H. Auden
line1: Silence the pianos and with muffled drum
line2: Bring out the coffin, let the mourners come.

author: W.H. Auden
line1: Let aeroplanes circle moaning overhead
line2: Scribbling on the sky the message ‘He is Dead’.

author: W.H. Auden
line1: Put crepe bows round the white necks of the public doves,
line2: Let the traffic policemen wear black cotton gloves.

author: W.H. Auden
line1: He was my North, my South, my East and West,
line2: My working week and my Sunday rest,

author: W.H. Auden
line1: My noon, my midnight, my talk, my song;
line2: I thought that love would last forever: I was wrong.

author: W.H. Auden
line1: The stars are not wanted now; put out every one,
line2: Pack up the moon and dismantle the sun,

author: W.H. Auden
line1: Pour away the ocean and sweep up the wood;
line2: For nothing now can ever come to any good.

poemTitle: To the Virgins, to Make Much of Time
author: Robert Herrick
line1: Gather ye rosebuds while ye may,
line2: Old Time is still a-flying;

poemTitle: To the Virgins, to Make Much of Time
author: Robert Herrick
line1: And this same flower that smiles today
line2: Tomorrow will be dying.

poemTitle: To the Virgins, to Make Much of Time
author: Robert Herrick
line1: The glorious lamp of heaven, the sun,
line2: The higher he’s a-getting,

poemTitle: To the Virgins, to Make Much of Time
author: Robert Herrick
line1: The sooner will his race be run,
line2: And nearer he’s to setting.

poemTitle: To the Virgins, to Make Much of Time
author: Robert Herrick
line1: That age is best which is the first,
line2: When youth and blood are warmer;

poemTitle: To the Virgins, to Make Much of Time
author: Robert Herrick
line1: But being spent, the worse, and worst
line2: Times still succeed the former.

poemTitle: To the Virgins, to Make Much of Time
author: Robert Herrick
line1: Then be not coy, but use your time,
line2: And while ye may, go marry;

poemTitle: To the Virgins, to Make Much of Time
author: Robert Herrick
line1: For having lost but once your prime,
line2: You may forever tarry.

author: William Blake
line1: The sun does arise,
line2: And make happy the skies.

author: William Blake
line1: The merry bells ring
line2: To welcome the spring.

author: William Blake
line1: The skylark and thrush,
line2: The birds of the bush,

author: William Blake
line1: Sing louder around,
line2: To the bells’ cheerful sound,

author: William Blake
line1: While our sports shall be seen
line2: On the echoing green.

author: William Blake
line1: Old John with white hair
line2: Does laugh away care,

author: William Blake
line1: Sitting under the oak,
line2: Among the old folk.

author: William Blake
line1: They laugh at our play,
line2: And soon they all say:

author: William Blake
line1: "Such, such were the joys
line2: When we all, girls and boys,

author: William Blake
line1: In our youth-time were seen
line2: On the echoing green."

author: William Blake
line1: Till the little ones weary
line2: No more can be merry;

author: William Blake
line1: The sun does descend,
line2: And our sports have an end.

author: William Blake
line1: Round the laps of their mother
line2: Many sisters and brothers,

author: William Blake
