author: Christina Rossetti
line1: Then must I knock, or call when just in sight?
line2: They will not keep you standing at that door.

author: Christina Rossetti
line1: Shall I find comfort, travel-sore and weak?
line2: Of labour you shall find the sum.

author: Christina Rossetti
line1: Will there be beds for me and all who seek?
line2: Yea, beds for all who come.

author: John Milton
line1: When I consider how my light is spent
line2: Ere half my days in this dark world and wide,

author: John Milton
line1: And that one talent which is death to hide
line2: Lodged with me useless, though my soul more bent

author: John Milton
line1: To serve therewith my Maker, and present
line2: My true account, lest he returning chide,

author: John Milton
line1: "Doth God exact day-labour, light denied?"
line2: I fondly ask. But Patience, to prevent

author: John Milton
line1: That murmur, soon replies: "God doth not need
line2: Either man’s work or his own gifts: who best

author: John Milton
line1: Bear his mild yoke, they serve him best. His state
line2: Is kingly; thousands at his bidding speed

author: John Milton
line1: And post o’er land and ocean without rest:
line2: They also serve who only stand and wait."

author: Nissim Ezekiel
line1: I remember the night my mother
line2: was stung by a scorpion. Ten hours

author: Nissim Ezekiel
line1: of steady rain had driven him
line2: to crawl beneath a sack of rice.

author: Nissim Ezekiel
line1: Parting with his poison - flash
line2: of diabolic tail in the dark room -

author: Nissim Ezekiel
line1: he risked the rain again.
line2: The peasants came like swarms of flies

author: Nissim Ezekiel
line1: and buzzed the name of God a hundred times
line2: to paralyse the Evil One.

author: Nissim Ezekiel
line1: With candles and with lanterns
line2: throwing giant scorpion shadows

author: Nissim Ezekiel
line1: on the mud-baked walls
line2: they searched for him: he was not found.

author: Nissim Ezekiel
line1: They clicked their tongues.
line2: With every movement that the scorpion made

author: Nissim Ezekiel
line1: his poison moved in Mother’s blood, they said.
line2: May he sit still, they said.

author: Nissim Ezekiel
line1: May the sins of your previous birth
line2: be burned away tonight, they said.

author: Nissim Ezekiel
line1: May your suffering decrease
line2: the misfortunes of your next birth, they said.

author: Nissim Ezekiel
line1: May the sum of all evil
line2: balanced in this unreal world

author: Nissim Ezekiel
line1: against the sum of good
line2: become diminished by your pain.

author: Nissim Ezekiel
line1: May the poison purify your flesh
line2: of desire, and your spirit of ambition,

author: Nissim Ezekiel
line1: they said, and they sat around
line2: on the floor with my mother in the centre,

author: Nissim Ezekiel
line1: the peace of understanding on each face.
line2: More candles, more lanterns, more neighbours,

author: Nissim Ezekiel
line1: more insects, and the endless rain.
line2: My mother twisted through and through,

author: Nissim Ezekiel
line1: groaning on a mat.
line2: My father, sceptic, rationalist,

author: Nissim Ezekiel
line1: trying every curse and blessing,
line2: powder, mixture, herb and hybrid.

author: Nissim Ezekiel
line1: He even poured a little paraffin
line2: upon the bitten toe and put a match to it.

author: Nissim Ezekiel
line1: I watched the flame feeding on my mother.
line2: I watched the holy man perform his rites

author: Nissim Ezekiel
line1: to tame the poison with an incantation.
line2: After twenty hours

author: Nissim Ezekiel
line1: it lost its sting.
line2: My mother only said

author: Nissim Ezekiel
line1: Thank God the scorpion picked on me
line2: And spared my children.

author: William Shakespeare
line1: All the world’s a stage,
line2: And all the men and women merely players:

author: William Shakespeare
line1: They have their exits and entrances;
line2: And one man in his time plays many parts,

author: William Shakespeare
line1: His acts being seven ages. At first the infant,
line2: Mewling and puking in the nurse’s arms.

author: William Shakespeare
line1: Then, the whining schoolboy with his satchel
line2: And shining morning face, creeping like snail

author: William Shakespeare
line1: Unwillingly to school. And then the lover,
line2: Sighing like furnace, with a woeful ballad

author: William Shakespeare
line1: Made to his mistress’ eyebrow. Then a soldier,
line2: Full of strange oaths, and bearded like the pard,

author: William Shakespeare
line1: Jealous in honour, sudden, and quick in quarrel,
line2: Seeking the bubble reputation

author: William Shakespeare
line1: Even in the cannon’s mouth. And then the justice
line2: In fair round belly, with good capon lin’d,

author: William Shakespeare
line1: With eyes severe, and beard of formal cut,
line2: Full of wise saws, and modern instances;

author: William Shakespeare
line1: And so he plays his part.
line2: The sixth age shifts

author: William Shakespeare
line1: Into the lean and slipper’d pantaloon,
line2: With spectacles on nose, and pouch on side,

author: William Shakespeare
line1: His youthful hose well sav’d, a world too wide,
line2: For his shrunk shank, and his big manly voice,

author: William Shakespeare
line1: Turning again towards childish treble, pipes
line2: And whistles in his sound. Last scene of all,

author: William Shakespeare
line1: That ends this strange eventful history,
line2: Is second childishness and mere oblivion,

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: Under a spreading chestnut-tree
line2: The village smithy stands;

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: The smith, a mighty man is he,
line2: With large and sinewy hands;

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: And the muscles of his brawny arms
line2: Are strong as iron bands.

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: His hair is crisp, and black, and long,
line2: His face is like the tan;

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: His brow is wet with honest sweat,
line2: He earns whate’er he can,

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: And looks the whole world in the face,
line2: For he owes not any man.

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: Week in, week out, from morn till night,
line2: You can hear his bellows blow;

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: You can hear him swing his heavy sledge,
line2: With measured beat and slow,

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: Like a sexton ringing the village bell,
line2: When the evening sun is low.

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: And children coming home from school
line2: Look in at the open door;

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: They love to see the flaming forge,
line2: And hear the bellows roar,

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: And catch the burning sparks that fly
line2: Like chaff from a threshing-floor.

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: He goes on Sunday to the church,
line2: And sits among his boys;

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: He hears the parson pray and preach,
line2: He hears his daughter’s voice,

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: Singing in the village choir,
line2: And it makes his heart rejoice.

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: It sounds to him like her mother’s voice,
line2: Singing in Paradise!

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: He needs must think of her once more,
line2: How in the grave she lies;

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: And with his hard, rough hand he wipes
line2: A tear out of his eyes.

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: Toiling,—rejoicing,—sorrowing,
line2: Onward through life he goes;

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: Each morning sees some task begin,
line2: Each evening sees it close;

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: Something attempted, something done,
line2: Has earned a night’s repose.

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: Thanks, thanks to thee, my worthy friend,
line2: For the lesson thou hast taught!

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: Thus at the flaming forge of life
line2: Our fortunes must be wrought;

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: Thus on its sounding anvil shaped
line2: Each burning deed and thought.

author: W.H. Auden
line1: Let me tell you a little story
line2: About Miss Edith Gee;

author: W.H. Auden
line1: She lived in Clevedon Terrace
line2: At number 83.

author: W.H. Auden
line1: She’d a slight squint in her left eye,
line2: Her lips they were thin and small,

author: W.H. Auden
line1: She had narrow sloping shoulders
line2: And she had no bust at all.

author: W.H. Auden
line1: She’d a velvet hat with trimmings,
line2: And a dark grey serge costume;

author: W.H. Auden
line1: She lived in Clevedon Terrace
line2: In a small bed-sitting room.

author: W.H. Auden
line1: She’d a purple mac for wet days,
line2: A green umbrella too to take,

author: W.H. Auden
line1: She’d a bicycle with shopping basket
line2: And a harsh back-pedal break.

author: W.H. Auden
line1: The Church of Saint Aloysius
line2: Was not so very far;

author: W.H. Auden
line1: She did a lot of knitting,
line2: Knitting for the Church Bazaar.

author: W.H. Auden
line1: Miss Gee looked up at the starlight
line2: And said, ‘Does anyone care

author: W.H. Auden
line1: That I live on Clevedon Terrace
line2: On one hundred pounds a year?’

author: W.H. Auden
line1: She dreamed a dream one evening
line2: That she was the Queen of France

author: W.H. Auden
line1: And the Vicar of Saint Aloysius
line2: Asked Her Majesty to dance.

author: W.H. Auden
line1: But a storm blew down the palace,
line2: She was biking through a field of corn,

author: W.H. Auden
line1: And a bull with the face of the Vicar
line2: Was charging with lowered horn.

author: W.H. Auden
line1: She could feel his hot breath behind her,
line2: He was going to overtake;

author: W.H. Auden
line1: And the bicycle went slower and slower
line2: Because of that back-pedal break.

author: W.H. Auden
line1: Summer made the trees a picture,
line2: Winter made them a wreck;

author: W.H. Auden
line1: She bicycled to the evening service
line2: With her clothes buttoned up to her neck.

author: W.H. Auden
line1: She passed by the loving couples,
line2: She turned her head away;

author: W.H. Auden
line1: She passed by the loving couples,
line2: And they didn’t ask her to stay.

author: W.H. Auden
line1: Miss Gee sat in the side-aisle,
line2: She heard the organ play;

author: W.H. Auden
line1: And the choir sang so sweetly
line2: At the ending of the day,

author: W.H. Auden
line1: Miss Gee knelt down in the side-aisle,
line2: She knelt down on her knees;

author: W.H. Auden
line1: ‘Lead me not into temptation
line2: But make me a good girl, please.’

author: W.H. Auden
line1: The days and nights went by her
line2: Like waves round a Cornish wreck;

author: W.H. Auden
line1: She bicycled down to the doctor
line2: With her clothes buttoned up to her neck.

author: W.H. Auden
line1: She bicycled down to the doctor,
line2: And rang the surgery bell;

author: W.H. Auden
line1: ‘O, doctor, I’ve a pain inside me,
line2: And I don’t feel very well.’

author: W.H. Auden
line1: Doctor Thomas looked her over,
line2: And then he looked some more;

author: W.H. Auden
line1: Walked over to his wash-basin,
line2: Said,’Why didn’t you come before?’

author: W.H. Auden
line1: Doctor Thomas sat over his dinner,
line2: Though his wife was waiting to ring,

author: W.H. Auden
line1: Rolling his bread into pellets;
line2: Said, ‘Cancer’s a funny thing.

author: W.H. Auden
line1: ‘Nobody knows what the cause is,
line2: Though some pretend they do;

author: W.H. Auden
line1: It’s like some hidden assassin
line2: Waiting to strike at you.

author: W.H. Auden
line1: ‘Childless women get it.
line2: And men when they retire;

author: W.H. Auden
line1: It’s as if there had to be some outlet
line2: For their foiled creative fire.’

author: W.H. Auden
line1: His wife she rang for the servent,
line2: Said, ‘Dont be so morbid, dear’;

author: W.H. Auden
line1: He said: ‘I saw Miss Gee this evening
line2: And she’s a goner, I fear.’

author: W.H. Auden
line1: They took Miss Gee to the hospital,
line2: She lay there a total wreck,

author: W.H. Auden
line1: Lay in the ward for women
line2: With her bedclothes right up to her neck.

author: W.H. Auden
line1: They lay her on the table,
line2: The students began to laugh;

author: W.H. Auden
line1: And Mr. Rose the surgeon
line2: He cut Miss Gee in half.

author: W.H. Auden
line1: Mr. Rose he turned to his students,
line2: Said, ‘Gentlemen if you please,

author: W.H. Auden
line1: We seldom see a sarcoma
line2: As far advanced as this.’

author: W.H. Auden
line1: They took her off the table,
line2: They wheeled away Miss Gee

author: W.H. Auden
line1: Down to another department
line2: Where they study Anatomy.

author: W.H. Auden
line1: They hung her from the ceiling
line2: Yes, they hung up Miss Gee;

author: W.H. Auden
line1: And a couple of Oxford Groupers
line2: Carefully dissected her knee.

author: T.S. Eliot
line1: Macavity’s a Mystery Cat: he’s called the Hidden Paw -
line2: For he’s the master criminal who can defy the Law.

author: T.S. Eliot
line1: He’s the bafflement of Scotland Yard, the Flying Squad’s despair:
line2: For when they reach the scene of crime - Macavity’s not there!

author: T.S. Eliot
line1: Macavity, Macavity, there’s no one like Macavity,
line2: He’s broken every human law, he breaks the law of gravity.

author: T.S. Eliot
line1: His powers of levitation would make a fakir stare,
line2: And when you reach the scene of crime - Macavity’s not there!

author: T.S. Eliot
line1: You may seek him in the basement, you may look up in the air -
line2: But I tell you once and once again, Macavity’s not there!

author: T.S. Eliot
line1: Mcavity’s a ginger cat, he’s very tall and thin;
line2: You would know him if you saw him, for his eyes are sunken in.

author: T.S. Eliot
line1: His brow is deeply lined with thought, his head is highly domed;
line2: His coat is dusty from neglect, his whiskers are uncombed.

author: T.S. Eliot
line1: He sways his head from side to side, with movements like a snake;
line2: And when you think he’s half asleep, he’s always wide awake.

author: T.S. Eliot
line1: Macavity, Macavity, there’s no one like Macavity,
line2: For he’s a fiend in feline shape, a monster of depravity.

author: T.S. Eliot
line1: You may meet him in a by-street, you may see him in the square -
line2: But when a crime’s discovered, then Macavity’s not there!

author: T.S. Eliot
line1: He’s outwardly respectable. (They say he cheats at cards.)
line2: And his footprints are not found in any file of Scotland Yard’s.

author: T.S. Eliot
line1: And when the larder’s looted, or the jewel-case is rifled,
line2: Or when the milk is missing, or another Peke’s been stifled,

author: T.S. Eliot
line1: Or the greenhouse glass is broken, and the trellis past repair -
line2: Ay, there’s the wonder of the thing! Macavity’s not there!

author: T.S. Eliot
line1: And when the Foreign Office find a Treaty’s gone astray,
line2: Or the Admiralty lose some plans and drawings by the way,

author: T.S. Eliot
line1: There may be a scrap of paper in the hall or on the stair -
line2: But it’s useless to investigate - Mcavity’s not there!

author: T.S. Eliot
line1: And when the loss has been disclosed, the Secret Service say:
line2: `It must have been Macavity!’ - but he’s a mile away.

author: T.S. Eliot
line1: You’ll be sure to find him resting, or a-licking of his thumbs,
line2: Or engaged in doing complicated long-division sums.

author: T.S. Eliot
line1: Macavity, Macavity, there’s no one like Macavity,
line2: There never was a Cat of such deceitfulness and suavity.

author: T.S. Eliot
line1: He always has an alibi, and one or two to spaer:
line2: At whatever time the deed took place - MACAVITY WASN’T THERE!

author: T.S. Eliot
line1: And they say that all the Cats whose wicked deeds are widely known
line2: (I might mention Mungojerrie, I might mention Griddlebone)

author: T.S. Eliot
line1: Are nothing more than agents for the Cat who all the time
line2: Just controls their operations: the Napoleon of Crime!

author: Robert Frost
line1: Nature’s first green is gold,
line2: Her hardest hue to hold.

author: Robert Frost
line1: Her early leaf’s a flower;
line2: But only so an hour.

author: Robert Frost
line1: Then leaf subsides to leaf.
line2: So Eden sank to grief,

author: Robert Frost
line1: So dawn goes down to day.
line2: Nothing gold can stay.

author: Lewis Carroll
line1: The sun was shining on the sea,
line2: Shining with all his might:

author: Lewis Carroll
line1: He did his very best to make
line2: The billows smooth and bright –

author: Lewis Carroll
line1: And this was odd, because it was
line2: The middle of the night.

author: Lewis Carroll
line1: The moon was shining sulkily,
line2: Because she thought the sun

author: Lewis Carroll
line1: Had got no business to be there
line2: After the day was done –

author: Lewis Carroll
line1: ‘It’s very rude of him.’ she said,
line2: ‘To come and spoil the fun!’

author: Lewis Carroll
line1: The sea was wet as wet could be,
line2: The sands were dry as dry.

author: Lewis Carroll
line1: You could not see a cloud, because
line2: No cloud was in the sky:

author: Lewis Carroll
line1: No birds were flying overhead –
line2: There were no birds to fly.

author: Lewis Carroll
line1: The Walrus and the Carpenter
line2: Were walking close at hand:

author: Lewis Carroll
line1: They wept like anything to see
line2: Such quantities of sand:

author: Lewis Carroll
line1: ‘If this were only cleared away,’
line2: They said, ‘it would be grand.’

author: Lewis Carroll
line1: ‘If seven maids with seven mops
line2: Swept it for half a year,

author: Lewis Carroll
line1: Do you suppose,’ the Walrus said,
line2: ‘That they could get it clear?’

author: Lewis Carroll
line1: ‘I doubt it,’ said the Carpenter,
line2: And shed a bitter tear.

author: Lewis Carroll
line1: ‘O Oysters, come and walk with us!
line2: The Walrus did beseech.

author: Lewis Carroll
line1: ‘A pleasant walk, a pleasant talk,
line2: Along the briny beach:

author: Lewis Carroll
line1: We cannot do with more than four,
line2: To give a hand to each.’

author: Lewis Carroll
line1: The eldest Oyster looked at him,
line2: But never a word he said:

author: Lewis Carroll
line1: The eldest Oyster winked his eye,
line2: And shook his heavy head –

author: Lewis Carroll
line1: Meaning to say he did not choose
line2: To leave the oyster-bed.

author: Lewis Carroll
line1: Out four young Oysters hurried up.
line2: All eager for the treat:

author: Lewis Carroll
line1: Their coats were brushed, their faces washed,
line2: Their shoes were clean and neat –

author: Lewis Carroll
line1: And this was odd, because, you know,
line2: They hadn’t any feet.

author: Lewis Carroll
line1: Four other Oysters followed them,
line2: And yet another four;

author: Lewis Carroll
line1: And thick and fast they came at last,
line2: And more, and more, and more –

author: Lewis Carroll
line1: All hopping through the frothy waves,
line2: And scrambling to the shore.

author: Lewis Carroll
line1: The Walrus and the Carpenter
line2: Walked on a mile or so,

author: Lewis Carroll
line1: And then they rested on a rock
line2: Conveniently low:

author: Lewis Carroll
line1: And all the little Oysters stood
line2: And waited in a row.

author: Lewis Carroll
line1: ‘The time has come,’ the Walrus said,
line2: ‘To talk of many things:

author: Lewis Carroll
line1: Of shoes – and ships – and sealing wax –
line2: Of cabbages – and kings –

author: Lewis Carroll
line1: And why the sea is boiling hot –
line2: And whether pigs have wings.’

author: Lewis Carroll
line1: ‘But wait a bit,’ the Oysters cried,
line2: ‘Before we have our chat;

author: Lewis Carroll
line1: For some of us are out of breath,
line2: And all of us are fat!’

author: Lewis Carroll
line1: ‘No hurry!’ said the Carpenter.
line2: They thanked him much for that.

author: Lewis Carroll
line1: ‘A loaf of bread,’ the Walrus said,
line2: ‘Is what we chiefly need:

author: Lewis Carroll
line1: Pepper and vinegar besides
line2: Are very good indeed –

author: Lewis Carroll
line1: Now, if you’re ready, Oysters dear,
line2: We can begin to feed.’

author: Lewis Carroll
line1: ‘But not on us!’ the Oysters cried,
line2: Turning a little blue.

author: Lewis Carroll
line1: ‘After such kindness, that would be
line2: A dismal thing to do!’

author: Lewis Carroll
line1: ‘The night is fine,’ the Walrus said,
line2: ‘Do you admire the view?’

author: Lewis Carroll
line1: ‘It was so kind of you to come!
line2: And you are very nice!’

author: Lewis Carroll
line1: The Carpenter said nothing but
line2: ‘Cut us another slice-

author: Lewis Carroll
line1: I wish you were not quite so deaf-
line2: I’ve had to ask you twice!’

author: Lewis Carroll
line1: ‘It seems a shame,’ the Walrus said,
line2: ‘To play them such a trick.

author: Lewis Carroll
line1: After we’ve brought them out so far,
line2: And made them trot so quick!’

author: Lewis Carroll
line1: The Carpenter said nothing but
line2: ‘The butter’s spread too thick!’

author: Lewis Carroll
line1: ‘I weep for you,’the Walrus said:
line2: ‘I deeply sympathize.’

author: Lewis Carroll
line1: With sobs and tears he sorted out
line2: Those of the largest size,

author: Lewis Carroll
line1: Holding his pocket-handkerchief
line2: Before his streaming eyes.

author: Lewis Carroll
line1: ‘O Oysters,’ said the Carpenter,
line2: ‘You’ve had a pleasant run!

author: Lewis Carroll
line1: Shall we be trotting home again?’
line2: But answer came there none –

author: Lewis Carroll
line1: And this was scarcely odd, because
line2: They’d eaten every one.

author: David Herbert Lawrence
line1: When did you start your tricks
line2: Monsieur?

author: David Herbert Lawrence
line1: What do you stand on such high legs for?
line2: Why this length of shredded shank

author: David Herbert Lawrence
line1: You exaltation?
line2: Is it so that you shall lift your centre of gravity upwards

author: David Herbert Lawrence
line1: And weigh no more than air as you alight upon me,
line2: Stand upon me weightless, you phantom?

author: David Herbert Lawrence
line1: I heard a woman call you the Winged Victory
line2: In sluggish Venice.

author: David Herbert Lawrence
line1: You turn your head towards your tail, and smile.
line2: How can you put so much devilry

author: David Herbert Lawrence
line1: Into that translucent phantom shred
line2: Of a frail corpus?

author: David Herbert Lawrence
line1: Queer, with your thin wings and your streaming legs
line2: How you sail like a heron, or a dull clot of air,

author: David Herbert Lawrence
line1: A nothingness.
line2: Yet what an aura surrounds you;

author: David Herbert Lawrence
line1: Your evil little aura, prowling, and casting a numbness on my mind.
line2: That is your trick, your bit of filthy magic:

author: David Herbert Lawrence
line1: Invisibility, and the anæsthetic power
line2: To deaden my attention in your direction.

author: David Herbert Lawrence
line1: But I know your game now, streaky sorcerer.
line2: Queer, how you stalk and prowl the air

author: David Herbert Lawrence
line1: In circles and evasions, enveloping me,
line2: Ghoul on wings

author: David Herbert Lawrence
line1: Winged Victory.
line2: Settle, and stand on long thin shanks

author: David Herbert Lawrence
line1: Eyeing me sideways, and cunningly conscious that I am aware,
line2: You speck.

author: David Herbert Lawrence
line1: I hate the way you lurch off sideways into air
line2: Having read my thoughts against you.

author: David Herbert Lawrence
line1: Come then, let us play at unawares,
line2: And see who wins in this sly game of bluff.

author: David Herbert Lawrence
line1: Man or mosquito.
line2: You don’t know that I exist, and I don’t know that you exist.

author: David Herbert Lawrence
line1: Now then!
line2: It is your trump

author: David Herbert Lawrence
line1: It is your hateful little trump
line2: You pointed fiend,

author: David Herbert Lawrence
line1: Which shakes my sudden blood to hatred of you:
line2: It is your small, high, hateful bugle in my ear.

author: David Herbert Lawrence
line1: Why do you do it?
line2: Surely it is bad policy.

author: David Herbert Lawrence
line1: They say you can’t help it.
line2: If that is so, then I believe a little in Providence protecting the

author: David Herbert Lawrence
line1: innocent.
line2: But it sounds so amazingly like a slogan

author: David Herbert Lawrence
line1: A yell of triumph as you snatch my scalp.
line2: Blood, red blood

author: David Herbert Lawrence
line1: Super-magical
line2: Forbidden liquor.

author: David Herbert Lawrence
line1: I behold you stand
line2: For a second enspasmed in oblivion,

author: David Herbert Lawrence
line1: Obscenely ecstasied
line2: Sucking live blood

author: David Herbert Lawrence
line1: My blood.
line2: Such silence, such suspended transport,

author: David Herbert Lawrence
line1: Such gorging,
line2: Such obscenity of trespass.

author: David Herbert Lawrence
line1: You stagger
line2: As well as you may.

author: David Herbert Lawrence
line1: Only your accursed hairy frailty
line2: Your own imponderable weightlessness

author: David Herbert Lawrence
line1: Saves you, wafts you away on the very draught my anger makes in
line2: its snatching.

author: David Herbert Lawrence
line1: Away with a pæan of derision
line2: You winged blood-drop.

author: David Herbert Lawrence
line1: Can I not overtake you?
line2: Are you one too many for me

author: David Herbert Lawrence
line1: Winged Victory?
line2: Am I not mosquito enough to out-mosquito you?

author: David Herbert Lawrence
line1: Queer, what a big stain my sucked blood makes
line2: Beside the infinitesimal faint smear of you!

poemTitle: Jerusalem [And did those feet in ancient time]
author: William Blake
line1: And did those feet in ancient time
line2: Walk upon Englands mountains green:

poemTitle: Jerusalem [And did those feet in ancient time]
author: William Blake
line1: And was the holy Lamb of God,
line2: On Englands pleasant pastures seen!

poemTitle: Jerusalem [And did those feet in ancient time]
author: William Blake
line1: And did the Countenance Divine,
line2: Shine forth upon our clouded hills?

poemTitle: Jerusalem [And did those feet in ancient time]
author: William Blake
line1: And was Jerusalem builded here,
line2: Among these dark Satanic Mills?

poemTitle: Jerusalem [And did those feet in ancient time]
author: William Blake
line1: Bring me my Bow of burning gold:
line2: Bring me my arrows of desire:

poemTitle: Jerusalem [And did those feet in ancient time]
author: William Blake
line1: Bring me my Spear: O clouds unfold!
line2: Bring me my Chariot of fire!

poemTitle: Jerusalem [And did those feet in ancient time]
author: William Blake
line1: I will not cease from Mental Fight,
line2: Nor shall my sword sleep in my hand:

poemTitle: Jerusalem [And did those feet in ancient time]
author: William Blake
line1: Till we have built Jerusalem,
line2: In Englands green & pleasant Land.

author: Robert Southey
line1: It was a summer evening,
line2: Old Kaspar’s work was done,

author: Robert Southey
line1: And he before his cottage door
line2: Was sitting in the sun,

author: Robert Southey
line1: And by him sported on the green
line2: His little grandchild Wilhelmine.

author: Robert Southey
line1: She saw her brother Peterkin
line2: Roll something large and round,

author: Robert Southey
line1: Which he beside the rivulet
line2: In playing there had found;

author: Robert Southey
line1: He came to ask what he had found,
line2: That was so large, and smooth, and round.

author: Robert Southey
line1: Old Kaspar took it from the boy,
line2: Who stood expectant by;

author: Robert Southey
line1: And then the old man shook his head,
line2: And, with a natural sigh,

author: Robert Southey
line1: "‘Tis some poor fellow’s skull," said he,
line2: "Who fell in the great victory.

author: Robert Southey
line1: "I find them in the garden,
line2: For there’s many here about;

author: Robert Southey
line1: And often when I go to plough,
line2: The ploughshare turns them out!

author: Robert Southey
line1: For many thousand men," said he,
line2: "Were slain in that great victory."

author: Robert Southey
line1: "Now tell us what ‘twas all about,"
line2: Young Peterkin, he cries;

author: Robert Southey
line1: And little Wilhelmine looks up
line2: With wonder-waiting eyes;

author: Robert Southey
line1: "Now tell us all about the war,
line2: And what they fought each other for."

author: Robert Southey
line1: "It was the English," Kaspar cried,
line2: "Who put the French to rout;

author: Robert Southey
line1: But what they fought each other for,
line2: I could not well make out;

author: Robert Southey
line1: But everybody said," quoth he,
line2: "That ‘twas a famous victory.

author: Robert Southey
line1: "My father lived at Blenheim then,
line2: Yon little stream hard by;

author: Robert Southey
line1: They burnt his dwelling to the ground,
line2: And he was forced to fly;

author: Robert Southey
line1: So with his wife and child he fled,
line2: Nor had he where to rest his head.

author: Robert Southey
line1: "With fire and sword the country round
line2: Was wasted far and wide,

author: Robert Southey
line1: And many a childing mother then,
line2: And new-born baby died;

author: Robert Southey
line1: But things like that, you know, must be
line2: At every famous victory.

author: Robert Southey
line1: "They say it was a shocking sight
line2: After the field was won;

author: Robert Southey
line1: For many thousand bodies here
line2: Lay rotting in the sun;

author: Robert Southey
line1: But things like that, you know, must be
line2: After a famous victory.

author: Robert Southey
line1: "Great praise the Duke of Marlbro’ won,
line2: And our good Prince Eugene."

author: Robert Southey
line1: "Why, ‘twas a very wicked thing!"
line2: Said little Wilhelmine.

author: Robert Southey
line1: "Nay… nay… my little girl," quoth he,
line2: "It was a famous victory.

author: Robert Southey
line1: "And everybody praised the Duke
line2: Who this great fight did win."

author: Robert Southey
line1: "But what good came of it at last?"
line2: Quoth little Peterkin.

author: Robert Southey
line1: "Why that I cannot tell," said he,
line2: "But ‘twas a famous victory."

poemTitle: The Burial of Sir John Moore at Corunna
author: Charles Wolfe
line1: Not a drum was heard, nor a funeral note,
line2: As his corse to the rampart we hurried;

poemTitle: The Burial of Sir John Moore at Corunna
author: Charles Wolfe
line1: Not a soldier discharged his farewell shot
line2: O’er the grave where our hero we buried.

poemTitle: The Burial of Sir John Moore at Corunna
author: Charles Wolfe
line1: We buried him darkly at dead of night,
line2: The sods with our bayonets turning;

poemTitle: The Burial of Sir John Moore at Corunna
author: Charles Wolfe
line1: By the struggling moonbeam’s misty light
line2: And the lanthorn dimly burning.

poemTitle: The Burial of Sir John Moore at Corunna
author: Charles Wolfe
line1: No useless coffin enclosed his breast,
line2: Nor in sheet nor in shroud we wound him;

poemTitle: The Burial of Sir John Moore at Corunna
author: Charles Wolfe
line1: But he lay like a warrior taking his rest
line2: With his martial cloak around him.

poemTitle: The Burial of Sir John Moore at Corunna
author: Charles Wolfe
line1: Few and short were the prayers we said,
line2: And we spoke not a word of sorrow;

poemTitle: The Burial of Sir John Moore at Corunna
author: Charles Wolfe
line1: But we steadfastly gazed on the face that was dead,
line2: And we bitterly thought of the morrow.

poemTitle: The Burial of Sir John Moore at Corunna
author: Charles Wolfe
line1: We thought, as we hollowed his narrow bed
line2: And smoothed down his lonely pillow,

poemTitle: The Burial of Sir John Moore at Corunna
author: Charles Wolfe
line1: That the foe and the stranger would tread o’er his head,
line2: And we far away on the billow!

poemTitle: The Burial of Sir John Moore at Corunna
author: Charles Wolfe
line1: Lightly they’ll talk of the spirit that’s gone
line2: And o’er his cold ashes upbraid him,–

poemTitle: The Burial of Sir John Moore at Corunna
author: Charles Wolfe
line1: But little he’ll reck, if they let him sleep on
line2: In the grave where a Briton has laid him.

poemTitle: The Burial of Sir John Moore at Corunna
author: Charles Wolfe
line1: But half of our heavy task was done
line2: When the clock struck the hour for retiring:

poemTitle: The Burial of Sir John Moore at Corunna
author: Charles Wolfe
line1: And we heard the distant and random gun
line2: That the foe was sullenly firing.

poemTitle: The Burial of Sir John Moore at Corunna
author: Charles Wolfe
line1: Slowly and sadly we laid him down,
line2: From the field of his fame fresh and gory;

poemTitle: The Burial of Sir John Moore at Corunna
author: Charles Wolfe
line1: We carved not a line, and we raised not a stone,
line2: But left him alone with his glory.

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: Listen my children and you shall hear
line2: Of the midnight ride of Paul Revere,

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: On the eighteenth of April, in Seventy-five;
line2: Hardly a man is now alive

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: Who remembers that famous day and year.
line2: He said to his friend, "If the British march

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: By land or sea from the town to-night,
line2: Hang a lantern aloft in the belfry arch

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: Of the North Church tower as a signal light,–
line2: One if by land, and two if by sea;

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: And I on the opposite shore will be,
line2: Ready to ride and spread the alarm

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: Through every Middlesex village and farm,
line2: For the country folk to be up and to arm."

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: Then he said "Good-night!" and with muffled oar
line2: Silently rowed to the Charlestown shore,

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: Just as the moon rose over the bay,
line2: Where swinging wide at her moorings lay

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: The Somerset, British man-of-war;
line2: A phantom ship, with each mast and spar

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: Across the moon like a prison bar,
line2: And a huge black hulk, that was magnified

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: By its own reflection in the tide.
line2: Meanwhile, his friend through alley and street

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: Wanders and watches, with eager ears,
line2: Till in the silence around him he hears

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: The muster of men at the barrack door,
line2: The sound of arms, and the tramp of feet,

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: And the measured tread of the grenadiers,
line2: Marching down to their boats on the shore.

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: Then he climbed the tower of the Old North Church,
line2: By the wooden stairs, with stealthy tread,

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: To the belfry chamber overhead,
line2: And startled the pigeons from their perch

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: On the sombre rafters, that round him made
line2: Masses and moving shapes of shade,–

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: By the trembling ladder, steep and tall,
line2: To the highest window in the wall,

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: Where he paused to listen and look down
line2: A moment on the roofs of the town

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: And the moonlight flowing over all.
line2: Beneath, in the churchyard, lay the dead,

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: In their night encampment on the hill,
line2: Wrapped in silence so deep and still

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: That he could hear, like a sentinel’s tread,
line2: The watchful night-wind, as it went

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: Creeping along from tent to tent,
line2: And seeming to whisper, "All is well!"

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: A moment only he feels the spell
line2: Of the place and the hour, and the secret dread

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: Of the lonely belfry and the dead;
line2: For suddenly all his thoughts are bent

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: On a shadowy something far away,
line2: Where the river widens to meet the bay,–

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: A line of black that bends and floats
line2: On the rising tide like a bridge of boats.

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: Meanwhile, impatient to mount and ride,
line2: Booted and spurred, with a heavy stride

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: On the opposite shore walked Paul Revere.
line2: Now he patted his horse’s side,

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: Now he gazed at the landscape far and near,
line2: Then, impetuous, stamped the earth,

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: And turned and tightened his saddle girth;
line2: But mostly he watched with eager search

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: The belfry tower of the Old North Church,
line2: As it rose above the graves on the hill,

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: Lonely and spectral and sombre and still.
line2: And lo! as he looks, on the belfry’s height

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: A glimmer, and then a gleam of light!
line2: He springs to the saddle, the bridle he turns,

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: But lingers and gazes, till full on his sight
line2: A second lamp in the belfry burns.

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: A hurry of hoofs in a village street,
line2: A shape in the moonlight, a bulk in the dark,

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: And beneath, from the pebbles, in passing, a spark
line2: Struck out by a steed flying fearless and fleet;

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: That was all! And yet, through the gloom and the light,
line2: The fate of a nation was riding that night;

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: And the spark struck out by that steed, in his flight,
line2: Kindled the land into flame with its heat.

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: He has left the village and mounted the steep,
line2: And beneath him, tranquil and broad and deep,

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: Is the Mystic, meeting the ocean tides;
line2: And under the alders that skirt its edge,

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: Now soft on the sand, now loud on the ledge,
line2: Is heard the tramp of his steed as he rides.

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: It was twelve by the village clock
line2: When he crossed the bridge into Medford town.

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: He heard the crowing of the cock,
line2: And the barking of the farmer’s dog,

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: And felt the damp of the river fog,
line2: That rises after the sun goes down.

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: It was one by the village clock,
line2: When he galloped into Lexington.

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: He saw the gilded weathercock
line2: Swim in the moonlight as he passed,

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: And the meeting-house windows, black and bare,
line2: Gaze at him with a spectral glare,

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: As if they already stood aghast
line2: At the bloody work they would look upon.

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: It was two by the village clock,
line2: When he came to the bridge in Concord town.

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: He heard the bleating of the flock,
line2: And the twitter of birds among the trees,

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: And felt the breath of the morning breeze
line2: Blowing over the meadow brown.

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: And one was safe and asleep in his bed
line2: Who at the bridge would be first to fall,

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: Who that day would be lying dead,
line2: Pierced by a British musket ball.

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: You know the rest. In the books you have read
line2: How the British Regulars fired and fled,—

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: How the farmers gave them ball for ball,
line2: From behind each fence and farmyard wall,

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: Chasing the redcoats down the lane,
line2: Then crossing the fields to emerge again

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: Under the trees at the turn of the road,
line2: And only pausing to fire and load.

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: So through the night rode Paul Revere;
line2: And so through the night went his cry of alarm

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: To every Middlesex village and farm,—
line2: A cry of defiance, and not of fear,

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: A voice in the darkness, a knock at the door,
line2: And a word that shall echo for evermore!

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: For, borne on the night-wind of the Past,
line2: Through all our history, to the last,

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: In the hour of darkness and peril and need,
line2: The people will waken and listen to hear

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: The hurrying hoof-beats of that steed,
line2: And the midnight message of Paul Revere.

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: In offices of tenderness, and pay
line2: Meet adoration to my household gods,

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: When I am gone. He works his work, I mine.
line2: There lies the port; the vessel puffs her sail:

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: There gloom the dark, broad seas. My mariners,
line2: Souls that have toil’d, and wrought, and thought with me —

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: That ever with a frolic welcome took
line2: The thunder and the sunshine, and opposed

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Free hearts, free foreheads — you and I are old;
line2: Old age hath yet his honour and his toil;

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Death closes all: but something ere the end,
line2: Some work of noble note, may yet be done,

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Not unbecoming men that strove with Gods.
line2: The lights begin to twinkle from the rocks:

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: The long day wanes: the slow moon climbs: the deep
line2: Moans round with many voices. Come, my friends,

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: ‘Tis not too late to seek a newer world.
line2: Push off, and sitting well in order smite

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: The sounding furrows; for my purpose holds
line2: To sail beyond the sunset, and the baths

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Of all the western stars, until I die.
line2: It may be that the gulfs will wash us down:

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: It may be we shall touch the Happy Isles,
line2: And see the great Achilles, whom we knew.

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Tho’ much is taken, much abides; and tho’
line2: We are not now that strength which in old days

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Moved earth and heaven, that which we are, we are;
line2: One equal temper of heroic hearts,

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Made weak by time and fate, but strong in will
line2: To strive, to seek, to find, and not to yield.

author: William Wordsworth
line1: Earth has not anything to show more fair:
line2: Dull would he be of soul who could pass by

author: William Wordsworth
line1: A sight so touching in its majesty:
line2: This City now doth, like a garment, wear

author: William Wordsworth
line1: The beauty of the morning; silent, bare,
line2: Ships, towers, domes, theatres, and temples lie

author: William Wordsworth
line1: Open unto the fields, and to the sky;
line2: All bright and glittering in the smokeless air.

author: William Wordsworth
line1: Never did sun more beautifully steep
line2: In his first splendour, valley, rock, or hill;

author: William Wordsworth
line1: Ne’er saw I, never felt, a calm so deep!
line2: The river glideth at his own sweet will:

author: William Wordsworth
line1: Dear God! the very houses seem asleep;
line2: And all that mighty heart is lying still!

author: Lord George Gordon Noel Byron
line1: Eternal Spirit of the chainless Mind!
line2: Brightest in dungeons, Liberty! thou art–

author: Lord George Gordon Noel Byron
line1: For there thy habitation is the heart–
line2: The heart which love of thee alone can bind;

author: Lord George Gordon Noel Byron
line1: And when thy sons to fetters are consign’d,
line2: To fetters, and the damp vault’s dayless gloom,

author: Lord George Gordon Noel Byron
line1: Their country conquers with their martyrdom,
line2: And Freedom’s fame finds wings on every wind.

author: Lord George Gordon Noel Byron
line1: Chillon! thy prison is a holy place,
line2: And thy sad floor an altar-for ‘t was rod,

author: Lord George Gordon Noel Byron
line1: Until his very steps have left a trace
line2: Worn, as if thy cold pavement were a sod,

author: Lord George Gordon Noel Byron
line1: By Bonnivard! May none those marks efface!
line2: For they appeal from tyranny to God.

author: James Shirley
line1: The glories of our blood and state
line2: Are shadows, not substantial things;

author: James Shirley
line1: There is no armour against Fate;
line2: Death lays his icy hand on kings:

author: James Shirley
line1: Sceptre and Crown
line2: Must tumble down,

author: James Shirley
line1: And in the dust be equal made
line2: With the poor crookèd scythe and spade.

author: James Shirley
line1: Some men with swords may reap the field,
line2: And plant fresh laurels where they kill:

author: James Shirley
line1: But their strong nerves at last must yield;
line2: They tame but one another still:

author: James Shirley
line1: Early or late
line2: They stoop to fate,

author: James Shirley
line1: And must give up their murmuring breath
line2: When they, pale captives, creep to death.

author: James Shirley
line1: The garlands wither on your brow,
line2: Then boast no more your mighty deeds!

author: James Shirley
line1: Upon Death’s purple altar now
line2: See where the victor-victim bleeds.

author: James Shirley
line1: Your heads must come
line2: To the cold tomb:

author: James Shirley
line1: Only the actions of the just
line2: Smell sweet and blossom in their dust.

poemTitle: On the death of Mr. Robert Levet A Practiser in Physic
author: Samuel Johnson
line1: Condemn’d to hope’s delusive mine,
line2: As on we toil from day to day,

poemTitle: On the death of Mr. Robert Levet A Practiser in Physic
author: Samuel Johnson
line1: By sudden blasts, or slow decline,
line2: Our social comforts drop away.

poemTitle: On the death of Mr. Robert Levet A Practiser in Physic
author: Samuel Johnson
line1: Well tried through many a varying year,
line2: See Levet to the grave descend;

poemTitle: On the death of Mr. Robert Levet A Practiser in Physic
author: Samuel Johnson
line1: Officious, innocent, sincere,
line2: Of ev’ry friendless name the friend.

poemTitle: On the death of Mr. Robert Levet A Practiser in Physic
author: Samuel Johnson
line1: Yet still he fills affection’s eye,
line2: Obscurely wise, and coarsely kind;

poemTitle: On the death of Mr. Robert Levet A Practiser in Physic
author: Samuel Johnson
line1: Nor, letter’d arrogance, deny
line2: Thy praise to merit unrefin’d.

poemTitle: On the death of Mr. Robert Levet A Practiser in Physic
author: Samuel Johnson
line1: When fainting nature call’d for aid,
line2: And hov’ring death prepar’d the blow,

poemTitle: On the death of Mr. Robert Levet A Practiser in Physic
author: Samuel Johnson
line1: His vig’rous remedy display’d
line2: The power of art without the show.

poemTitle: On the death of Mr. Robert Levet A Practiser in Physic
author: Samuel Johnson
line1: In misery’s darkest caverns known,
line2: His useful care was ever nigh,

poemTitle: On the death of Mr. Robert Levet A Practiser in Physic
author: Samuel Johnson
line1: Where hopeless anguish pour’d his groan,
line2: And lonely want retir’d to die.

poemTitle: On the death of Mr. Robert Levet A Practiser in Physic
author: Samuel Johnson
line1: No summons mock’d by chill delay,
line2: No petty gain disdain’d by pride,

poemTitle: On the death of Mr. Robert Levet A Practiser in Physic
author: Samuel Johnson
line1: The modest wants of ev’ry day
line2: The toil of ev’ry day supplied.

poemTitle: On the death of Mr. Robert Levet A Practiser in Physic
author: Samuel Johnson
line1: His virtues walk’d their narrow round,
line2: Nor made a pause, nor left a void;

poemTitle: On the death of Mr. Robert Levet A Practiser in Physic
author: Samuel Johnson
line1: And sure th’ Eternal Master found
line2: The single talent well-employ’d.

poemTitle: On the death of Mr. Robert Levet A Practiser in Physic
author: Samuel Johnson
line1: The busy day, the peaceful night,
line2: Unfelt, uncounted, glided by;

poemTitle: On the death of Mr. Robert Levet A Practiser in Physic
author: Samuel Johnson
line1: His frame was firm, his powers were bright,
line2: Tho’ now his eightieth year was nigh.

poemTitle: On the death of Mr. Robert Levet A Practiser in Physic
author: Samuel Johnson
line1: Then with no throbbing fiery pain,
line2: No cold gradations of decay,

poemTitle: On the death of Mr. Robert Levet A Practiser in Physic
author: Samuel Johnson
line1: Death broke at once the vital chain,
line2: And free’d his soul the nearest way.

author: Lord George Gordon Noel Byron
line1: I see before me the Gladiator lie:
line2: He leans upon his hand – his manly brow

author: Lord George Gordon Noel Byron
line1: Consents to death, but conquers agony,
line2: And his droop’d head sinks gradually low –

author: Lord George Gordon Noel Byron
line1: And through his side the last drops, ebbing slow
line2: From the red gash, fall heavy, one by one,

author: Lord George Gordon Noel Byron
line1: Like the first of a thunder-shower; and now
line2: The arena swims around him – he is gone,

author: Lord George Gordon Noel Byron
line1: Ere ceased the inhuman shout which hail’d the wretch who won.
line2: He heard it, but he heeded not – his eyes

author: Lord George Gordon Noel Byron
line1: Were with his heart, and that was far away;
line2: He reck’d not of the life he lost nor prize,

author: Lord George Gordon Noel Byron
line1: But where his rude hut by the Danube lay,
line2: There where his young barbarians all at play,

author: Lord George Gordon Noel Byron
line1: There was their Dacian mother – he, their sire,
line2: Butcher’d to make a Roman holiday –

author: Lord George Gordon Noel Byron
line1: All this rush’d with his blood – Shall he expire
line2: And unavenged? Arise! ye Goths, and glut your ire!

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: That story which the bold Sir Bedivere,
line2: First made and latest left of all the knights,

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Told, when the man was no more than a voice
line2: In the white winter of his age, to those

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: With whom he dwelt, new faces, other minds.
line2: For on their march to westward, Bedivere,

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Who slowly paced among the slumbering host,
line2: Heard in his tent the moanings of the King:

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: ‘I found Him in the shining of the stars,
line2: I marked Him in the flowering of His fields,

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: But in His ways with men I find Him not.
line2: I waged His wars, and now I pass and die.

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: O me! for why is all around us here
line2: As if some lesser god had made the world,

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: But had not force to shape it as he would,
line2: Till the High God behold it from beyond,

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: And enter it, and make it beautiful?
line2: Or else as if the world were wholly fair,

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: But that these eyes of men are dense and dim,
line2: And have not power to see it as it is:

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Perchance, because we see not to the close;–
line2: For I, being simple, thought to work His will,

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: And have but stricken with the sword in vain;
line2: And all whereon I leaned in wife and friend

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Is traitor to my peace, and all my realm
line2: Reels back into the beast, and is no more.

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: My God, thou hast forgotten me in my death;
line2: Nay–God my Christ–I pass but shall not die.’

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Then, ere that last weird battle in the west,
line2: There came on Arthur sleeping, Gawain killed

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: In Lancelot’s war, the ghost of Gawain blown
line2: Along a wandering wind, and past his ear

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Went shrilling, ‘Hollow, hollow all delight!
line2: Hail, King! tomorrow thou shalt pass away.

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Farewell! there is an isle of rest for thee.
line2: And I am blown along a wandering wind,

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: And hollow, hollow, hollow all delight.’
line2: And fainter onward, like wild birds that change

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Their season in the night and wail their way
line2: From cloud to cloud, down the long wind the dream

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Shrilled; but in going mingled with dim cries
line2: Far in the moonlit haze among the hills,

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: As of some lonely city sacked by night,
line2: When all is lost, and wife and child with wail

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Pass to new lords; and Arthur woke and called,
line2: ‘Who spake? A dream. O light upon the wind,

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Thine, Gawain, was the voice–are these dim cries
line2: Thine? or doth all that haunts the waste and wild

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Mourn, knowing it will go along with me?’
line2: This heard the bold Sir Bedivere and spake:

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: ‘O me, my King, let pass whatever will,
line2: Elves, and the harmless glamour of the field;

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: But in their stead thy name and glory cling
line2: To all high places like a golden cloud

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: For ever: but as yet thou shalt not pass.
line2: Light was Gawain in life, and light in death

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Is Gawain, for the ghost is as the man;
line2: And care not thou for dreams from him, but rise–

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: I hear the steps of Modred in the west,
line2: And with him many of thy people, and knights

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Once thine, whom thou hast loved, but grosser grown
line2: Than heathen, spitting at their vows and thee.

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Right well in heart they know thee for the King.
line2: Arise, go forth and conquer as of old.’

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Then spake King Arthur to Sir Bedivere:
line2: ‘Far other is this battle in the west

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Whereto we move, than when we strove in youth,
line2: And brake the petty kings, and fought with Rome,

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Or thrust the heathen from the Roman wall,
line2: And shook him through the north. Ill doom is mine

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: To war against my people and my knights.
line2: The king who fights his people fights himself.

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: And they my knights, who loved me once, the stroke
line2: That strikes them dead is as my death to me.

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Yet let us hence, and find or feel a way
line2: Through this blind haze, which ever since I saw

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: One lying in the dust at Almesbury,
line2: Hath folded in the passes of the world.’

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Then rose the King and moved his host by night,
line2: And ever pushed Sir Modred, league by league,

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Back to the sunset bound of Lyonnesse–
line2: A land of old upheaven from the abyss

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: By fire, to sink into the abyss again;
line2: Where fragments of forgotten peoples dwelt,

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: And the long mountains ended in a coast
line2: Of ever-shifting sand, and far away

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: The phantom circle of a moaning sea.
line2: There the pursuer could pursue no more,

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: And he that fled no further fly the King;
line2: And there, that day when the great light of heaven

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Burned at his lowest in the rolling year,
line2: On the waste sand by the waste sea they closed.

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Nor ever yet had Arthur fought a fight
line2: Like this last, dim, weird battle of the west.

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: A deathwhite mist slept over sand and sea:
line2: Whereof the chill, to him who breathed it, drew

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Down with his blood, till all his heart was cold
line2: With formless fear; and even on Arthur fell

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Confusion, since he saw not whom he fought.
line2: For friend and foe were shadows in the mist,

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: And friend slew friend not knowing whom he slew;
line2: And some had visions out of golden youth,

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: And some beheld the faces of old ghosts
line2: Look in upon the battle; and in the mist

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Was many a noble deed, many a base,
line2: And chance and craft and strength in single fights,

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: And ever and anon with host to host
line2: Shocks, and the splintering spear, the hard mail hewn,

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Shield-breakings, and the clash of brands, the crash
line2: Of battleaxes on shattered helms, and shrieks

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: After the Christ, of those who falling down
line2: Looked up for heaven, and only saw the mist;

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: And shouts of heathen and the traitor knights,
line2: Oaths, insults, filth, and monstrous blasphemies,

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Sweat, writhings, anguish, labouring of the lungs
line2: In that close mist, and cryings for the light,

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Moans of the dying, and voices of the dead.
line2: Last, as by some one deathbed after wail

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Of suffering, silence follows, or through death
line2: Or deathlike swoon, thus over all that shore,

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Save for some whisper of the seething seas,
line2: A dead hush fell; but when the dolorous day

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Grew drearier toward twilight falling, came
line2: A bitter wind, clear from the North, and blew

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: The mist aside, and with that wind the tide
line2: Rose, and the pale King glanced across the field

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Of battle: but no man was moving there;
line2: Nor any cry of Christian heard thereon,

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Nor yet of heathen; only the wan wave
line2: Brake in among dead faces, to and fro

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Swaying the helpless hands, and up and down
line2: Tumbling the hollow helmets of the fallen,

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: And shivered brands that once had fought with Rome,
line2: And rolling far along the gloomy shores

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: The voice of days of old and days to be.
line2: Then spake King Arthur to Sir Bedivere,

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: And whiter than the mist that all day long
line2: Had held the field of battle was the King:

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: ‘Hearest thou this great voice that shakes the world,
line2: And wastes the narrow realm whereon we move,

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: And beats upon the faces of the dead,
line2: My dead, as though they had not died for me?–

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: O Bedivere, for on my heart hath fallen
line2: Confusion, till I know not what I am,

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Nor whence I am, nor whether I be King.
line2: Behold, I seem but King among the dead.’

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Then spake the bold Sir Bedivere: ‘My King,
line2: King everywhere! and so the dead have kings,

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: There also will I worship thee as King.
line2: Yet still thy life is whole, and still I live

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Who love thee; but who hates thee, he that brought
line2: The heathen back among us, yonder stands,

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Modred, unharmed, the traitor of thine house.’
line2: Then spake the King: ‘My house hath been my doom.

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: But call not thou this traitor of my house
line2: Who hath but dwelt beneath one roof with me.

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: My house are rather they who sware my vows,
line2: Yea, even while they brake them, owned me King.

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: And well for thee, saying in my dark hour,
line2: When all the purport of my throne hath failed,

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: That quick or dead thou holdest me for King.
line2: King am I, whatsoever be their cry;

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: And one last act of kinghood shalt thou see
line2: Yet, ere I pass.’ And uttering this the King

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Made at the man: then Modred smote his liege
line2: Hard on that helm which many a heathen sword

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Had beaten thin; while Arthur at one blow,
line2: Striking the last stroke with Excalibur,

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Slew him, and all but slain himself, he fell.
line2: So all day long the noise of battle rolled

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Among the mountains by the winter sea;
line2: Until King Arthur’s Table, man by man,

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Had fallen in Lyonnesse about their lord,
line2: King Arthur. Then, because his wound was deep,

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: The bold Sir Bedivere uplifted him,
line2: And bore him to a chapel nigh the field,

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: A broken chancel with a broken cross,
line2: That stood on a dark strait of barren land:

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: On one side lay the Ocean, and on one
line2: Lay a great water, and the moon was full.

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Then spake King Arthur to Sir Bedivere:
line2: ‘The sequel of today unsolders all

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: The goodliest fellowship of famous knights
line2: Whereof this world holds record. Such a sleep

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: They sleep–the men I loved. I think that we
line2: Shall never more, at any future time,

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Delight our souls with talk of knightly deeds,
line2: Walking about the gardens and the halls

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Of Camelot, as in the days that were.
line2: I perish by this people which I made,–

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Though Merlin sware that I should come again
line2: To rule once more; but, let what will be, be,

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: I am so deeply smitten through the helm
line2: That without help I cannot last till morn.

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Thou therefore take my brand Excalibur,
line2: Which was my pride: for thou rememberest how

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: In those old days, one summer noon, an arm
line2: Rose up from out the bosom of the lake,

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Clothed in white samite, mystic, wonderful,
line2: Holding the sword–and how I rowed across

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: And took it, and have worn it, like a king;
line2: And, wheresoever I am sung or told

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: In aftertime, this also shall be known:
line2: But now delay not: take Excalibur,

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: And fling him far into the middle mere:
line2: Watch what thou seest, and lightly bring me word.’

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: To him replied the bold Sir Bedivere:
line2: ‘It is not meet, Sir King, to leave thee thus,

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Aidless, alone, and smitten through the helm–
line2: A little thing may harm a wounded man;

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Yet I thy hest will all perform at full,
line2: Watch what I see, and lightly bring thee word.’

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: So saying, from the ruined shrine he stept,
line2: And in the moon athwart the place of tombs,

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Where lay the mighty bones of ancient men,
line2: Old knights, and over them the sea-wind sang

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Shrill, chill, with flakes of foam. He, stepping down
line2: By zigzag paths, and juts of pointed rock,

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Came on the shining levels of the lake.
line2: There drew he forth the brand Excalibur,

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: And o’er him, drawing it, the winter moon,
line2: Brightening the skirts of a long cloud, ran forth

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: And sparkled keen with frost against the hilt:
line2: For all the haft twinkled with diamond sparks,

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Myriads of topaz-lights, and jacinth-work
line2: Of subtlest jewellery. He gazed so long

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: That both his eyes were dazzled as he stood,
line2: This way and that dividing the swift mind,

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: In act to throw: but at the last it seemed
line2: Better to leave Excalibur concealed

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: There in the many-knotted waterflags,
line2: That whistled stiff and dry about the marge.

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: So strode he back slow to the wounded King.
line2: Then spake King Arthur to Sir Bedivere:

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: ‘Hast thou performed my mission which I gave?
line2: What is it thou hast seen? or what hast heard?’

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: And answer made the bold Sir Bedivere:
line2: ‘I heard the ripple washing in the reeds,

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: And the wild water lapping on the crag.’
line2: To whom replied King Arthur, faint and pale:

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: ‘Thou hast betrayed thy nature and thy name,
line2: Not rendering true answer, as beseemed

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Thy fealty, nor like a noble knight:
line2: For surer sign had followed, either hand,

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Or voice, or else a motion of the mere.
line2: This is a shameful thing for men to lie.

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Yet now, I charge thee, quickly go again,
line2: As thou art lief and dear, and do the thing

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: I bade thee, watch, and lightly bring me word.’
line2: Then went Sir Bedivere the second time

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Across the ridge, and paced beside the mere,
line2: Counting the dewy pebbles, fixed in thought;

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: But when he saw the wonder of the hilt,
line2: How curiously and strangely chased, he smote

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: His palms together, and he cried aloud:
line2: ‘And if indeed I cast the brand away,

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Surely a precious thing, one worthy note,
line2: Should thus be lost for ever from the earth,

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Which might have pleased the eyes of many men.
line2: What good should follow this, if this were done?

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: What harm, undone? Deep harm to disobey,
line2: Seeing obedience is the bond of rule.

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Were it well to obey then, if a king demand
line2: An act unprofitable, against himself?

line1: Either from lust of gold, or like a girl

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: And caught him by the hilt, and brandished him

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson

line1: ‘Now see I by thine eyes that this is done.

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: And answer made the bold Sir Bedivere:

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Then with both hands I flung him, wheeling him;

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Clothed in white samite, mystic, wonderful,

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Three times, and drew him under in the mere.’
line2: And answer made King Arthur, breathing hard:

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: ‘My end draws nigh; ’tis time that I were gone.

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: And bear me to the margin; yet I fear

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: So saying, from the pavement he half rose,

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Like one that feels a nightmare on his bed

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: And colourless, and like the withered moon

author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: And all his greaves and cuisses dashed with drops
