author: William Butler Yeats
line1: A shadow of cloud on the stream
line2: Changes minute by minute;

author: William Butler Yeats
line1: A horse-hoof slides on the brim,
line2: And a horse plashes within it

author: William Butler Yeats
line1: Where long-legged moor-hens dive,
line2: And hens to moor-cocks call.

author: William Butler Yeats
line1: Minute by minute they live:
line2: The stone’s in the midst of all.

author: William Butler Yeats
line1: Too long a sacrifice
line2: Can make a stone of the heart.

author: William Butler Yeats
line1: O when may it suffice?
line2: That is heaven’s part, our part

author: William Butler Yeats
line1: To murmur name upon name,
line2: As a mother names her child

author: William Butler Yeats
line1: When sleep at last has come
line2: On limbs that had run wild.

author: William Butler Yeats
line1: What is it but nightfall?
line2: No, no, not night but death;

author: William Butler Yeats
line1: Was it needless death after all?
line2: For England may keep faith

author: William Butler Yeats
line1: For all that is done and said.
line2: We know their dream; enough

author: William Butler Yeats
line1: To know they dreamed and are dead.
line2: And what if excess of love

author: William Butler Yeats
line1: Bewildered them till they died?
line2: I write it out in a verse –

author: William Butler Yeats
line1: MacDonagh and MacBride
line2: And Connolly and Pearse

author: William Butler Yeats
line1: Now and in time to be,
line2: Wherever green is worn,

author: William Butler Yeats
line1: Are changed, changed utterly:
line2: A terrible beauty is born.

author: Sarojini Naidu
line1: Bangle sellers are we who bear
line2: Our shining loads to the temple fair…

author: Sarojini Naidu
line1: Who will buy these delicate, bright
line2: Rainbow-tinted circles of light?

author: Sarojini Naidu
line1: Lustrous tokens of radiant lives,
line2: For happy daughters and happy wives.

author: Sarojini Naidu
line1: Some are meet for a maiden’s wrist,
line2: Silver and blue as the mountain mist,

author: Sarojini Naidu
line1: Some are flushed like the buds that dream
line2: On the tranquil brow of a woodland stream,

author: Sarojini Naidu
line1: Some are aglow wth the bloom that cleaves
line2: To the limpid glory of new born leaves

author: Sarojini Naidu
line1: Some are like fields of sunlit corn,
line2: Meet for a bride on her bridal morn,

author: Sarojini Naidu
line1: Some, like the flame of her marriage fire,
line2: Or, rich with the hue of her heart’s desire,

author: Sarojini Naidu
line1: Tinkling, luminous, tender, and clear,
line2: Like her bridal laughter and bridal tear.

author: Sarojini Naidu
line1: Some are purple and gold flecked grey
line2: For she who has journeyed through life midway,

author: Sarojini Naidu
line1: Whose hands have cherished, whose love has blest,
line2: And cradled fair sons on her faithful breast,

author: Sarojini Naidu
line1: And serves her household in fruitful pride,
line2: And worships the gods at her husband’s side.

author: Rupert Brooke
line1: If I should die, think only this of me:
line2: That there’s some corner of a foreign field

author: Rupert Brooke
line1: That is for ever England. There shall be
line2: In that rich earth a richer dust concealed;

author: Rupert Brooke
line1: A dust whom England bore, shaped, made aware,
line2: Gave, once, her flowers to love, her ways to roam,

author: Rupert Brooke
line1: A body of England’s, breathing English air,
line2: Washed by the rivers, blest by suns of home.

author: Rupert Brooke
line1: And think, this heart, all evil shed away,
line2: A pulse in the eternal mind, no less

author: Rupert Brooke
line1: Gives somewhere back the thoughts by England given;
line2: Her sights and sounds; dreams happy as her day;

author: Rupert Brooke
line1: And laughter, learnt of friends; and gentleness,
line2: In hearts at peace, under an English heaven.

author: Robert Southey
line1: No stir in the air, no stir in the sea,
line2: The Ship was still as she could be;

author: Robert Southey
line1: Her sails from heaven received no motion,
line2: Her keel was steady in the ocean.

author: Robert Southey
line1: Without either sign or sound of their shock,
line2: The waves flow’d over the Inchcape Rock;

author: Robert Southey
line1: So little they rose, so little they fell,
line2: They did not move the Inchcape Bell.

author: Robert Southey
line1: The Abbot of Aberbrothok
line2: Had placed that bell on the Inchcape Rock;

author: Robert Southey
line1: On a buoy in the storm it floated and swung,
line2: And over the waves its warning rung.

author: Robert Southey
line1: When the Rock was hid by the surge’s swell,
line2: The Mariners heard the warning Bell;

author: Robert Southey
line1: And then they knew the perilous Rock,
line2: And blest the Abbot of Aberbrothok

author: Robert Southey
line1: The Sun in the heaven was shining gay,
line2: All things were joyful on that day;

author: Robert Southey
line1: The sea-birds scream’d as they wheel’d round,
line2: And there was joyaunce in their sound.

author: Robert Southey
line1: The buoy of the Inchcpe Bell was seen
line2: A darker speck on the ocean green;

author: Robert Southey
line1: Sir Ralph the Rover walk’d his deck,
line2: And fix’d his eye on the darker speck.

author: Robert Southey
line1: He felt the cheering power of spring,
line2: It made him whistle, it made him sing;

author: Robert Southey
line1: His heart was mirthful to excess,
line2: But the Rover’s mirth was wickedness.

author: Robert Southey
line1: His eye was on the Inchcape Float;
line2: Quoth he, "My men, put out the boat,

author: Robert Southey
line1: And row me to the Inchcape Rock,
line2: And I’ll plague the Abbot of Aberbrothok."

author: Robert Southey
line1: The boat is lower’d, the boatmen row,
line2: And to the Inchcape Rock they go;

author: Robert Southey
line1: Sir Ralph bent over from the boat,
line2: And he cut the bell from the Inchcape Float.

author: Robert Southey
line1: Down sank the Bell with a gurgling sound,
line2: The bubbles rose and burst around;

author: Robert Southey
line1: Quoth Sir Ralph, "The next who comes to the Rock,
line2: Won’t bless the Abbot of Aberbrothok."

author: Robert Southey
line1: Sir ralph the Rover sail’d away,
line2: He scour’d the seas for many a day;

author: Robert Southey
line1: And now grown rich with plunder’d store,
line2: He steers his course for Scotland’s shore.

author: Robert Southey
line1: So thick a haze o’erspreads the sky,
line2: They cannot see the sun on high;

author: Robert Southey
line1: The wind hath blown a gale all day,
line2: At evening it hath died away.

author: Robert Southey
line1: On the deck the Rover takes his stand,
line2: So dark it is they see no land.

author: Robert Southey
line1: Quoth Sir Ralph, "It will be lighter soon,
line2: For there is the dawn of the rising Moon."

author: Robert Southey
line1: "Canst hear," said one, "the breakers roar?
line2: For methinks we should be near the shore."

author: Robert Southey
line1: "Now, where we are I cannot tell,
line2: But I wish we could hear the Inchcape Bell."

author: Robert Southey
line1: They hear no sound, the swell is strong,
line2: Though the wind hath fallen they drift along;

author: Robert Southey
line1: Till the vessel strikes with a shivering shock,
line2: "Oh Christ! It is the Inchcape Rock!"

author: Robert Southey
line1: Sir Ralph the Rover tore his hair,
line2: He curst himself in his despair;

author: Robert Southey
line1: The waves rush in on every side,
line2: The ship is sinking beneath the tide.

author: Robert Southey
line1: But even is his dying fear,
line2: One dreadful sound could the Rover hear;

author: Robert Southey
line1: A sound as if with the Inchcape Bell,
line2: The Devil below was ringing his knell.

author: Sir Walter Scott
line1: O, young Lochinvar is come out of the west,
line2: Through all the wide Border his steed was the best;

author: Sir Walter Scott
line1: And save his good broadsword, he weapons had none,
line2: He rode all unarm’d, and he rode all alone.

author: Sir Walter Scott
line1: So faithful in love, and so dauntless in war,
line2: There never was knight like the young Lochinvar.

author: Sir Walter Scott
line1: He staid not for brake, and he stopp’d not for stone,
line2: He swam the Eske river where ford there was none;

author: Sir Walter Scott
line1: But ere he alighted at Netherby gate,
line2: The bride had consented, the gallant came late:

author: Sir Walter Scott
line1: For a laggard in love, and a dastard in war,
line2: Was to wed the fair Ellen of brave Lochinvar.

author: Sir Walter Scott
line1: So boldly he entered the Netherby Hall,
line2: Among bride’s-men, and kinsmen, and brothers, and all:

author: Sir Walter Scott
line1: Then spoke the bride’s father, his hand on his sword,
line2: (For the poor craven bridegroom said never a word,)

author: Sir Walter Scott
line1: "O come ye in peace here, or come ye in war,
line2: Or to dance at our bridal, young Lord Lochinvar?"–

author: Sir Walter Scott
line1: "I long woo’d your daughter, my suit you denied;–
line2: Love swells like the Solway, but ebbs like its tide–

author: Sir Walter Scott
line1: And now am I come, with this lost love of mine,
line2: To lead but one measure, drink one cup of wine.

author: Sir Walter Scott
line1: There are maidens in Scotland more lovely by far,
line2: That would gladly be bride to the young Lochinvar."

author: Sir Walter Scott
line1: The bride kiss’d the goblet; the knight took it up,
line2: He quaff’d off the wine, and he threw down the cup.

author: Sir Walter Scott
line1: She look’d down to blush, and she look’d up to sigh,
line2: With a smile on her lips, and a tear in her eye.

author: Sir Walter Scott
line1: He took her soft hand, ere her mother could bar,–
line2: "Now tread we a measure!" said young Lochinvar.

author: Sir Walter Scott
line1: So stately his form, and so lovely her face,
line2: That never a hall such a galliard did grace;

author: Sir Walter Scott
line1: While her mother did fret, and her father did fume,
line2: And the bridegroom stood dangling his bonnet and plume;

author: Sir Walter Scott
line1: And the bride-maidens whisper’d, " ‘Twere better by far
line2: To have match’d our fair cousin with young Lochinvar."

author: Sir Walter Scott
line1: One touch to her hand, and one word in her ear,
line2: When they reach’d the hall-door, and the charger stood near;

author: Sir Walter Scott
line1: So light to the croupe the fair lady he swung,
line2: So light to the saddle before her he sprung!

author: Sir Walter Scott
line1: "She is won! we are gone, over bank, bush, and scaur;
line2: They’ll have fleet steeds that follow," quoth young Lochinvar.

author: Sir Walter Scott
line1: There was mounting ‘mong Grfmes of the Netherby clan;
line2: Forsters, Fenwicks, and Musgraves, they rode and they ran:

author: Sir Walter Scott
line1: There was racing and chasing, on Cannobie Lee,
line2: But the lost bride of Netherby ne’er did they see.

author: Sir Walter Scott
line1: So daring in love, and so dauntless in war,
line2: Have ye e’er heard of gallant like young Lochinvar?

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: Beside the ungathered rice he lay,
line2: His sickle in his hand;

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: His breast was bare, his matted hair
line2: Was buried in the sand.

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: Again, in the mist and shadow of sleep,
line2: He saw his Native Land.

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: Wide through the landscape of his dreams
line2: The lordly Niger flowed;

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: Beneath the palm-trees on the plain
line2: Once more a king he strode;

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: And heard the tinkling caravans
line2: Descend the mountain-road.

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: He saw once more his dark-eyed queen
line2: Among her children stand;

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: They clasped his neck, they kissed his cheeks,
line2: They held him by the hand!–

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: A tear burst from the sleeper’s lids
line2: And fell into the sand.

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: And then at furious speed he rode
line2: Along the Niger’s bank;

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: His bridle-reins were golden chains,
line2: And, with a martial clank,

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: At each leap he could feel his scabbard of steel
line2: Smiting his stallion’s flank.

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: Before him, like a blood-red flag,
line2: The bright flamingoes flew;

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: >From morn till night he followed their flight,
line2: O’er plains where the tamarind grew,

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: Till he saw the roofs of Caffre huts,
line2: And the ocean rose to view.

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: At night he heard the lion roar,
line2: And the hyena scream,

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: And the river-horse, as he crushed the reeds
line2: Beside some hidden stream;

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: And it passed, like a glorious roll of drums,
line2: Through the triumph of his dream.

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: The forests, with their myriad tongues,
line2: Shouted of liberty;

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: And the Blast of the Desert cried aloud,
line2: With a voice so wild and free,

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: That he started in his sleep and smiled
line2: At their tempestuous glee.

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: He did not feel the driver’s whip,
line2: Nor the burning heat of day;

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: For Death had illumined the Land of Sleep,
line2: And his lifeless body lay

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: A worn-out fetter, that the soul
line2: Had broken and thrown away!

author: Robert Browning
line1: I
line2: Hamelin Town’s in Brunswick,

author: Robert Browning
line1: By famous Hanover city;
line2: The river Weser, deep and wide,

author: Robert Browning
line1: Washes its wall on the southern side;
line2: A pleasanter spot you never spied;

author: Robert Browning
line1: But, when begins my ditty,
line2: Almost five hundred years ago,

author: Robert Browning
line1: To see the townsfolk suffer so
line2: From vermin, was a pity.

author: Robert Browning
line1: II
line2: Rats!

author: Robert Browning
line1: They fought the dogs and killed the cats,
line2: And bit the babies in the cradles,

author: Robert Browning
line1: And ate the cheeses out of the vats,
line2: And licked the soup from the cooks’ own ladle’s,

author: Robert Browning
line1: Split open the kegs of salted sprats,
line2: Made nests inside men’s Sunday hats,

author: Robert Browning
line1: And even spoiled the women’s chats
line2: By drowning their speaking

author: Robert Browning
line1: With shrieking and squeaking
line2: ln fifty different sharps and flats.

author: Robert Browning
line1: III
line2: At last the people in a body

author: Robert Browning
line1: To the town hall came flocking:
line2: "Tis clear," cried they, ‘our Mayor’s a noddy;

author: Robert Browning
line1: And as for our Corporation–shocking
line2: To think we buy gowns lined with ermine

author: Robert Browning
line1: For dolts that can’t or won’t determine
line2: What’s best to rid us of our vermin!

author: Robert Browning
line1: You hope, because you’re old and obese,
line2: To find in the furry civic robe ease?

author: Robert Browning
line1: Rouse up, sirs! Give your brains a racking
line2: To find the remedy we’re lacking,

author: Robert Browning
line1: Or, sure as fate, we’ll send you packing!"
line2: At this the Mayor and Corporation

author: Robert Browning
line1: Quaked with a mighty consternation.
line2: IV

author: Robert Browning
line1: An hour they sat in council,
line2: At length the Mayor broke silence:

author: Robert Browning
line1: "For a guilder I’d my ermine gown sell,
line2: I wish I were a mile hence!

author: Robert Browning
line1: It’s easy to bid one rack one’s brain–
line2: I’m sure my poor head aches again,

author: Robert Browning
line1: I’ve scratched it so, and all in vain
line2: Oh for a trap, a trap, a trap!"

author: Robert Browning
line1: Just as he said this, what should hap
line2: At the chamber door but a gentle tap?

author: Robert Browning
line1: "Bless us,’ cried the Mayor, "what’s that?"
line2: (With the Corporation as he sat,

author: Robert Browning
line1: Looking little though wondrous fat;
line2: Nor brighter was his eye, nor moister

author: Robert Browning
line1: Than a too-long-opened oyster,
line2: Save when at noon his paunch grew mutinous

author: Robert Browning
line1: For a plate of turtle green and glutinous)
line2: "Only a scraping of shoes on the mat?

author: Robert Browning
line1: Anything like the sound of a rat
line2: Makes my heart go pit-a-pat!"

author: Robert Browning
line1: V
line2: "Come in!"–the Mayor cried, looking bigger:

author: Robert Browning
line1: And in did come the strangest figure!
line2: His queer long coat from heel to head

author: Robert Browning
line1: Was half of yellow and half of red
line2: And he himself was tall and thin,

author: Robert Browning
line1: With sharp blue eyes, each like a pin,
line2: And light loose hair, yet swarthy skin,

author: Robert Browning
line1: No tuft on cheek nor beard on chin,
line2: But lips where smiles went out and in;

author: Robert Browning
line1: There was no guessing his kith and kin:
line2: And nobody could enough admire

author: Robert Browning
line1: The tall man and his quaint attire.
line2: Quoth one:"It’s as my great-grandsire,

author: Robert Browning
line1: Starting up at the trump of doom’s tone,
line2: Had walked this way from his painted tombstone!"

author: Robert Browning
line1: VI
line2: He advanced to the council-table:

author: Robert Browning
line1: And, "Please your honors," said he, "I’m able,
line2: By means of a secret charm, to draw

author: Robert Browning
line1: All creatures living beneath the sun,
line2: That creep or swim or fly or run,

author: Robert Browning
line1: After me so as you never saw
line2: And I chiefly use my charm

author: Robert Browning
line1: On creatures that do people harm,
line2: The mole and toad and newt and viper;

author: Robert Browning
line1: And People call me the Pied Piper."
line2: (And here they noticed round his neck

author: Robert Browning
line1: A scarf of red and yellow stripe,
line2: To match with his coat of the self-same check;

author: Robert Browning
line1: And at the scarf’s end hung a pipe;
line2: And his fingers, they noticed, were ever straying

author: Robert Browning
line1: As if impatient to be playing
line2: Upon this pipe, as low it dangled

author: Robert Browning
line1: Over his vesture so old-fangled.)
line2: "Yet," said he, "poor piper as I am,

author: Robert Browning
line1: In Tartary I freed the Cham,
line2: Last June, from his huge swarm of gnats;

author: Robert Browning
line1: I eased in Asia the Nizam
line2: Of a monstrous brood of vampyre-bats:

author: Robert Browning
line1: And as for what your brain bewilders–
line2: If I can rid your town of rats

author: Robert Browning
line1: Will you give me a thousand guilders?"
line2: "One? Fifty thousand!" was the exclamation

author: Robert Browning
line1: Of the astonished Mayor and Corporation.
line2: VII

author: Robert Browning
line1: Into the street the Piper stept,
line2: Smiling first a little smile,

author: Robert Browning
line1: As if he knew what magic slept
line2: In his quiet pipe the while;

author: Robert Browning
line1: Then, like a musical adept,
line2: To blow the pipe his lips he wrinkled,

author: Robert Browning
line1: And green and blue his sharp eyes twinkled,
line2: Like a candle-flame where salt is sprinkled;

author: Robert Browning
line1: And ere three shrill notes the pipe uttered,
line2: You heard as if an army muttered;

author: Robert Browning
line1: And the muttering grew to a grumbling;
line2: And the grumbling grew to a mighty rumbling;

author: Robert Browning
line1: And out of the houses the rats came tumbling.
line2: Great rats, small rats, lean rats, brawny rats,

author: Robert Browning
line1: Brown rats, black rats, gray rats, tawny rats,
line2: Grave old plodders, gay young friskers,

author: Robert Browning
line1: Fathers, mothers, uncles, cousins,
line2: Cocking tails and pricking whiskers,

author: Robert Browning
line1: Families by tens and dozens,
line2: Brothers, sisters, husbands, wives–

author: Robert Browning
line1: Followed the Piper for their lives.
line2: From street to street he piped advancing,

author: Robert Browning
line1: And step for step they followed dancing,
line2: Until they came to the river Weser

author: Robert Browning
line1: Wherein all plunged and perished!
line2: Save one who, stout as Julius Caesar,

author: Robert Browning
line1: Swam across and lived to carry
line2: (As the manuscript he cherished)

author: Robert Browning
line1: To Rat-land home his commentary:
line2: Which was, "At the first shrill notes of the pipe,

author: Robert Browning
line1: I heard a sound as of scraping tripe,
line2: And putting apples, wondrous ripe,

author: Robert Browning
line1: Into a cider-press’s gripe:
line2: And a moving away of pickle-tub-boards,

author: Robert Browning
line1: And a leaving ajar of conserve-cupboards,
line2: And a drawing the corks of train-oil-flasks,

author: Robert Browning
line1: And a breaking the hoops of butter-casks:
line2: And it seemed as if a voice

author: Robert Browning
line1: (Sweeter far than by harp or by psaltery
line2: Is breathed) called out, ‘Oh rats, rejoice!

author: Robert Browning
line1: The world is grown to one vast dry-saltery!
line2: So munch on, crunch on, take your nuncheon,

author: Robert Browning
line1: Breakfast, supper, dinner, luncheon!’
line2: And just as a bulky sugar-puncheon,

author: Robert Browning
line1: All ready staved, like a great sun shone
line2: Glorious scarce an inch before me,

author: Robert Browning
line1: Just as methought it said ‘Come bore me!’
line2: –I found the Weser rolling o’er me."

author: Robert Browning
line1: VIII
line2: You should have heard the Hamelin people

author: Robert Browning
line1: Ringing the bells till they rocked the steeple.
line2: Go," cried the Mayor, "and get long poles!

author: Robert Browning
line1: Poke out the nests and block up the holes!
line2: Consult with carpenters and builders

author: Robert Browning
line1: And leave in our town not even a trace
line2: Of the rats!"– when suddenly, up the face

author: Robert Browning
line1: Of the Piper perked in the market-place,
line2: With a, "First, if you please, my thousand guilders!"

author: Robert Browning
line1: IX
line2: A thousand guilders! The Mayor looked blue;

author: Robert Browning
line1: So did the Corporation too.
line2: For council dinners made rare havoc

author: Robert Browning
line1: With Claret, Moselle, Vin-de-Grave, Hock;
line2: And half the money would replenish

author: Robert Browning
line1: Their cellar’s biggest butt with Rhenish.
line2: To pay this sum to a wandering fellow

author: Robert Browning
line1: With a gipsy coat of red and yellow!
line2: "Beside," quoth the Mayor with a knowing wink,

author: Robert Browning
line1: "Our business was done at the river’s brink;
line2: We saw with our eyes the vermin sink,

author: Robert Browning
line1: And what’s dead can’t come to life, I think.
line2: So, friend, we’re not the folks to shrink

author: Robert Browning
line1: From the duty of giving you something for drink,
line2: And a matter of money to put in your poke;

author: Robert Browning
line1: But as for the guilders, what we spoke
line2: Of them, as you very well know, was in joke.

author: Robert Browning
line1: Beside, our losses have made us thrifty.
line2: A thousand guilders! Come, take fifty!

author: Robert Browning
line1: X
line2: The Piper’s face fell, and he cried

author: Robert Browning
line1: "No trifling! I can’t wait, beside!
line2: I’ve promised to visit by dinnertime

author: Robert Browning
line1: Bagdat, and accept the prime
line2: Of the Head-Cooks pottage, all he’s rich in,

author: Robert Browning
line1: For having left, in the Caliph’s kitchen,
line2: Of a nest of scorpions no survivor:

author: Robert Browning
line1: With him I proved no bargain-driver,
line2: With you, don’t think I’ll bate a stiver!

author: Robert Browning
line1: And folks who put me in a passion
line2: May find me pipe after another fashion."

author: Robert Browning
line1: XI
line2: "How?" cried the Mayor, "d’ye think I brook

author: Robert Browning
line1: Being worse treated than a Cook?
line2: Insulted by a lazy ribald

author: Robert Browning
line1: With idle pipe and vesture piebald?
line2: You threaten us, fellow? Do your worst,

author: Robert Browning
line1: Blow your pipe there till you burst!
line2: XII

author: Robert Browning

author: Robert Browning

author: Robert Browning
line1: Of all all the pleasant sights they see,

author: Robert Browning

author: Robert Browning
line1: Where waters gushed and fruit-trees grew

author: Robert Browning

author: Robert Browning
line1: And found myself outside the hill,

author: Robert Browning
line1: To go now limping as before,
line2: And never hear of that country more!"

author: Alfred Noyes
line1: PART ONE
line2: THE wind was a torrent of darkness among the gusty trees,

author: Alfred Noyes
line1: The moon was a ghostly galleon tossed upon cloudy seas,
line2: The road was a ribbon of moonlight over the purple moor,

author: Alfred Noyes
line1: And the highwayman came riding–
line2: Riding–riding–

author: Alfred Noyes
line1: The highwayman came riding, up to the old inn-door.
line2: He’d a French cocked-hat on his forehead, a bunch of lace at his

author: Alfred Noyes
line1: chin,
line2: A coat of the claret velvet, and breeches of brown doe-skin;

author: Alfred Noyes
line1: They fitted with never a wrinkle: his boots were up to the thigh!
line2: And he rode with a jewelled twinkle,

author: Alfred Noyes
line1: His pistol butts a-twinkle,
line2: His rapier hilt a-twinkle, under the jewelled sky.

author: Alfred Noyes
line1: Over the cobbles he clattered and clashed in the dark inn-yard,
line2: And he tapped with his whip on the shuters, but all was locked and

author: Alfred Noyes
line1: barred;
line2: He whistled a tune to the window, and who should be waiting there

author: Alfred Noyes
line1: But the landlord’s black-eyed daughter,
line2: Bess, the landlord’s daughter,

author: Alfred Noyes
line1: Plaiting a dark red love-knot into her long black hair.
line2: And dark in the dark old inn-yard a stable-wicket creaked

author: Alfred Noyes
line1: Where Tim the ostler listened; his face was white and peaked;
line2: His eyes were hollows of madness, his hair like mouldy hay,

author: Alfred Noyes
line1: But he loved the landlord’s daughter,
line2: The landlord’s red-lipped daughter,

author: Alfred Noyes
line1: Dumb as a dog he listened, and he heard the robber say–
line2: "One kiss, my bonny sweetheart, I’m after a prize to-night,

author: Alfred Noyes
line1: But I shall be back with the yellow gold before the morning light;
line2: Yet, if they press me sharply, and harry me through the day,

author: Alfred Noyes
line1: Then look for me by moonlight,
line2: Watch for me by moonlight,

author: Alfred Noyes
line1: I’ll come to thee by moonlight, though hell should bar the way."
line2: He rose upright in the stirrups; he scarce could reach her hand,

line2: brand

author: Alfred Noyes

author: Alfred Noyes

author: Alfred Noyes
line1: He did not come in the dawning; he did not come at noon;
line2: And out o’ the tawny sunset, before the rise o’ the moon,

author: Alfred Noyes
line1: When the road was a gypsy’s ribbon, looping the purple moor,
line2: A red-coat troop came marching–

author: Alfred Noyes
line1: Marching–marching–
line2: King George’s men came matching, up to the old inn-door.

author: Alfred Noyes
line1: They said no word to the landlord, they drank his ale instead,
line2: But they gagged his daughter and bound her to the foot of her

author: Alfred Noyes
line1: narrow bed;
line2: Two of them knelt at her casement, with muskets at their side!

author: Alfred Noyes
line1: There was death at every window;
line2: And hell at one dark window;

author: Alfred Noyes
line1: For Bess could see, through her casement, the road that he would
line2: ride.

author: Alfred Noyes
line1: They had tied her up to attention, with many a sniggering jest;
line2: They had bound a musket beside her, with the barrel beneath her

author: Alfred Noyes
line1: breast!

author: Alfred Noyes
line1: She heard the dead man say–

author: Alfred Noyes
line1: Watch for me by moonlight;
line2: I’ll come to thee by moonlight, though hell should bar the way!

author: Alfred Noyes
line1: She twisted her hands behind her; but all the knots held good!

author: Alfred Noyes
line1: They stretched and strained in the darkness, and the hours crawled

author: Alfred Noyes

author: Alfred Noyes

author: Alfred Noyes

author: Alfred Noyes
line1: For the road lay bare in the moonlight;
line2: Blank and bare in the moonlight;

author: Alfred Noyes

line1: hear?

author: Alfred Noyes
line1: When the moon is a ghostly galleon tossed upon cloudy seas,
line2: When the road is a ribbon of moonlight over the purple moor,

author: Alfred Noyes
line1: A highwayman comes riding–
line2: Riding–riding–

author: Alfred Noyes
line1: A highwayman comes riding, up to the old inn-door.
line2: Over the cobbles he clatters and clangs in the dark inn-yard;

author: Alfred Noyes
line1: He taps with his whip on the shutters, but all is locked and barred;
line2: He whistles a tune to the window, and who should be waiting there

author: Alfred Noyes
line1: But the landlord’s black-eyed daughter,
line2: Bess, the landlord’s daughter,

author: Oliver Goldsmith
line1: Beside yon straggling fence that skirts the way
line2: With blossom’d furze unprofitably gay,

author: Oliver Goldsmith
line1: There, in his mansion, skill’d to rule,
line2: The village master taught his little school;

author: Oliver Goldsmith
line1: A man severe he was, and stern to view,
line2: I knew him well, and every truant knew;

author: Oliver Goldsmith
line1: Well had the boding tremblers learn’d to trace
line2: The days disasters in his morning face;

author: Oliver Goldsmith
line1: Full well they laugh’d with counterfeited glee,
line2: At all his jokes, for many a joke had he:

author: Oliver Goldsmith
line1: Full well the busy whisper, circling round,
line2: Convey’d the dismal tidings when he frown’d:

author: Oliver Goldsmith
line1: Yet he was kind; or if severe in aught,
line2: The love he bore to learning was in fault.

author: Oliver Goldsmith
line1: The village all declar’d how much he knew;
line2: ‘Twas certain he could write, and cipher too:

author: Oliver Goldsmith
line1: Lands he could measure, terms and tides presage,
line2: And e’en the story ran that he could gauge.

author: Oliver Goldsmith
line1: In arguing too, the person own’d his skill,
line2: For e’en though vanquish’d he could argue still;

author: Oliver Goldsmith
line1: While words of learned length and thund’ring sound
line2: Amazed the gazing rustics rang’d around;

author: Oliver Goldsmith
line1: And still they gaz’d and still the wonder grew,
line2: That one small head could carry all he knew.

author: Oliver Goldsmith
line1: But past is all his fame. The very spot
line2: Where many a time he triumph’d is forgot.

author: William Wordsworth
line1: Behold her, single in the field,
line2: Yon solitary Highland Lass!

author: William Wordsworth
line1: Reaping and singing by herself;
line2: Stop here, or gently pass!

author: William Wordsworth
line1: Alone she cuts and binds the grain,
line2: And sings a melancholy strain;

author: William Wordsworth
line1: O listen! for the Vale profound
line2: Is overflowing with the sound.

author: William Wordsworth
line1: No Nightingale did ever chaunt
line2: More welcome notes to weary bands

author: William Wordsworth
line1: Of travellers in some shady haunt,
line2: Among Arabian sands:

author: William Wordsworth
line1: A voice so thrilling ne’er was heard
line2: In spring-time from the Cuckoo-bird,

author: William Wordsworth
line1: Breaking the silence of the seas
line2: Among the farthest Hebrides.

author: William Wordsworth
line1: Will no one tell me what she sings?–
line2: Perhaps the plaintive numbers flow

author: William Wordsworth
line1: For old, unhappy, far-off things,
line2: And battles long ago:

author: William Wordsworth
line1: Or is it some more humble lay,
line2: Familiar matter of to-day?

author: William Wordsworth
line1: Some natural sorrow, loss, or pain,
line2: That has been, and may be again?

author: William Wordsworth
line1: Whate’er the theme, the Maiden sang
line2: As if her song could have no ending;

author: William Wordsworth
line1: I saw her singing at her work,
line2: And o’er the sickle bending;–

author: William Wordsworth
line1: I listened, motionless and still;
line2: And, as I mounted up the hill

author: William Wordsworth
line1: The music in my heart I bore,
line2: Long after it was heard no more.

author: Sir Thomas Campbell
line1: A chieftain, to the Highlands bound,
line2: Cries, "Boatman, do not tarry!

author: Sir Thomas Campbell
line1: And I’ll give thee a silver pound
line2: To row us o’er the ferry!"–

author: Sir Thomas Campbell
line1: "Now, who be ye, would cross Lochgyle,
line2: This dark and stormy weather?"

author: Sir Thomas Campbell
line1: "O, I’m the chief of Ulva’s isle,
line2: And this, Lord Ullin’s daughter.–

author: Sir Thomas Campbell
line1: "And fast before her father’s men
line2: Three days we’ve fled together,

author: Sir Thomas Campbell
line1: For should he find us in the glen,
line2: My blood would stain the heather.

author: Sir Thomas Campbell
line1: "His horsemen hard behind us ride;
line2: Should they our steps discover,

author: Sir Thomas Campbell
line1: Then who will cheer my bonny bride
line2: When they have slain her lover?"–

author: Sir Thomas Campbell
line1: Out spoke the hardy Highland wight,–
line2: "I’ll go, my chief–I’m ready:–

author: Sir Thomas Campbell
line1: It is not for your silver bright;
line2: But for your winsome lady:

author: Sir Thomas Campbell
line1: "And by my word! the bonny bird
line2: In danger shall not tarry;

author: Sir Thomas Campbell
line1: So, though the waves are raging white,
line2: I’ll row you o’er the ferry."–

author: Sir Thomas Campbell
line1: By this the storm grew loud apace,
line2: The water-wraith was shrieking;

author: Sir Thomas Campbell
line1: And in the scowl of heaven each face
line2: Grew dark as they were speaking.

author: Sir Thomas Campbell
line1: But still as wilder blew the wind,
line2: And as the night grew drearer,

author: Sir Thomas Campbell
line1: Adown the glen rode armèd men,
line2: Their trampling sounded nearer.–

author: Sir Thomas Campbell
line1: "O haste thee, haste!" the lady cries,
line2: "Though tempests round us gather;

author: Sir Thomas Campbell
line1: I’ll meet the raging of the skies,
line2: But not an angry father."–

author: Sir Thomas Campbell
line1: The boat has left a stormy land,
line2: A stormy sea before her,–

author: Sir Thomas Campbell
line1: When, O! too strong for human hand,
line2: The tempest gather’d o’er her.

author: Sir Thomas Campbell
line1: And still they row’d amidst the roar
line2: Of waters fast prevailing:

author: Sir Thomas Campbell
line1: Lord Ullin reach’d that fatal shore,–
line2: His wrath was changed to wailing.

author: Sir Thomas Campbell
line1: For, sore dismay’d through storm and shade,
line2: His child he did discover:–

author: Sir Thomas Campbell
line1: One lovely hand she stretch’d for aid,
line2: And one was round her lover.

author: Sir Thomas Campbell
line1: "Come back! come back!" he cried in grief
line2: "Across this stormy water:

author: Sir Thomas Campbell
line1: And I’ll forgive your Highland chief,
line2: My daughter!–O my daughter!"

author: Sir Thomas Campbell
line1: ‘Twas vain: the loud waves lash’d the shore,
line2: Return or aid preventing:

author: Sir Thomas Campbell
line1: The waters wild went o’er his child,
line2: And he was left lamenting.

author: Robert Southey
line1: My days among the Dead are past;
line2: Around me I behold,

author: Robert Southey
line1: Where’er these casual eyes are cast,
line2: The mighty minds of old:

author: Robert Southey
line1: My never-failing friends are they,
line2: With whom I converse day by day.

author: Robert Southey
line1: With them I take delight in weal
line2: And seek relief in woe;

author: Robert Southey
line1: And while I understand and feel
line2: How much to them I owe,

author: Robert Southey
line1: My cheeks have often been bedew’d
line2: With tears of thoughtful gratitude.

author: Robert Southey
line1: My thoughts are with the Dead; with them
line2: I live in long-past years,

author: Robert Southey
line1: Their virtues love, their faults condemn,
line2: Partake their hopes and fears,

author: Robert Southey
line1: And from their lessons seek and find
line2: Instruction with an humble mind.

author: Robert Southey
line1: My hopes are with the Dead; anon
line2: My place with them will be,

author: Robert Southey
line1: And I with them shall travel on
line2: Through all Futurity;

author: Robert Southey
line1: Yet leaving here a name, I trust,
line2: That will not perish in the dust.

author: James Leigh Hunt
line1: Abou Ben Adhem (may his tribe increase!)
line2: Awoke one night from a deep dream of peace,

author: James Leigh Hunt
line1: And saw, within the moonlight in his room,
line2: Making it rich, and like a lily in bloom,

author: James Leigh Hunt
line1: An Angel writing in a book of gold:
line2: Exceeding peace had made Ben Adhem bold,

author: James Leigh Hunt
line1: And to the Presence in the room he said,
line2: "What writest thou?" The Vision raised its head,

author: James Leigh Hunt
line1: And with a look made of all sweet accord
line2: Answered, "The names of those who love the Lord."

author: James Leigh Hunt
line1: "And is mine one?" said Abou. "Nay, not so,"
line2: Replied the Angel. Abou spoke more low,

author: James Leigh Hunt
line1: But cheerily still; and said, "I pray thee, then,
line2: Write me as one who loves his fellow men."

author: James Leigh Hunt
line1: The Angel wrote, and vanished. The next night
line2: It came again with a great wakening light,

author: James Leigh Hunt
line1: And showed the names whom love of God had blessed,
line2: And, lo! Ben Adhem’s name led all the rest!

author: Robert Louis Stevenson
line1: Give to me the life I love,
line2: Let the lave go by me,

author: Robert Louis Stevenson
line1: Give the jolly heaven above
line2: And the byway nigh me.

author: Robert Louis Stevenson
line1: Bed in the bush with stars to see,
line2: Bread I dip in the river -

author: Robert Louis Stevenson
line1: There’s the life for a man like me,
line2: There’s the life for ever.

author: Robert Louis Stevenson
line1: Let the blow fall soon or late,
line2: Let what will be o’er me;

author: Robert Louis Stevenson
line1: Give the face of earth around
line2: And the road before me.

author: Robert Louis Stevenson
line1: Wealth I seek not, hope nor love,
line2: Nor a friend to know me;

author: Robert Louis Stevenson
line1: All I seek, the heaven above
line2: And the road below me.

author: Robert Louis Stevenson
line1: Or let autumn fall on me
line2: Where afield I linger,

author: Robert Louis Stevenson
line1: Silencing the bird on tree,
line2: Biting the blue finger.

author: Robert Louis Stevenson
line1: White as meal the frosty field -
line2: Warm the fireside haven -

author: Robert Louis Stevenson
line1: Not to autumn will I yield,
line2: Not to winter even!

author: Robert Louis Stevenson
line1: Let the blow fall soon or late,
line2: Let what will be o’er me;

author: Robert Louis Stevenson
line1: Give the face of earth around,
line2: And the road before me.

author: Robert Louis Stevenson
line1: Wealth I ask not, hope nor love,
line2: Nor a friend to know me;

author: Robert Louis Stevenson
line1: All I ask, the heaven above
line2: And the road below me.

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: A Lay Made About the Year Of The City CCCLX
line2: I

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: Lars Porsena of Closium
line2: By the Nine Gods he swore

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: That the great house of Tarquin
line2: Should suffer wrong no more.

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: By the Nine Gods he swore it,
line2: And named a trysting day,

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: And bade his messengers ride forth,
line2: East and west and south and north,

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: To summon his array.
line2: II

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: East and west and south and north
line2: The messengers ride fast,

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: And tower and town and cottage
line2: Have heard the trumpet’s blast.

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: Shame on the false Etruscan
line2: Who lingers in his home,

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: When Porsena of Clusium
line2: Is on the march for Rome.

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: III
line2: The horsemen and the footmen

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: Are pouring in amain
line2: From many a stately market-place,

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: From many a fruitful plain,
line2: From many a lonely hamlet,

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: Which, hid by beech and pine,
line2: Like an eagle’s nest, hangs on the crest

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: Of purple Apennine;
line2: IV

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: From lordly Volaterræ,
line2: Where scowls the far-famed hold

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: Piled by the hands of giants
line2: For godlike kings of old;

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: From seagirt Populonia,
line2: Whose sentinels descry

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: Sardinia’s snowy mountain-tops
line2: Fringing the southern sky;

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: V
line2: From the proud mart of Pisæ,

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: Queen of the western waves,
line2: Where ride Massilia’s triremes

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: Heavy with fair-haired slaves;
line2: From where sweet Clanis wanders

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: Through corn and vines and flowers;
line2: From where Cortona lifts to heaven

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: Her diadem of towers.
line2: VI

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: Tall are the oaks whose acorns
line2: Drop in dark Auser’s rill;

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: Fat are the stags that champ the boughs
line2: Of the Ciminian hill;

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: Beyond all streams Clitumnus
line2: Is to the herdsman dear;

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: Best of all pools the fowler loves
line2: The great Volsinian mere.

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: VII
line2: But now no stroke of woodman

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: Is heard by Auser’s rill;
line2: No hunter tracks the stag’s green path

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: Up the Ciminian hill;
line2: Unwatched along Clitumnus

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: Grazes the milk-white steer;
line2: Unharmed the water fowl may dip

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: In the Volsminian mere.
line2: VIII

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: The harvests of Arretium,
line2: This year, old men shall reap;

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: This year, young boys in Umbro
line2: Shall plunge the struggling sheep;

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: And in the vats of Luna,
line2: This year, the must shall foam

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: Round the white feet of laughing girls
line2: Whose sires have marched to Rome.

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: IX
line2: There be thirty chosen prophets,

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: The wisest of the land,
line2: Who alway by Lars Porsena

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: Both morn and evening stand:
line2: Evening and morn the Thirty

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: Have turned the verses o’er,
line2: Traced from the right on linen white

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: By mighty seers of yore.
line2: X

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: And with one voice the Thirty
line2: Have their glad answer given:

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: "Go forth, go forth, Lars Porsena;
line2: Go forth, beloved of Heaven;

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: Go, and return in glory
line2: To Clusium’s royal dome;

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: And hang round Nurscia’s altars
line2: The golden shields of Rome."

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: XI
line2: And now hath every city

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: Sent up her tale of men;
line2: The foot are fourscore thousand,

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: The horse are thousands ten.
line2: Before the gates of Sutrium

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: Is met the great array.
line2: A proud man was Lars Porsena

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: Upon the trysting day.
line2: XII

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: For all the Etruscan armies
line2: Were ranged beneath his eye,

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: And many a banished Roman,
line2: And many a stout ally;

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: And with a mighty following
line2: To join the muster came

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: The Tusculan Mamilius,
line2: Prince of the Latian name.

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: XIII
line2: But by the yellow Tiber

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: Was tumult and affright:
line2: From all the spacious champaign

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: To Rome men took their flight.
line2: A mile around the city,

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: The throng stopped up the ways;
line2: A fearful sight it was to see

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: Through two long nights and days.
line2: XIV

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: For aged folks on crutches,
line2: And women great with child,

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: And mothers sobbing over babes
line2: That clung to them and smiled,

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: And sick men borne in litters
line2: High on the necks of slaves,

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: And troops of sun-burned husbandmen
line2: With reaping-hooks and staves,

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: XV
line2: And droves of mules and asses

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: Laden with skins of wine,
line2: And endless flocks of goats and sheep,

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: And endless herds of kine,
line2: And endless trains of wagons

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: That creaked beneath the weight
line2: Of corn-sacks and of household goods,

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: Choked every roaring gate.
line2: XVI

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: Now, from the rock Tarpeian,
line2: Could the wan burghers spy

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: The line of blazing villages
line2: Red in the midnight sky.

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: The Fathers of the City,
line2: They sat all night and day,

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: For every hour some horseman come
line2: With tidings of dismay.

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: XVII
line2: To eastward and to westward

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: Have spread the Tuscan bands;
line2: Nor house, nor fence, nor dovecote

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: In Crustumerium stands.
line2: Verbenna down to Ostia

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: Hath wasted all the plain;
line2: Astur hath stormed Janiculum,

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: And the stout guards are slain.
line2: XVIII

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: I wis, in all the Senate, [wis: know]
line2: There was no heart so bold,

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: But sore it ached, and fast it beat,
line2: When that ill news was told.

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: Forthwith up rose the Consul,
line2: Up rose the Fathers all;

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: In haste they girded up their gowns,
line2: And hied them to the wall.

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: XIX
line2: They held a council standing,

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: Before the River-Gate;
line2: Short time was there, ye well may guess,

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: For musing or debate.
line2: Out spake the Consul roundly:

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: "The bridge must straight go down;
line2: For, since Janiculum is lost,

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: Nought else can save the town."
line2: XX

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: Just then a scout came flying,
line2: All wild with haste and fear:

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: "To arms! to arms! Sir Consul:
line2: Lars Porsena is here."

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: On the low hills to westward
line2: The Consul fixed his eye,

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: And saw the swarthy storm of dust
line2: Rise fast along the sky.

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: XXI
line2: And nearer fast and nearer

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: Doth the red whirlwind come;
line2: And louder still and still more loud,

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: From underneath that rolling cloud,
line2: Is heard the trumpet’s war-note proud,

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: The trampling, and the hum.
line2: And plainly and more plainly

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: Now through the gloom appears,
line2: Far to left and far to right,

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: In broken gleams of dark-blue light,
line2: The long array of helmets bright,

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: The long array of spears.
line2: XXII

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: And plainly and more plainly,
line2: Above that glimmering line,

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: Now might ye see the banners
line2: Of twelve fair cities shine;

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: But the banner of proud Clusium
line2: Was highest of them all,

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: The terror of the Umbrian,
line2: The terror of the Gaul.

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: XXIII
line2: And plainly and more plainly

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: Now might the burghers know,
line2: By port and vest, by horse and crest,

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: Each warlike Lucumo.
line2: There Cilnius of Arretium

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: On his fleet roan was seen;
line2: And Astur of the four-fold shield,

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: Girt with the brand none else may wield,
line2: Tolumnius with the belt of gold,

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: And dark Verbenna from the hold
line2: By reedy Thrasymene.

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: XXIV
line2: Fast by the royal standard,

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: O’erlooking all the war,
line2: Lars Porsena of Clusium

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: Sat in his ivory car.
line2: By the right wheel rode Mamilius,

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: Prince of the Latian name;
line2: And by the left false Sextus,

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: That wrought the deed of shame.
line2: XXV

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: But when the face of Sextus
line2: Was seen among the foes,

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: A yell that rent the firmament
line2: From all the town arose.

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: On the house-tops was no woman
line2: But spat towards him and hissed,

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: No child but screamed out curses,
line2: And shook its little fist.

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: XXVI
line2: But the Consul’s brow was sad,

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: And the Consul’s speech was low,
line2: And darkly looked he at the wall,

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: And darkly at the foe.
line2: "Their van will be upon us

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: Before the bridge goes down;
line2: And if they once may win the bridge,

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: What hope to save the town?"
line2: XXVII

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: Then out spake brave Horatius,
line2: The Captain of the Gate:

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: "To every man upon this earth
line2: Death cometh soon or late.

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: And how can man die better
line2: Than facing fearful odds,

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: For the ashes of his fathers,
line2: And the temples of his gods,

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: XXVIII
line2: "And for the tender mother

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: Who dandled him to rest,
line2: And for the wife who nurses

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: His baby at her breast,
line2: And for the holy maidens

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: Who feed the eternal flame,
line2: To save them from false Sextus

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: That wrought the deed of shame?
line2: XXIX

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: "Haul down the bridge, Sir Consul,
line2: With all the speed ye may;

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: I, with two more to help me,
line2: Will hold the foe in play.

author: Thomas Babbington Macaulay
line1: In yon strait path a thousand

author: Lord George Gordon Noel Byron
line1: And all that’s best of dark and bright
line2: Meet in her aspect and her eyes;

author: Lord George Gordon Noel Byron
line1: Thus mellowed to the tender light
line2: Which heaven to gaudy day denies.

author: Lord George Gordon Noel Byron
line1: One ray the more, one shade the less
line2: Had half impaired the nameless grace

author: Lord George Gordon Noel Byron
line1: Which waves in every raven tress
line2: Or softly lightens o’er her face,

author: Lord George Gordon Noel Byron
line1: Where thoughts serenely sweet express
line2: How pure, how dear their dwelling place.

author: Lord George Gordon Noel Byron
line1: And on that cheek and o’er that brow
line2: So soft, so calm yet eloquent,

author: Lord George Gordon Noel Byron
line1: The smiles that win, the tints that glow
line2: But tell of days in goodness spent

author: Lord George Gordon Noel Byron
line1: A mind at peace with all below,
line2: A heart whose love is innocent.

poemTitle: Believe Me, if all those Endearing Young Charms
author: Thomas Moore
line1: Believe me, if all those endearing young charms,
line2: Which I gaze on so fondly to-day

poemTitle: Believe Me, if all those Endearing Young Charms
author: Thomas Moore
line1: Were to change by to-morrow, and fleet in my arms,
line2: Like fairy-gifts fading away,

poemTitle: Believe Me, if all those Endearing Young Charms
author: Thomas Moore
line1: Thou wouldst still be adored, as this moment thou art,
line2: Let thy loveliness fade as it will,

poemTitle: Believe Me, if all those Endearing Young Charms
author: Thomas Moore
line1: And around the dear ruin each wish of my heart
line2: Would entwine itself verdantly still.

poemTitle: Believe Me, if all those Endearing Young Charms
author: Thomas Moore
line1: It is not while beauty and youth are thine own,
line2: And they cheeks unprofaned by a tear,

poemTitle: Believe Me, if all those Endearing Young Charms
author: Thomas Moore
line1: That the fervor and faith of a soul can be known,
line2: To which time will but make thee more dear;

poemTitle: Believe Me, if all those Endearing Young Charms
author: Thomas Moore
line1: No, the heart that has truly loved never forgets,
line2: But as truly loves on to the close,

poemTitle: Believe Me, if all those Endearing Young Charms
author: Thomas Moore
line1: As the sun-flower turns on her god, when he sets,
line2: The same look which she turned when he rose.

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: I arise from dreams of thee
line2: In the first sweet sleep of night,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: When the winds are breathing low,
line2: And the stars are shining bright

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: I arise from dreams of thee,
line2: And a spirit in my feet

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Hath led me — who knows how? —
line2: To thy chamber window, Sweet!

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: The wandering airs they faint
line2: On the dark, the silent stream —

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: The champak odors fail
line2: Like sweet thoughts in a dream;

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: The nightingale’s complaint,
line2: It dies upon her heart;

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: As I must on thine,
line2: Oh, beloved as thou art!

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: O lift me from the grass!
line2: I die! I faint! I fail!

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Let thy love in kisses rain
line2: On my lips and eyelids pale.

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: My cheek is cold and white, alas!
line2: My heart beats loud and fast;—

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Oh! press it to thine own again,
line2: Where it will break at last.

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And the first grey of morning fill’d the east,
line2: And the fog rose out of the Oxus stream.

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: But all the Tartar camp along the stream
line2: Was hush’d, and still the men were plunged in sleep;

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Sohrab alone, he slept not; all night long
line2: He had lain wakeful, tossing on his bed;

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: But when the grey dawn stole into his tent,
line2: He rose, and clad himself, and girt his sword,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And took his horseman’s cloak, and left his tent,
line2: And went abroad into the cold wet fog,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Through the dim camp to Peran-Wisa’s tent.
line2: Through the black Tartar tents he pass’d, which stood

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Clustering like bee-hives on the low flat strand
line2: Of Oxus, where the summer-floods o’erflow

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: When the sun melts the snows in high Pamere:
line2: Through the black tents he pass’d, o’er that low strand,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And to a hillock came, a little back
line2: From the stream’s brink, the spot where first a boat,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Crossing the stream in summer, scrapes the land.
line2: The men of former times had crown’d the top

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: With a clay fort: but that was fall’n; and now
line2: The Tartars built there Peran-Wisa’s tent,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: A dome of laths, and o’er it felts were spread.
line2: And Sohrab came there, and went in, and stood

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Upon the thick piled carpets in the tent,
line2: And found the old man sleeping on his bed

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Of rugs and felts, and near him lay his arms.
line2: And Peran-Wisa heard him, though the step

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Was dull’d; for he slept light, an old man’s sleep;
line2: And he rose quickly on one arm, and said:—

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: ‘Who art thou? for it is not yet clear dawn.
line2: Speak! is there news, or any night alarm?’

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: But Sohrab came to the bedside, and said:—
line2: ‘Thou know’st me, Peran-Wisa! it is I.

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: The sun is not yet risen, and the foe
line2: Sleep; but I sleep not; all night long I lie

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Tossing and wakeful, and I come to thee.
line2: For so did King Afrasiab bid me seek

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Thy counsel and to heed thee as thy son,
line2: In Samarcand, before the army march’d;

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And I will tell thee what my heart desires.
line2: Thou know’st if, since from Ader-baijan first

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: I came among the Tartars and bore arms,
line2: I have still served Afrasiab well, and shown,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: At my boy’s years, the courage of a man.
line2: This too thou know’st, that while I still bear on

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: The conquering Tartar ensigns through the world,
line2: And beat the Persians back on every field,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: I seek one man, one man, and one alone—
line2: Rustum, my father; who I hoped should greet,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Should one day greet, upon some well fought field,
line2: His not unworthy, not inglorious son.

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: So I long hoped, but him I never find.
line2: Come then, hear now, and grant me what I ask.

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Let the two armies rest to-day; but I
line2: Will challenge forth the bravest Persian lords

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: To meet me, man to man; if I prevail,
line2: Rustum will surely hear it; if I fall—

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Old man, the dead need no one, claim no kin.
line2: Dim is the rumour of a common fight,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Where host meets host, and many names are sunk;
line2: But of a single combat Fame speaks clear.’

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: He spoke; and Peran-Wisa took the hand
line2: Of the young man in his, and sigh’d, and said:—

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: ‘O Sohrab, an unquiet heart is thine!
line2: Canst thou not rest among the Tartar chiefs,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And share the battle’s common chance with us
line2: Who love thee, but must press for ever first,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: In single fight incurring single risk,
line2: To find a father thou hast never seen?

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: That were far best, my son, to stay with us
line2: Unmurmuring; in our tents, while it is war,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And when ’tis truce, then in Afrasiab’s towns.
line2: But, if this one desire indeed rules all,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: To seek out Rustum—seek him not through fight:
line2: Seek him in peace, and carry to his arms,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: O Sohrab, carry an unwounded son!
line2: But far hence seek him, for he is not here.

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: For now it is not as when I was young,
line2: When Rustum was in front of every fray:

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: But now he keeps apart, and sits at home,
line2: In Seistan, with Zal, his father old.

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Whether that his own mighty strength at last
line2: Feels the abhorr’d approaches of old age;

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Or in some quarrel with the Persian King.
line2: There go!—Thou wilt not? Yet my heart forebodes

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Danger or death awaits thee on this field.
line2: Fain would I know thee safe and well, though lost

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: To us; fain therefore send thee hence, in peace
line2: To seek thy father, not seek single fights

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: In vain;—but who can keep the lion’s cub
line2: From ravening, and who govern Rustum’s son?

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Go: I will grant thee what thy heart desires.’
line2: So said he, and dropp’d Sohrab’s hand, and left

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: His bed, and the warm rugs whereon he lay;
line2: And o’er his chilly limbs his woollen coat

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: He pass’d, and tied his sandals on his feet,
line2: And threw a white cloak round him, and he took

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: In his right hand a ruler’s staff, no sword;
line2: And on his head he set his sheep-skin cap,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Black, glossy, curl’d, the fleece of Kara-Kul;
line2: And raised the curtain of his tent, and call’d

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: His herald to his side, and went abroad.
line2: The sun by this had risen, and clear’d the fog

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: From the broad Oxus and the glittering sands.
line2: And from their tents the Tartar horsemen filed

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Into the open plain; so Haman bade;
line2: Haman, who next to Peran-Wisa ruled

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: The host, and still was in his lusty prime.
line2: From their black tents, long files of horse, they stream’d:

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: As when some grey November morn the files,
line2: In marching order spread, of long-neck’d cranes

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Stream over Casbin and the southern slopes
line2: Of Elburz, from the Aralian estuaries,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Or some frore Caspian reed-bed, southward bound
line2: For the warm Persian sea-board: so they stream’d.

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: The Tartars of the Oxus, the King’s guard,
line2: First, with black sheep-skin caps and with long spears;

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Large men, large steeds; who from Bokhara come
line2: And Khiva, and ferment the milk of mares.

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Next, the more temperate Toorkmuns of the south,
line2: The Tukas, and the lances of Salore,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And those from Attruck and the Caspian sands;
line2: Light men and on light steeds, who only drink

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: The acrid milk of camels, and their wells.
line2: And then a swarm of wandering horse, who came

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: From far, and a more doubtful service own’d;
line2: The Tartars of Ferghana, from the banks

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Of the Jaxartes, men with scanty beards
line2: And close-set skull-caps; and those wilder hordes

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Who roam o’er Kipchak and the northern waste,
line2: Kalmucks and unkempt Kuzzaks, tribes who stray

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Nearest the Pole, and wandering Kirghizzes,
line2: Who come on shaggy ponies from Pamere;

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: These all filed out from camp into the plain.
line2: And on the other side the Persians form’d;—

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: First a light cloud of horse, Tartars they seem’d,
line2: The Ilyats of Khorassan, and behind,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: The royal troops of Persia, horse and foot,
line2: Marshall’d battalions bright in burnish’d steel.

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: But Peran-Wisa with his herald came,
line2: Threading the Tartar squadrons to the front,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And with his staff kept back the foremost ranks.
line2: And when Ferood, who led the Persians, saw

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: That Peran-Wisa kept the Tartars back,
line2: He took his spear, and to the front he came,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And check’d his ranks, and fix’d them where they stood.
line2: And the old Tartar came upon the sand

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Betwixt the silent hosts, and spake, and said:—
line2: ‘Ferood, and ye, Persians and Tartars, hear!

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Let there be truce between the hosts to-day.
line2: But choose a champion from the Persian lords

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: To fight our champion Sohrab, man to man.’
line2: As, in the country, on a morn in June,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: When the dew glistens on the pearled ears,
line2: A shiver runs through the deep corn for joy—

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: So, when they heard what Peran-Wisa said,
line2: A thrill through all the Tartar squadrons ran

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Of pride and hope for Sohrab, whom they loved.
line2: But as a troop of pedlars, from Cabool,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Cross underneath the Indian Caucasus,
line2: That vast sky-neighbouring mountain of milk snow;

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Crossing so high, that, as they mount, they pass
line2: Long flocks of travelling birds dead on the snow,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Choked by the air, and scarce can they themselves
line2: Slake their parch’d throats with sugar’d mulberries—

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: In single file they move, and stop their breath,
line2: For fear they should dislodge the o’er hanging snows—

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: So the pale Persians held their breath with fear.
line2: And to Ferood his brother chiefs came up

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: To counsel; Gudurz and Zoarrah came
line2: And Feraburz, who ruled the Persian host

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Second, and was the uncle of the King
line2: These came and counsell’d, and then Gudurz said:—

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: ‘Ferood, shame bids us take their challenge up,
line2: Yet champion have we none to match this youth.

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: He has the wild stag’s foot, the lion’s heart.
line2: But Rustum came last night; aloof he sits

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And sullen, and has pitch’d his tents apart.
line2: Him will I seek, and carry to his ear

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: The Tartar challenge, and this young man’s name.
line2: Haply he will forget his wrath, and fight.

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Stand forth the while, and take their challenge up.’
line2: So spake he; and Ferood stood forth and cried.—

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: ‘Old man, be it agreed as thou hast said!
line2: Let Sohrab arm, and we will find a man.’

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: He spake: and Peran-Wisa turn’d, and strode
line2: Back through the opening squadrons to his tent.

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: But through the anxious Persians Gudurz ran,
line2: And cross’d the camp which lay behind, and reach’d,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Out on the sands beyond it, Rustum’s tents.
line2: Of scarlet cloth they were, and glittering gay

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Just pitch’d; the high pavilion in the midst
line2: Was Rustum’s, and his men lay camp’d around.

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And Gudurz enter’d Rustum’s tent, and found
line2: Rustum; his morning meal was done, but still

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: The table stood before him, charged with food—
line2: A side of roasted sheep, and cakes of bread,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And dark green melons; and there Rustum sate
line2: Listless, and held a falcon on his wrist,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And play’d with it; but Gudurz came and stood
line2: Before him; and he look’d, and saw him stand,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And with a cry sprang up and dropp’d the bird,
line2: And greeted Gudurz with both hands, and said:—

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: ‘Welcome! these eyes could see no better sight.
line2: What news? but sit down first, and eat and drink.’

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: But Gudurz stood in the tent-door, and said:—
line2: ‘Not now! a time will come to eat and drink,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: But not to-day; to-day has other needs.
line2: The armies are drawn out, and stand at gaze;

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: For from the Tartars is a challenge brought
line2: To pick a champion from the Persian lords

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: To fight their champion—and thou know’st his name—
line2: Sohrab men call him, but his birth is hid.

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: O Rustum, like thy might is this young man’s!
line2: He has the wild stag’s foot, the lion’s heart;

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And he is young, and Iran’s chiefs are old,
line2: Or else too weak; and all eyes turn to thee.

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Come down and help us, Rustum, or we lose!’
line2: He spoke; but Rustum answer’d with a smile:—

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: ‘Go to! if Iran’s chiefs are old, then I
line2: Am older; if the young are weak, the King

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Errs strangely; for the King, for Kai Khosroo,
line2: Himself is young, and honours younger men,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And lets the aged moulder to their graves.
line2: Rustum he loves no more, but loves the young—

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: The young may rise at Sohrab’s vaunts, not I.
line2: For what care I, though all speak Sohrab’s fame?

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: For would that I myself had such a son,
line2: And not that one slight helpless girl I have,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: A son so famed, so brave, to send to war,
line2: And I to tarry with the snow-hair’d Zal,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: My father, whom the robber Afghans vex,
line2: And clip his borders short, and drive his herds,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And he has none to guard his weak old age.
line2: There would I go, and hang my armour up,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And with my great name fence that weak old man,
line2: And spend the goodly treasures I have got,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And rest my age, and hear of Sohrab’s fame,
line2: And leave to death the hosts of thankless kings,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And with these slaughterous hands draw sword no more.’
line2: He spoke, and smiled; and Gudurz made reply:—-

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: ‘What then, O Rustum, will men say to this,
line2: When Sohrab dares our bravest forth, and seeks

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Thee most of all, and thou, whom most he seeks,
line2: Hidest thy face? Take heed lest men should say:

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Like some old miser, Rustum hoards his fame,
line2: And shuns to peril it with younger men.’

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And, greatly moved, then Rustum made reply:—
line2: ‘O Gudurz, wherefore dost thou say such words?

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Thou knowest better words than this to say.
line2: What is one more, one less, obscure or famed,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Valiant or craven, young or old, to me?
line2: Are not they mortal, am not I myself?

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: But who for men of nought would do great deeds?
line2: Come, thou shalt see how Rustum hoards his fame!

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: But I will fight unknown, and in plain arms;
line2: Let not men say of Rustum, he was match’d

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: In single fight with any mortal man.’
line2: He spoke, and frown’d; and Gudurz turn’d, and ran

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Back quickly through the camp in fear and joy—
line2: Fear at his wrath, but joy that Rustum came.

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: But Rustum strode to his tent-door, and call’d
line2: His followers in, and bade them bring his arms,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And clad himself in steel; the arms he chose
line2: Were plain, and on his shield was no device,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Only his helm was rich, inlaid with gold,
line2: And, from the fluted spine atop, a plume

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Of horsehair waved, a scarlet horsehair plume.
line2: So arm’d, he issued forth; and Ruksh, his horse,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Follow’d him like a faithful hound at heel—
line2: Ruksh, whose renown was noised through all the earth,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: The horse, whom Rustum on a foray once
line2: Did in Bokhara by the river find

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: A colt beneath its dam, and drove him home,
line2: And rear’d him; a bright bay, with lofty crest,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Dight with a saddle-cloth of broider’d green
line2: Crusted with gold, and on the ground were work’d

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: All beasts of chase, all beasts which hunters know:
line2: So follow’d, Rustum left his tents, and cross’d

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: The camp, and to the Persian host appear’d.
line2: And all the Persians knew him, and with shouts

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Hail’d; but the Tartars knew not who he was.
line2: And dear as the wet diver to the eyes

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Of his pale wife who waits and weeps on shore,
line2: By sandy Bahrein, in the Persian Gulf,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Plunging all day in the blue waves, at night,
line2: Having made up his tale of precious pearls,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Rejoins her in their hut upon the sands—
line2: So dear to the pale Persians Rustum came.

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And Rustum to the Persian front advanced,
line2: And Sohrab arm’d in Haman’s tent, and came.

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And as afield the reapers cut a swath
line2: Down through the middle of a rich man’s corn,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And on each side are squares of standing corn,
line2: And in the midst a stubble, short and bare—

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: So on each side were squares of men, with spears
line2: Bristling, and in the midst, the open sand.

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And Rustum came upon the sand, and cast
line2: His eyes toward the Tartar tents, and saw

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Sohrab come forth, and eyed him as he came.
line2: As some rich woman, on a winter’s morn,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Eyes through her silken curtains the poor drudge
line2: Who with numb blacken’d fingers makes her fire—

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: At cock-crow, on a starlit winter’s morn,
line2: When the frost flowers the whiten’d window-panes—

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And wonders how she lives, and what the thoughts
line2: Of that poor drudge may be; so Rustum eyed

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: The unknown adventurous youth, who from afar
line2: Came seeking Rustum, and defying forth

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: All the most valiant chiefs; long he perused
line2: His spirited air, and wonder’d who he was.

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: For very young he seem’d, tenderly rear’d;
line2: Like some young cypress, tall, and dark, and straight,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Which in a queen’s secluded garden throws
line2: Its slight dark shadow on the moonlit turf,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: By midnight, to a bubbling fountain’s sound—
line2: So slender Sohrab seem’d, so softly rear’d.

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And a deep pity enter’d Rustum’s soul
line2: As he beheld him coming; and he stood,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And beckon’d to him with his hand, and said:—
line2: ‘O thou young man, the air of Heaven is soft,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And warm, and pleasant; but the grave is cold!
line2: Heaven’s air is better than the cold dead grave.

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Behold me! I am vast, and clad in iron,
line2: And tried; and I have stood on many a field

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Of blood, and I have fought with many a foe—
line2: Never was that field lost, or that foe saved.

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: O Sohrab, wherefore wilt thou rush on death?
line2: Be govern’d! quit the Tartar host, and come

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: To Iran, and be as my son to me,
line2: And fight beneath my banner till I die!

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: There are no youths in Iran brave as thou.’
line2: So he spake, mildly; Sohrab heard his voice,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: The mighty voice of Rustum, and he saw
line2: His giant figure planted on the sand,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Sole, like some single tower, which a chief
line2: Hath builded on the waste in former years

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Against the robbers; and he saw that head,
line2: Streak’d with its first grey hairs;—hope filled his soul,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And he ran forward and embraced his knees
line2: And clasp’d his hand within his own, and said:—

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: ‘O, by thy father’s head! by thine own soul!
line2: Art thou not Rustum? speak! art thou not he?’

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: But Rustum eyed askance the kneeling youth,
line2: And turn’d away, and spake to his own soul:—

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: ‘Ah me, I muse what this young fox may mean!
