line2: False, wily, boastful, are these Tartar boys.

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: For if I now confess this thing he asks,
line2: And hide it not, but say—Rustum is here—

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: He will not yield indeed, nor quit our foes,
line2: But he will find some pretext not to fight,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And praise my fame, and proffer courteous gifts,
line2: A belt or sword perhaps, and go his way.

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And on a feast-tide, in Afrasiab’s hall,
line2: In Samarcand, he will arise and cry:

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: ‘I challenged once, when the two armies camp’d
line2: Beside the Oxus, all the Persian lords

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: To cope with me in single fight; but they
line2: Shrank, only Rustum dared; then he and I

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Changed gifts, and went on equal terms away.’
line2: So will he speak, perhaps, while men applaud;

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Then were the chiefs of Iran shamed through me.’
line2: And then he turn’d, and sternly spake aloud:—

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: ‘Rise! wherefore dost thou vainly question thus
line2: Of Rustum? I am here, whom thou hast call’d

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: By challenge forth; make good thy vaunt, or yield!
line2: Is it with Rustum only thou wouldst fight?

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Rash boy, men look on Rustum’s face and flee
line2: For well I know, that did great Rustum stand

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Before thy face this day, and were reveal’d,
line2: There would be then no talk of fighting more.

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: But being what I am, I tell thee this—
line2: Do thou record it in thine inmost soul:

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Either thou shalt renounce thy vaunt and yield,
line2: Or else thy bones shall strew this sand, till winds

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Bleach them, or Oxus with his summer-floods,
line2: Oxus in summer wash them all away.’

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: He spoke; and Sohrab answer’d, on his feet:—
line2: ‘Art thou so fierce? Thou wilt not fright me so!

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: I am no girl, to be made pale by words.
line2: Yet this thou hast said well, did Rustum stand

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Here on this field, there were no fighting then.
line2: But Rustum is far hence, and we stand here.

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Begin! thou art more vast, more dread than I,
line2: And thou art proved, I know, and I am young—

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: But yet success sways with the breath of Heaven.
line2: And though thou thinkest that thou knowest sure

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Thy victory, yet thou canst not surely know.
line2: For we are all, like swimmers in the sea,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Poised on the top of a huge wave of fate,
line2: Which hangs uncertain to which side to fall.

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And whether it will heave us up to land,
line2: Or whether it will roll us out to sea,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Back out to sea, to the deep waves of death,
line2: We know not, and no search will make us know;

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Only the event will teach us in its hour.’
line2: He spoke, and Rustum answer’d not, but hurl’d

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: His spear; down from the shoulder, down it came,
line2: As on some partridge in the corn a hawk,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: That long has tower’d in the airy clouds,
line2: Drops like a plummet; Sohrab saw it come,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And sprang aside, quick as a flash; the spear
line2: Hiss’d, and went quivering down into the sand,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Which it sent flying wide;—then Sohrab threw
line2: In turn, and full struck Rustum’s shield; sharp rang,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: The iron plates rang sharp, but turn’d the spear.
line2: And Rustum seized his club, which none but he

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Could wield; an unlopp’d trunk it was, and huge,
line2: Still rough—like those which men in treeless plains

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: To build them boats fish from the flooded rivers,
line2: Hyphasis or Hydaspes, when, high up

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: By their dark springs, the wind in winter-time
line2: Hath made in Himalayan forests wrack,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And strewn the channels with torn boughs; so huge
line2: The club which Rustum lifted now, and struck

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: One stroke; but again Sohrab sprang aside,
line2: Lithe as the glancing snake, and the club came

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Thundering to earth, and leapt from Rustum’s hand.
line2: And Rustum follow’d his own blow, and fell

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: To his knees, and with his fingers clutch’d the sand;
line2: And now might Sohrab have unsheathed his sword,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And pierced the mighty Rustum while he lay
line2: Dizzy, and on his knees, and choked with sand;

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: But he look’d on, and smiled, nor bared his sword,
line2: But courteously drew back, and spoke, and said:—

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: ‘Thou strik’st too hard! that club of thine will float
line2: Upon the summer-floods, and not my bones.

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: But rise, and be not wroth! not wroth am I;
line2: No, when I see thee, wrath forsakes my soul.

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Thou say’st, thou art not Rustum; be it so!
line2: Who art thou then, that canst so touch my soul?

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Boy as I am, I have seen battles too—
line2: Have waded foremost in their bloody waves,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And heard their hollow roar of dying men;
line2: But never was my heart thus touch’d before.

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Are they from Heaven, these softenings of the heart?
line2: O thou old warrior, let us yield to Heaven!

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Come, plant we here in earth our angry spears,
line2: And make a truce, and sit upon this sand,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And pledge each other in red wine, like friends,
line2: And thou shalt talk to me of Rustum’s deeds.

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: There are enough foes in the Persian host,
line2: Whom I may meet, and strike, and feel no pang;

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Champions enough Afrasiab has, whom thou
line2: Mayst fight; fight them, when they confront thy spear!

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: But oh, let there be peace ’twixt thee and me!’
line2: He ceas’d, but while he spake, Rustum had risen,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And stood erect, trembling with rage; his club
line2: He left to lie, but had regain’d his spear,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Whose fiery point now in his mail’d right-hand
line2: Blazed bright and baleful, like that autumn-star,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: The baleful sign of fevers; dust had soil’d
line2: His stately crest, and dimm’d his glittering arms.

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: His breast heaved, his lips foam’d, and twice his voice
line2: Was choked with rage; at last these words broke way.—

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: ‘Girl! nimble with thy feet, not with thy hands!
line2: Curl’d minion, dancer, coiner of sweet words!

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Fight, let me hear thy hateful voice no more!
line2: Thou art not in Afrasiab’s gardens now

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: With Tartar girls, with whom thou art wont to dance;
line2: But on the Oxus-sands, and in the dance

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Of battle, and with me, who make no play
line2: Of war; I fight it out, and hand to hand.

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Speak not to me of truce, and pledge, and wine!
line2: Remember all thy valour; try thy feints

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And cunning! all the pity I had is gone;
line2: Because thou hast shamed me before both the hosts

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: With thy light skipping tricks, and thy girl’s wiles.’
line2: He spoke, and Sohrab kindled at his taunts,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And he too drew his sword; at once they rush’d
line2: Together, as two eagles on one prey

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Come rushing down together from the clouds,
line2: One from the east, one from the west; their shields

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Dash’d with a clang together, and a din
line2: Rose, such as that the sinewy woodcutters

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Make often in the forest’s heart at morn,
line2: Of hewing axes, crashing trees—such blows

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Rustum and Sohrab on each other hail’d.
line2: And you would say that sun and stars took part

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: In that unnatural conflict; for a cloud
line2: Grew suddenly in Heaven, and dark’d the sun

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Over the fighters’ heads; and a wind rose
line2: Under their feet, and moaning swept the plain,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And in a sandy whirlwind wrapp’d the pair.
line2: In gloom they twain were wrapp’d, and they alone;

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: For both the on-looking hosts on either hand
line2: Stood in broad daylight, and the sky was pure,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And the sun sparkled on the Oxus stream.
line2: But in the gloom they fought, with bloodshot eyes

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And labouring breath; first Rustum struck the shield
line2: Which Sohrab held stiff out; the steel-spiked spear

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Rent the tough plates, but fail’d to reach the skin,
line2: And Rustum pluck’d it back with angry groan.

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Then Sohrab with his sword smote Rustum’s helm,
line2: Nor clove its steel quite through; but all the crest

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: He shore away, and that proud horsehair plume,
line2: Never till now defiled, sank to the dust;

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And Rustum bow’d his head; but then the gloom
line2: Grew blacker, thunder rumbled in the air,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And lightnings rent the cloud; and Ruksh, the horse,
line2: Who stood at hand, utter’d a dreadful cry;—

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: No horse’s cry was that, most like the roar
line2: Of some pain’d desert-lion, who all day

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Hath trail’d the hunter’s javelin in his side,
line2: And comes at night to die upon the sand.

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: The two hosts heard that cry, and quaked for fear,
line2: And Oxus curdled as it cross’d his stream.

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: But Sohrab heard, and quail’d not, but rush’d on,
line2: And struck again; and again Rustum bow’d

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: His head; but this time all the blade, like glass,
line2: Sprang in a thousand shivers on the helm,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And in the hand the hilt remain’d alone.
line2: Then Rustum raised his head; his dreadful eyes

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Glared, and he shook on high his menacing spear,
line2: And shouted: Rustum!—Sohrab heard that shout,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And shrank amazed; back he recoil’d one step,
line2: And scann’d with blinking eyes the advancing form,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And then he stood bewilder’d; and he dropp’d
line2: His covering shield, and the spear pierced his side.

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: He reel’d, and staggering back, sank to the ground;
line2: And then the gloom dispersed, and the wind fell,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And the bright sun broke forth, and melted all
line2: The cloud; and the two armies saw the pair—

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Saw Rustum standing, safe upon his feet,
line2: And Sohrab, wounded, on the bloody sand.

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Then, with a bitter smile, Rustum began:—
line2: ‘Sohrab, thou thoughtest in thy mind to kill

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: A Persian lord this day, and strip his corpse
line2: And bear thy trophies to Afrasiab’s tent.

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Or else that the great Rustum would come down
line2: Himself to fight, and that thy wiles would move

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: His heart to take a gift, and let thee go.
line2: And then that all the Tartar host would praise

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Thy courage or thy craft, and spread thy fame,
line2: To glad thy father in his weak old age.

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Fool, thou art slain, and by an unknown man!
line2: Dearer to the red jackals shalt thou be

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Than to thy friends, and to thy father old.’
line2: And, with a fearless mien, Sohrab replied:—

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: ‘Unknown thou art; yet thy fierce vaunt is vain.
line2: Thou dost not slay me, proud and boastful man!

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: No! Rustum slays me, and this filial heart.
line2: For were I match’d with ten such men as thee,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And I were that which till to-day I was,
line2: They should be lying here, I standing there.

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: But that belovèd name unnerved my arm—
line2: That name, and something, I confess, in thee,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Which troubles all my heart, and made my shield
line2: Fall; and thy spear transfix’d an unarm’d foe.

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And now thou boastest, and insult’st my fate.
line2: But hear thou this, fierce man, tremble to hear:

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: The mighty Rustum shall avenge my death!
line2: My father, whom I seek through all the world,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: He shall avenge my death, and punish thee!’
line2: As when some hunter in the spring hath found

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: A breeding eagle sitting on her nest,
line2: Upon the craggy isle of a hill-lake,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And pierced her with an arrow as she rose,
line2: And follow’d her to find her where she fell

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Far off;—anon her mate comes winging back
line2: From hunting, and a great way off descries

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: His huddling young left sole; at that, he checks
line2: His pinion, and with short uneasy sweeps

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Circles above his eyry, with loud screams
line2: Chiding his mate back to her nest; but she

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Lies dying, with the arrow in her side,
line2: In some far stony gorge out of his ken,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: A heap of fluttering feathers: never more
line2: Shall the lake glass her, flying over it;

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Never the black and dripping precipices
line2: Echo her stormy scream as she sails by—

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: As that poor bird flies home, nor knows his loss,
line2: So Rustum knew not his own loss, but stood

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Over his dying son, and knew him not.
line2: But, with a cold, incredulous voice, he said:—

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: ‘What prate is this of fathers and revenge?
line2: The mighty Rustum never had a son.’

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And, with a failing voice, Sohrab replied:—
line2: ‘Ah yes, he had! and that lost son am I.

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Surely the news will one day reach his ear,
line2: Reach Rustum, where he sits, and tarries long,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Somewhere, I know not where, but far from here;
line2: And pierce him like a stab, and make him leap

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: To arms, and cry for vengeance upon thee.
line2: Fierce man, bethink thee, for an only son!

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: What will that grief, what will that vengeance be?
line2: Oh, could I live, till I that grief had seen!

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Yet him I pity not so much, but her,
line2: My mother, who in Ader-baijan dwells

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: With that old king, her father, who grows grey
line2: With age, and rules over the valiant Koords.

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Her most I pity, who no more will see
line2: Sohrab returning from the Tartar camp,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: With spoils and honour, when the war is done.
line2: But a dark rumour will be bruited up,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: From tribe to tribe, until it reach her ear;
line2: And then will that defenceless woman learn

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: That Sohrab will rejoice her sight no more,
line2: But that in battle with a nameless foe,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: By the far-distant Oxus, he is slain.’
line2: He spoke; and as he ceased, he wept aloud,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Thinking of her he left, and his own death.
line2: He spoke; but Rustum listen’d, plunged in thought.

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Nor did he yet believe it was his son
line2: Who spoke, although he call’d back names he knew;

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: For he had had sure tidings that the babe,
line2: Which was in Ader-baijan born to him,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Had been a puny girl, no boy at all—
line2: So that sad mother sent him word, for fear

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Rustum should seek the boy, to train in arms.
line2: And so he deem’d that either Sohrab took,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: By a false boast, the style of Rustum’s son;
line2: Or that men gave it him, to swell his fame.

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: So deem’d he; yet he listen’d, plunged in thought
line2: And his soul set to grief, as the vast tide

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Of the bright rocking Ocean sets to shore
line2: At the full moon; tears gather’d in his eyes;

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: For he remember’d his own early youth,
line2: And all its bounding rapture; as, at dawn,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: The shepherd from his mountain-lodge descries
line2: A far, bright city, smitten by the sun,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Through many rolling clouds;—so Rustum saw
line2: His youth; saw Sohrab’s mother, in her bloom;

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And that old king, her father, who loved well
line2: His wandering guest, and gave him his fair child

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: With joy; and all the pleasant life they led,
line2: They three, in that long-distant summer-time—

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: The castle, and the dewy woods, and hunt
line2: And hound, and morn on those delightful hills

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: In Ader-baijan. And he saw that Youth,
line2: Of age and looks to be his own dear son,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Piteous and lovely, lying on the sand,
line2: Like some rich hyacinth which by the scythe

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Of an unskilful gardener has been cut,
line2: Mowing the garden grass-plots near its bed,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And lies, a fragrant tower of purple bloom,
line2: On the mown, dying grass—so Sohrab lay,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Lovely in death, upon the common sand.
line2: And Rustum gazed on him with grief, and said:—

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: ‘O Sohrab, thou indeed art such a son
line2: Whom Rustum, wert thou his, might well have loved!

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Yet here thou errest, Sohrab, or else men
line2: Have told thee false—thou art not Rustum’s son.

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: For Rustum had no son; one child he had—
line2: But one—a girl; who with her mother now

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Plies some light female task, nor dreams of us—
line2: Of us she dreams not, nor of wounds, nor war.’

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: But Sohrab answer’d him in wrath: for now
line2: The anguish of the deep-fix’d spear grew fierce,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And he desired to draw forth the steel,
line2: And let the blood flow free, and so to die;

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: But first he would convince his stubborn foe—
line2: And, rising sternly on one arm, he said:—

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: ‘Man, who art thou who dost deny my words?
line2: Truth sits upon the lips of dying men,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And falsehood, while I lived, was far from mine.
line2: I tell thee, prick’d upon this arm I bear

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: That seal which Rustum to my mother gave,
line2: That she might prick it on the babe she bore.’

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: He spoke; and all the blood left Rustum’s cheeks,
line2: And his knees totter’d, and he smote his hand

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Against his breast, his heavy mailed hand,
line2: That the hard iron corslet clank’d aloud;

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And to his heart he press’d the other hand,
line2: And in a hollow voice he spake, and said:—

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: ‘Sohrab, that were a proof which could not lie!
line2: If thou show this, then art thou Rustum’s son.’

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Then, with weak hasty fingers, Sohrab loosed
line2: His belt, and near the shoulder bared his arm,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And show’d a sign in faint vermilion points
line2: Prick’d; as a cunning workman, in Pekin,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Pricks with vermilion some clear porcelain vase,
line2: An emperor’s gift—at early morn he paints,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And all day long, and, when night comes, the lamp
line2: Lights up his studious forehead and thin hands:—

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: So delicately prick’d the sign appear’d
line2: On Sohrab’s arm, the sign of Rustum’s seal.

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: It was that griffin, which of old rear’d Zal,
line2: Rustum’s great father, whom they left to die,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: A helpless babe, among the mountain-rocks;
line2: Him that kind creature found, and rear’d, and lov’d—

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Then Rustum took it for his glorious sign.
line2: And Sohrab bared that image on his arm,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And himself scann’d it long with mournful eyes,
line2: And then he touch’d it with his hand and said:—

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: ‘How say’st thou? Is that sign the proper sign
line2: Of Rustum’s son, or of some other man’s?’

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: He spoke; but Rustum gazed, and gazed, and stood
line2: Speechless; and then he utter’d one sharp cry:

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: O boy—thy father!—and his voice choked there.
line2: And then a dark cloud pass’d before his eyes,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And his head swam, and he sank down to earth.
line2: But Sohrab crawl’d to where he lay, and cast

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: His arms about his neck, and kiss’d his lips,
line2: And with fond faltering fingers stroked his cheeks,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Trying to call him back to life; and life
line2: Came back to Rustum, and he oped his eyes,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And they stood wide with horror; and he seized
line2: In both his hands the dust which lay around,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And threw it on his head, and smirch’d his hair,—
line2: His hair, and face, and beard, and glittering arms;

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And strong convulsive groanings shook his breast,
line2: And his sobs choked him; and he clutch’d his sword,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: To draw it, and for ever let life out.
line2: But Sohrab saw his thoughts, and held his hands

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And with a soothing voice he spake, and said:—
line2: ‘Father, forbear! for I but meet to-day

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: The doom which at my birth was written down
line2: In Heaven, and thou art Heaven’s unconscious hand.

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Surely my heart cried out that it was thou,
line2: When first I saw thee; and thy heart spoke too,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: I know it! but fate trod those promptings down
line2: Under its iron heel; fate, fate engaged

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: The strife, and hurl’d me on my father’s spear.
line2: But let us speak no more of this! I find

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: My father; let me feel that I have found!
line2: Come, sit beside me on this sand, and take

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: My head betwixt thy hands, and kiss my cheeks,
line2: And wash them with thy tears, and say: My son!

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Quick! quick! for number’d are my sands of life
line2: And swift; for like the lightning to this field

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: I came, and like the wind I go away—
line2: Sudden, and swift, and like a passing wind.

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: But it was writ in Heaven that this should be.’
line2: So said he, and his voice released the heart

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Of Rustum, and his tears broke forth; he cast
line2: His arms round his son’s neck, and wept aloud,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And kiss’d him. And awe fell on both the hosts,
line2: When they saw Rustum’s grief; and Ruksh, the horse,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: With his head bowing to the ground and mane
line2: Sweeping the dust, came near, and in mute woe

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: First to the one then to the other moved
line2: His head, as if enquiring what their grief

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Might mean; and from his dark, compassionate eyes,
line2: The big warm tears roll’d down, and caked the sand.

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: But Rustum chid him with stern voice, and said:—
line2: ‘Ruksh, now thou grievest; but, O Ruksh, thy feet

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Should first have rotted on their nimble joints,
line2: Or ere they brought thy master to this field!’

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: But Sohrab look’d upon the horse and said;—
line2: ‘Is this, then, Ruksh? How often, in past days

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: My mother told me of thee, thou brave steed,
line2: My terrible father’s terrible horse! and said,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: That I should one day find thy lord and thee.
line2: Come, let me lay my hand upon thy mane!

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: O Ruksh, thou art more fortunate than I;
line2: For thou has gone where I shall never go,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And snuff’d the breezes of my father’s home.
line2: And thou hast trod the sands of Seistan

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And seen the River of Helmund, and the Lake
line2: Of Zirrah; and the aged Zal himself

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Has often stroked thy neck, and given thee food,
line2: Corn in a golden platter soak’d with wine,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And said; O Ruksh! bear Rustrum well!—but I
line2: Have never known my grandsire’s furrow’d face,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Nor seen his lofty house in Seistan,
line2: Nor slaked my thirst at the clear Helmund stream:

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: But lodged among my father’s foes, and seen
line2: Afrasiab’s cities only, Samarcand,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Bokhara, and lone Khiva in the waste,
line2: And the black Toorkmun tents; and only drunk

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: The desert rivers, Moorghab and Tejend,
line2: Kohik, and where the Kalmuks feed their sheep,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: The northern Sir; and this great Oxus stream,
line2: The yellow Oxus, by whose brink I die.’

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Then, with a heavy groan, Rustum bewail’d:—
line2: ‘Oh, that its waves were flowing over me!

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Oh, that I saw its grains of yellow silt
line2: Roll, tumbling in the current o’er my head!’

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: But, with a grave mild voice, Sohrab replied:—
line2: ‘Desire not that, my father! thou must live.

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: For some are born to do great deeds, and live,
line2: As some are born to be obscured, and die.

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Do thou the deeds I die too young to do,
line2: And reap a second glory in thine age;

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Thou art my father, and thy gain is mine.
line2: But come! thou seest this great host of men

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Which follow me; I pray thee, slay not these!
line2: Let me entreat for them; what have they done?

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: They follow’d me, my hope, my fame, my star.
line2: Let them all cross the Oxus back in peace.

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: But me thou must bear hence, not send with them,
line2: But carry me with thee to Seistan,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And place me on a bed, and mourn for me,
line2: Thou, and the snow-hair’d Zal, and all thy friends.

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And thou must lay me in that lovely earth,
line2: And heap a stately mound above my bones,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And plant a far-seen pillar over all.
line2: That so the passing horseman on the waste

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: May see my tomb a great way off, and cry:
line2: Sohrab!, the mighty Rustum’s son, lies there,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Whom his great father did in ignorance kill—
line2: And I be not forgotten in my grave.’

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And, with a mournful voice, Rustum replied:—
line2: ‘Fear not! as thou hast said, Sohrab, my son,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: So shall it be; for I will burn my tents,
line2: And quit the host, and bear thee hence with me,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And carry thee away to Seistan,
line2: And place thee on a bed, and mourn for thee,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: With the snow-headed Zal, and all my friends.
line2: And I will lay thee in the lovely earth,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And heap a stately mound above thy bones,
line2: And plant a far-seen pillar over all,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And men shall not forget thee in thy grave.
line2: And I will spare thy host: yea, let them go!

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Let them all cross the Oxus back in peace!
line2: What should I do with slaying any more?

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: For would that all whom I have ever slain
line2: Might be once more alive; my bitterest foes

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And they who were call’d champions in their time,
line2: And through whose death I won that fame I have—

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And I were nothing but a common man,
line2: A poor, mean soldier, and without renown,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: So thou mightest live too, my son, my son!
line2: Or rather would that I, even I myself,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Might now be lying on this bloody sand,
line2: Near death, and by an ignorant stroke of thine,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Not thou of mine! and I might die, not thou;
line2: And I, not thou, be borne to Seistan;

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And Zal might weep above my grave, not thine;
line2: And say: O son, I weep thee not too sore,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: For willingly, I know, thou met’st thine end.—
line2: But now in blood and battles was my youth,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And full of blood and battles is my age,
line2: And I shall never end this life of blood.’

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Then, at the point of death, Sohrab replied.—
line2: ‘A life of blood indeed, thou dreadful man!

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: But thou shalt yet have peace; only not now,
line2: Not yet! but thou shalt have it on that day,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: When thou shalt sail in a high-masted ship,
line2: Thou and the other peers of Kai Khosroo,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Returning home over the salt blue sea,
line2: From laying thy dear master in his grave.’

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And Rustum gazed in Sohrab’s face, and said.—
line2: ‘Soon be that day, my son, and deep that sea!

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Till then, if Fate so wills, let me endure.’
line2: He spoke; and Sohrab smiled on him, and took

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: The spear, and drew it from his side, and eased
line2: His wound’s imperious anguish; but the blood

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Came welling from the open gash, and life
line2: Flow’d with the stream;—all down his cold white side

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: The crimson torrent ran, dim now and soil’d,
line2: Like the soil’d tissue of white violets

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Left, freshly gather’d, on their native bank,
line2: By children whom their nurses call with haste

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Indoors from the sun’s eye; his head droop’d low,
line2: His limbs grew slack; motionless, white, he lay—

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: White, with eyes closed; only when heavy gasps,
line2: Deep heavy gasps quivering through all his frame,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Convulsed him back to life, he open’d them,
line2: And fix’d them feebly on his father’s face;

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Till now all strength was ebb’d, and from his limbs
line2: Unwillingly the spirit fled away,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Regretting the warm mansion which it left,
line2: And youth, and bloom, and this delightful world.

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: So, on the bloody sand, Sohrab lay dead;
line2: And the great Rustum drew his horseman’s cloak

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Down o’er his face, and sate by his dead son.
line2: As those black granite pillars, once high-rear’d

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: By Jemshid in Persepolis, to bear
line2: His house, now ’mid their broken flights of steps

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Lie prone, enormous, down the mountain side—
line2: So in the sand lay Rustum by his son.

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And night came down over the solemn waste,
line2: And the two gazing hosts, and that sole pair,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And darken’d all; and a cold fog, with night,
line2: Crept from the Oxus. Soon a hum arose,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: As of a great assembly loosed, and fires
line2: Began to twinkle through the fog; for now

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Both armies moved to camp, and took their meal;
line2: The Persians took it on the open sands

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Southward, the Tartars by the river marge;
line2: And Rustum and his son were left alone.

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: But the majestic river floated on,
line2: Out of the mist and hum of that low land,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Into the frosty starlight, and there moved,
line2: Rejoicing, through the hush’d Chorasmian waste,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Under the solitary moon: he flow’d
line2: Right for the polar star, past Orgunjè,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Brimming, and bright, and large; then sands begin
line2: To hem his watery march, and dam his streams,

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And split his currents; that for many a league
line2: The shorn and parcell’d Oxus strains along

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: Through beds of sand and matted rushy isles—
line2: Oxus, forgetting the bright speed he had

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: In his high mountain-cradle in Pamere,
line2: A foil’d circuitous wanderer:—till at last

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: The long’d-for dash of waves is heard, and wide
line2: His luminous home of waters opens, bright

author: Matthew Arnold
line1: And tranquil, from whose floor the new-bathed stars
line2: Emerge, and shine upon the Aral Sea.

poemTitle: She is not Fair to Outward View
author: Hartley Coleridge
line1: She is not fair to outward view
line2: As many maidens be,

poemTitle: She is not Fair to Outward View
author: Hartley Coleridge
line1: Her loveliness I never knew
line2: Until she smiled on me;

poemTitle: She is not Fair to Outward View
author: Hartley Coleridge
line1: O, then I saw her eye was bright,
line2: A well of love, a spring of light!

poemTitle: She is not Fair to Outward View
author: Hartley Coleridge
line1: But now her looks are coy and cold,
line2: To mine they ne’er reply,

poemTitle: She is not Fair to Outward View
author: Hartley Coleridge
line1: And yet I cease not to behold
line2: The love-light in her eye:

poemTitle: She is not Fair to Outward View
author: Hartley Coleridge
line1: Her very frowns are fairer far
line2: Than smiles of other maidens are.

poemTitle: The Charge of the Light Brigade
author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: I
line2: Half a league, half a league,

poemTitle: The Charge of the Light Brigade
author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Half a league onward,
line2: All in the valley of Death

poemTitle: The Charge of the Light Brigade
author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Rode the six hundred.
line2: “Forward, the Light Brigade!

poemTitle: The Charge of the Light Brigade
author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Charge for the guns!” he said.
line2: Into the valley of Death

poemTitle: The Charge of the Light Brigade
author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Rode the six hundred.
line2: II

poemTitle: The Charge of the Light Brigade
author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: “Forward, the Light Brigade!”
line2: Was there a man dismayed?

poemTitle: The Charge of the Light Brigade
author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Not though the soldier knew
line2: Someone had blundered.

poemTitle: The Charge of the Light Brigade
author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Theirs not to make reply,
line2: Theirs not to reason why,

poemTitle: The Charge of the Light Brigade
author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Theirs but to do and die.
line2: Into the valley of Death

poemTitle: The Charge of the Light Brigade
author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Rode the six hundred.
line2: III

poemTitle: The Charge of the Light Brigade
author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Cannon to right of them,
line2: Cannon to left of them,

poemTitle: The Charge of the Light Brigade
author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Cannon in front of them
line2: Volleyed and thundered;

poemTitle: The Charge of the Light Brigade
author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Stormed at with shot and shell,
line2: Boldly they rode and well,

poemTitle: The Charge of the Light Brigade
author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Into the jaws of Death,
line2: Into the mouth of hell

poemTitle: The Charge of the Light Brigade
author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Rode the six hundred.
line2: IV

poemTitle: The Charge of the Light Brigade
author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Flashed all their sabres bare,
line2: Flashed as they turned in air

poemTitle: The Charge of the Light Brigade
author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Sabring the gunners there,
line2: Charging an army, while

poemTitle: The Charge of the Light Brigade
author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: All the world wondered.
line2: Plunged in the battery-smoke

poemTitle: The Charge of the Light Brigade
author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Right through the line they broke;
line2: Cossack and Russian

poemTitle: The Charge of the Light Brigade
author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Reeled from the sabre stroke
line2: Shattered and sundered.

poemTitle: The Charge of the Light Brigade
author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Then they rode back, but not
line2: Not the six hundred.

poemTitle: The Charge of the Light Brigade
author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: V
line2: Cannon to right of them,

poemTitle: The Charge of the Light Brigade
author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Cannon to left of them,
line2: Cannon behind them

poemTitle: The Charge of the Light Brigade
author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Volleyed and thundered;
line2: Stormed at with shot and shell,

poemTitle: The Charge of the Light Brigade
author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: While horse and hero fell.
line2: They that had fought so well

poemTitle: The Charge of the Light Brigade
author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Came through the jaws of Death,
line2: Back from the mouth of hell,

poemTitle: The Charge of the Light Brigade
author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: All that was left of them,
line2: Left of six hundred.

poemTitle: The Charge of the Light Brigade
author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: VI
line2: When can their glory fade?

poemTitle: The Charge of the Light Brigade
author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: O the wild charge they made!
line2: All the world wondered.

poemTitle: The Charge of the Light Brigade
author: Lord Alfred Tennyson
line1: Honour the charge they made!
line2: Honour the Light Brigade,

author: Joseph Furtado
line1: One autumn morn
line2: I chanced to cross

author: Joseph Furtado
line1: A field of corn
line2: And there it was—

author: Joseph Furtado
line1: That blessed morn—.
line2: To sinful me

author: Joseph Furtado
line1: The grace was given
line2: On earth to see

author: Joseph Furtado
line1: The ways of Heaven:
line2: I saw a bird

author: Joseph Furtado
line1: And heard a voice
line2: I ne’er before

author: Joseph Furtado
line1: Had seen or heard.
line2: I did rejoice,

author: Joseph Furtado
line1: Yet with my joy
line2: Was Holy Awe

author: Joseph Furtado
line1: At what I saw;
line2: And what I said

author: Joseph Furtado
line1: I know not well,
line2: For, strange to tell,

author: Joseph Furtado
line1: I instantly
line2: Was on my knee.

author: Joseph Furtado
line1: The bird saw too
line2: Yet did not stir;

author: Joseph Furtado
line1: And, ‘Who are you
line2: To ask me, sir?.

author: Joseph Furtado
line1: It said, and I
line2: Did thus reply.

author: Joseph Furtado
line1: Grown bolder now,
line2: I know not how:

author: Joseph Furtado
line1: ‘An untaught poet
line2: Of trees and birds

author: Joseph Furtado
line1: Whom no man knoweth
line2: And, wanting words,

author: Joseph Furtado
line1: But dreams and sings
line2: Of simple things’—

author: Joseph Furtado
line1: ‘Peace ! all Heaven knoweth;
line2: From Heaven come I:

author: Joseph Furtado
line1: Come, simple poet’,
line2: The bird rejoined.

author: Joseph Furtado
line1: So, I drew near
line2: And heard it say—

author: Joseph Furtado
line1: That blessed day—
line2: To an ear of corn,

author: Joseph Furtado
line1: A tiny ear
line2: ‘Grow quick, my dear!

author: Joseph Furtado
line1: There’s dearth and death
line2: on every hand,

author: Joseph Furtado
line1: In every breath
line2: Upon this land—

author: Joseph Furtado
line1: Grow quick, my dear!’
line2: Then was revealed

author: Joseph Furtado
line1: At every ear,
line2: Throughout the field,

author: Joseph Furtado
line1: A bird, and clear
line2: A voice, ‘Spare, spare !’

author: Joseph Furtado
line1: Was it my prayer
line2: That blessed morn

author: Joseph Furtado
line1: While I did cross
line2: The field of corn ?

author: Joseph Furtado
line1: Perchance it was:
line2: To sinful me

author: Joseph Furtado
line1: Such grace was given—
line2: On earth to see

author: Patrick Fernando
line1: Dawn fires the surface into gold,
line2: Gold-eyed the herons stilt and stalk.

author: Patrick Fernando
line1: At silver noon the waters hold
line2: Wheelings of a mirrored hawk.

author: Patrick Fernando
line1: I’ve not seen water lie so still
line2: As here. Perhaps an otter may

author: Patrick Fernando
line1: Disturb its peace, or white-cranes till
line2: The green edge, wading tall-knee-deep.

author: Patrick Fernando
line1: In gusts of wind, a faint wood hum—
line2: Plucked leaves and broken petals dance,

author: Patrick Fernando
line1: The wind departs, the wood is dumb,
line2: And floating yellows gather brown.

author: Patrick Fernando
line1: To think up to a mile ago
line2: This river bounded like a hound,

author: Patrick Fernando
line1: Convulsed and nearly wrecked our boat,
line2: And lies here gentle as a pond !

author: Patrick Fernando
line1: A rich practical man I’m told
line2: Demanded, why this idleness?

author: Patrick Fernando
line1: He got no answer and compelled
line2: The river into harness.

author: Patrick Fernando
line1: Like frightened birds the minutes fled
line2: Pursued by roaring steel and fire.

author: Patrick Fernando
line1: The river slaved and profits grew
line2: To almost overtake desire.

author: Patrick Fernando
line1: Until, they say, one windy night,
line2: In deepest vigils of the owl,

author: Patrick Fernando
line1: The river rose and foaming white
line2: Descended like a murderer.

author: Patrick Fernando
line1: At dawn the waters shone restored
line2: The wreckage stood like blasted rocks

author: Patrick Fernando
line1: Round which the burnished mirror showed
line2: Artistry of a wild brown hawk.

author: William Wordsworth
line1: O Friend! I know not which way I must look
line2: For comfort, being, as I am, opprest,

author: William Wordsworth
line1: To think that now our life is only drest
line2: For show; mean handy-work of craftsman, cook,

author: William Wordsworth
line1: Or groom! — We must run glittering like a brook
line2: In the open sunshine, or we are unblest:

author: William Wordsworth
line1: The wealthiest man among us is the best:
line2: No grandeur now in nature or in book

author: William Wordsworth
line1: Delights us. Rapine, avarice, expense,
line2: This is idolatry; and these we adore:

author: William Wordsworth
line1: Plain living and high thinking are no more:
line2: The homely beauty of the good old cause

author: William Wordsworth
line1: Is gone; our peace, our fearful innocence,
line2: And pure religion breathing household laws.

author: John Keats
line1: When I have fears that I may cease to be
line2: Before my pen has glean’d my teeming brain,

author: John Keats
line1: Before high-piled books, in charact’ry,
line2: Hold like rich garners the full-ripen’d grain;

author: John Keats
line1: When I behold, upon the night’s starr’d face,
line2: Huge cloudy symbols of a high romance,

author: John Keats
line1: And think that I may never live to trace
line2: Their shadows with the magic hand of chance;

author: John Keats
line1: And when I feel, fair creature of an hour!
line2: That I shall never look upon thee more,

author: John Keats
line1: Never have relish in the faery power
line2: Of unreflecting love—then on the shore

author: John Keats
line1: Of the wide world I stand alone, and think
line2: Till love and fame to nothingness do sink.

author: Wilfred Owen
line1: Move him into the sun—
line2: Gently its touch awoke him once,

author: Wilfred Owen
line1: At home, whispering of fields half-sown.
line2: Always it woke him, even in France,

author: Wilfred Owen
line1: Until this morning and this snow.
line2: If anything might rouse him now

author: Wilfred Owen
line1: The kind old sun will know.
line2: Think how it wakes the seeds—

author: Wilfred Owen
line1: Woke once the clays of a cold star.
line2: Are limbs, so dear-achieved, are sides

author: Wilfred Owen
line1: Full-nerved, still warm, too hard to stir?
line2: Was it for this the clay grew tall?

author: Wilfred Owen
line1: —O what made fatuous sunbeams toil
line2: To break earth’s sleep at all?

poemTitle: A Fisherman Mourned by His Wife
author: Patrick Fernando
line1: When you were not quite thirty and the sun
line2: Had not yet tanned you into old-boat brown,

poemTitle: A Fisherman Mourned by His Wife
author: Patrick Fernando
line1: When you were not quite thirty and had not begun
line2: To be embittered like the rest, nor grown

poemTitle: A Fisherman Mourned by His Wife
author: Patrick Fernando
line1: obsessed with death, then would you come
line2: Hot with continence upon the sea

poemTitle: A Fisherman Mourned by His Wife
author: Patrick Fernando
line1: Chaste as a gull flying pointed home,
line2: In haste to be with me!

poemTitle: A Fisherman Mourned by His Wife
author: Patrick Fernando
line1: Now that, being dead, you are beyond detection.
line2: And I need not be discreet, let us confess.

poemTitle: A Fisherman Mourned by His Wife
author: Patrick Fernando
line1: It was not love that married us no affection,
line2: But elders’ persuasion, not even loneliness,

poemTitle: A Fisherman Mourned by His Wife
author: Patrick Fernando
line1: Recall how first you were so impatient and afraid,
line2: My eyes were open in the dark unlike in love,

poemTitle: A Fisherman Mourned by His Wife
author: Patrick Fernando
line1: Trembling, lest in fear, you’ll let me go a maid,
line2: Trembling on the other hand for my virginity.

poemTitle: A Fisherman Mourned by His Wife
author: Patrick Fernando
line1: Three months the monsoon thrashed the sea, and you
line2: Remained at home; the sky cracked like a shell

poemTitle: A Fisherman Mourned by His Wife
author: Patrick Fernando
line1: In thunder, the rain broke through,
line2: At last when the pouring ceased the storm winds fell,

poemTitle: A Fisherman Mourned by His Wife
author: Patrick Fernando
line1: When gulls returned new plumed and wild,
line2: When in our wind-torn flamboyante

poemTitle: A Fisherman Mourned by His Wife
author: Patrick Fernando
line1: New buds broke. I was with child.
line2: My face was wan while telling you and voice fell low

poemTitle: A Fisherman Mourned by His Wife
author: Patrick Fernando
line1: And you seemed full of guilt and not to know
line2: Whether to repent or rejoice over the situation

poemTitle: A Fisherman Mourned by His Wife
author: Patrick Fernando
line1: You nodded at the ground and went to sea.
line2: But soon I was to you more than God or temptation.

poemTitle: A Fisherman Mourned by His Wife
author: Patrick Fernando
line1: And so were you to me.
line2: Men come and go, some say they understand.

poemTitle: A Fisherman Mourned by His Wife
author: Patrick Fernando
line1: Our children weep, the youngest thinks you’re fast asleep:
line2: There is fear and wonderment.

poemTitle: A Fisherman Mourned by His Wife
author: Patrick Fernando
line1: You had grown so familiar as my hand,
line2: That I cannot with simple grief

poemTitle: A Fisherman Mourned by His Wife
author: Patrick Fernando
line1: Assuage dismemberment.
line2: Outside the wind despoils of leaf

poemTitle: A Fisherman Mourned by His Wife
author: Patrick Fernando
line1: Tree that it used to nurse;
line2: Once more the flamboyante is torn,

poemTitle: A Fisherman Mourned by His Wife
author: Patrick Fernando
line1: The sky cracks like a shell again.
line2: So someone practical has gone

poemTitle: A Fisherman Mourned by His Wife
author: Patrick Fernando
line1: To make them bring the hearse
line2: Before the rain.

author: Toru Dutt
line1: Three happy children in a darkened room!
line2: What do they gaze on with wide-open eyes?

author: Toru Dutt
line1: A dense, dense forest, where no sunbeam pries,
line2: And in its centre a cleared spot.—There bloom

author: Toru Dutt
line1: Gigantic flowers on creepers that embrace
line2: Tall trees; there, in a quiet lucid lake

author: Toru Dutt
line1: The while swans glide; there, “whirring from the brake,
line2: The peacock springs; there, herds of wild deer race;

author: Toru Dutt
line1: There, patches gleam with yellow waving grain;
line2: There, blue smoke from strange altars rises light,

author: Toru Dutt
line1: There, dwells in peace, the poet-anchorite.
line2: But who is this fair lady? Not in vain

author: Toru Dutt
line1: She weeps.—for lo! at every tear she sheds
line2: Tears from three pairs of young eyes fall amain,

author: Toru Dutt
line1: And bowed in sorrow are the three young heads.
line2: It is an old, old story, and the lay

author: Toru Dutt
line1: Which has evoked sad Sîta from the past
line2: Is by a mother sung… ‘Tis hushed at last

author: Toru Dutt
line1: And melts the picture from their sight away,
line2: Yet shall they dream of it until the day!

author: Toru Dutt
line1: When shall those children by their mother’s side
line2: Gather, ah me! as erst at eventide?

author: Kamala Das
line1: There is a house now far away where once
line2: I received love… That woman died,

author: Kamala Das
line1: The house withdrew into silence, snakes moved
line2: Among books, I was then too young

author: Kamala Das
line1: To read, and, my blood turned cold like the moon.
line2: How often I think of going

author: Kamala Das
line1: There, to peer through blind eyes of windows or
line2: Just listen to the frozen air,

author: Kamala Das
line1: Or in wild despair, pick an armful of
line2: Darkness to bring it here to lie

author: Kamala Das
line1: Behind my bedroom door like a brooding
line2: Dog… you cannot believe, darling,

author: Kamala Das
line1: Can you, that I lived in such a house and
line2: Was proud, and loved…. I who have lost

author: Kamala Das
line1: My way and beg now at strangers’ doors to
line2: Receive love, at least in small change?

author: Armando Menezes
line1: Earth lies a violin to my bow:
line2: And as I rush,

author: Armando Menezes
line1: A thousand shapes of music grow
line2: Out of the hush.

author: Armando Menezes
line1: The leaping flame within me draws,
line2: As it shoots lone,

author: Armando Menezes
line1: Between each throbbing pause and pause.
line2: Tone upon tone.

author: Armando Menezes
line1: Strange orchestrated sounds unroll
line2: From waiting woods;

author: Armando Menezes
line1: And as my passion thrills the soul
line2: Of solitudes,

author: Armando Menezes
line1: I hear a far-off rapture sweep
line2: Me as I pass;

author: Armando Menezes
line1: Loud waters dying in the deep,
line2: Low sighs of grass;

author: Armando Menezes
line1: Long echoes rolling to the ridge,
line2: Or valley green;

author: Armando Menezes
line1: The different notes of tunnel, bridge,
line2: Or cleft ravine;

author: Armando Menezes
line1: The fury of neglected stations—
line2: A shrieking wind

author: Armando Menezes
line1: Shrill with a million execrations
line2: Of hag or fiend;

author: Armando Menezes
line1: The murmurous silence when I stop,
line2: Live with the noise

author: Armando Menezes
line1: Of water drowsing drop by drop
line2: Or human voice.

author: Armando Menezes
line1: A little pause, and off I go…
line2: My simple art

author: Armando Menezes
line1: Touches to music with my bow
line2: Earth’s silent heart.

author: Shoshee Chunder Dutt
line1: When Maharajah Jeswant Singh, being defeated by Aurungzeb, fled
line2: for refuge to his own capital, his wife, with Spartan haughtiness,

author: Shoshee Chunder Dutt
line1: refused him admittance, saying “This man is an impostor, for the
line2: brave never return with dishonour. My husband sleeps on the field of

author: Shoshee Chunder Dutt
line1: battle.”
line2: Heard ye that lofty pealing sound

author: Shoshee Chunder Dutt
line1: Upon the balmy air,
line2: The exulting shout that best proclaims

author: Shoshee Chunder Dutt
line1: The deeds which heroes dare?
line2: In triumph blow their trumpets proud,

author: Shoshee Chunder Dutt
line1: The clouds repeat their voice;
line2: Go, greet the laurell’d victors home,

author: Shoshee Chunder Dutt
line1: And bid our realms rejoice.
line2: Let poets tune their golden harps,

author: Shoshee Chunder Dutt
line1: Let maidens wear their smile,
line2: And young and old their cares lay by,

author: Shoshee Chunder Dutt
line1: And cease to mourn awhile.
line2: What! hear’st thou not their joyous din?

author: Shoshee Chunder Dutt
line1: Behold, above the vale,
line2: Their haughty plumes and ensigns red

author: Shoshee Chunder Dutt
line1: Are fluttering in the gale;
line2: And helmets cleft, and canvas torn,

author: Shoshee Chunder Dutt
line1: Proclaim the fighting done;
line2: And neighing steeds, and bloody spears,

author: Shoshee Chunder Dutt
line1: Announce the battle won.
line2: Alas! the vision mocks my sight;

author: Shoshee Chunder Dutt
line1: I see no gallant throng,
line2: No trophies meet my longing eyes;

author: Shoshee Chunder Dutt
line1: Bid cease the joyous song.
line2: That recreant slave is not my lord;

author: Shoshee Chunder Dutt
line1: Ne’er thus the brave return;
line2: Go, bid the city-gates be barr’d,

author: Shoshee Chunder Dutt
line1: And leave me lone to mourn.
line2: I know him not, I never knew

author: Shoshee Chunder Dutt
line1: A low, ignoble love;
line2: My warrior sleeps upon the moor,

author: Shoshee Chunder Dutt
line1: His soul hath soar’d above.
line2: Upon the battle-field he lies,

author: Shoshee Chunder Dutt
line1: His garments stain’d with gore;
line2: With sword in hand prepared he sleeps

author: Shoshee Chunder Dutt
line1: To fight the battle o’er.
line2: His shiver’d shield, his broken spear,

author: Shoshee Chunder Dutt
line1: Around him scatter’d lie;
line2: The iron-breasted Moslems shook

author: Shoshee Chunder Dutt
line1: To see my hero die.
line2: Where helmets rang, where sabres smote,

author: Shoshee Chunder Dutt
line1: He found his gory bed;
line2: Join, mourners, join, and loudly raise

author: Shoshee Chunder Dutt
line1: The requiem of the dead.
line2: Expel yon vile impostor hence;

author: Shoshee Chunder Dutt
line1: I will not trust his tale;
line2: Our warriors on the crimson field

author: Shoshee Chunder Dutt
line1: Their chieftain’s loss bewail.
line2: The mountain-torrent rushing down

author: Shoshee Chunder Dutt
line1: Can ne’er its course retrace,
line2: And souls that speed on glory’s path

author: Shoshee Chunder Dutt
line1: Must ever onward press:
line2: Aye, onward press—to bleed and die,

author: Shoshee Chunder Dutt
line1: Triumphant still in death;
line2: Impostor, hence! in other lands

author: Hur Chunder Dutt
line1: The monarch lay upon his bier,
line2: Censers were burning low

author: Hur Chunder Dutt
line1: As through the lofty arches streamed
line2: the setting sun’s red glow,

author: Hur Chunder Dutt
line1: Still grasped he in his hand the blade
line2: which well fought fields had won

author: Hur Chunder Dutt
line1: And Aurangzeb beside him knelt;
line2: Usurper, proud and son.

author: Hur Chunder Dutt
line1: Remorse had stricken his false heart
line2: and quenched his wonted fire

author: Hur Chunder Dutt
line1: With gloomy brow and look intent
line2: he gazed upon his sire,

author: Hur Chunder Dutt
line1: Can tyrant death make him afraid?
line2: hot tears burst from his eyes

author: Hur Chunder Dutt
line1: As thus his grief found vent in words
line2: to the warrior trains surprise

author: Hur Chunder Dutt
line1: “Father thou wert the goodliest king
line2: that e’er the sceptre swayed,

author: Hur Chunder Dutt
line1: How could I then lift up my hand
line2: against thee undismayed?

author: Hur Chunder Dutt
line1: How could I send thee here to pine,
line2: usurp the peacock throne

author: Hur Chunder Dutt
line1: O had I perished in the womb
line2: that deed were left undone.

author: Hur Chunder Dutt
line1: Look all is changed that was estranged
line2: awake my sire, my king,

author: Hur Chunder Dutt
line1: Look soldiers in their war array
line2: thy son in fetters bring,

author: Hur Chunder Dutt
line1: Thy rebel son who will abide
line2: thy word whate’er it be

author: Hur Chunder Dutt
line1: And fearless meet the rack or steel;
line2: rise up once more and see.

author: Hur Chunder Dutt
line1: Thou will not hear—thou will not speak;
line2: it is the last long sleep.—

author: Hur Chunder Dutt
line1: And am I not a king myself
line2: what mean these stirrings deep,

author: Hur Chunder Dutt
line1: O foolish eyes what means this rheum,
line2: I will not call them tears

author: Hur Chunder Dutt
line1: My heart which nothing e’er could daunt
line2: is faint with boding fears.

author: Hur Chunder Dutt
line1: The past appears! a checker’d field
line2: Of guilt and shame and war,

author: Hur Chunder Dutt
line1: What evil influence ruled my birth,
line2: What swart malignant star?

author: Hur Chunder Dutt
line1: Why did I barter peace of mind
line2: For royal pomp and state?

author: Hur Chunder Dutt
line1: Mad for the baleful meteor’s gleam
line2: With worldly joys elate

author: Hur Chunder Dutt
line1: Remembered voices speak my name
line2: and call me parricide

author: Hur Chunder Dutt
line1: The murdered Dara beckons me—
line2: he was thy joy and pride.

author: Hur Chunder Dutt
line1: And thus I fling the dear bought crown
line2: but whither can I fly?

author: Hur Chunder Dutt
line1: The awful thought still follows me
line2: that even kings will die.”

author: Gieve Patel
line1: It takes much time to kill a tree,
line2: Not a simple jab of the knife

author: Gieve Patel
line1: Will do it. It has grown
line2: Slowly consuming the earth,

author: Gieve Patel
line1: Rising out of it, feeding
line2: Upon its crust, absorbing

author: Gieve Patel
line1: Years of sunlight, air, water,
line2: And out of its leperous hide

author: Gieve Patel
line1: Sprouting leaves.
line2: So hack and chop

author: Gieve Patel
line1: But this alone wont do it.
line2: Not so much pain will do it.

author: Gieve Patel
line1: The bleeding bark will heal
line2: And from close to the ground

author: Gieve Patel
line1: Will rise curled green twigs,
line2: Miniature boughs

author: Gieve Patel
line1: Which if unchecked will expand again
line2: To former size.

author: Gieve Patel
line1: No,
line2: The root is to be pulled out —

author: Gieve Patel
line1: Out of the anchoring earth;
line2: It is to be roped, tied,

author: Gieve Patel
line1: And pulled out — snapped out
line2: Or pulled out entirely.

author: Gieve Patel
line1: Out from the earth-cave,
line2: And the strength of the tree exposed,

author: Gieve Patel
line1: The source, white and wet,
line2: The most sensitive, hidden

author: Gieve Patel
line1: For years inside the earth.
line2: Then the matter

author: Gieve Patel
line1: Of scorching and choking
line2: In sun and air,

author: Gieve Patel
line1: Browning, hardening,
line2: Twisting, withering,

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: I
line2: Loudly the midnight tempest sang,

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: Ah! it was thy dirge, fair Liberty!
line2: And clouds in thundering accents roar’d

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: Unheeded warning from on high;
line2: The rain in darksome torrents fell,

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: Hydaspes’ waves did onward sweep,
line2: Like fiery Passion’s headlong flow,

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: To meet th’ awaken’d calling deep;
line2: The lighting flashed bright— dazzling,like

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: Fair women’s glance from ‘neath her veil;’
line2: And on the heaving, troubled air,

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: There was a moaning sound of wail
line2: But, Ind! thy unsuspecting sons

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: Did heedless slumber,— while the foe
line2: Came in stealthy step of death,—

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: Came as the tiger, noiseless, slow,
line2: To close at once its victim’s breath!

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: Alas! they knew not ‘midst this gloom’
line2: This war of elements was burst,—

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: Like to an earthquake in the womb
line2: Of a volcano,— deep and low—

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: A deadlier storm—on them to burst!
line2: II

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: ‘Twas morn; the Lord of Day
line2: From gold Sumero’s palace bright,

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: Look’d his own sweet dime,
line2: But lo! the glorious flag,

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: To which the world in awe once bow’d,
line2: There in defiance waved

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: On India’s gales— triumphant—proud!—
line2: Then, rose the dreadful yell,—

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: Then lion-like, each warrior brave
line2: Rushed on the coming foe,

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: To strike for freedom—or the grave!
line2: Oh Death! upon thy gory altar

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: What blood-libations freely flow’d!
line2: Oh Earth! on that bright morn, what thousands

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: Rendered to thee the dust they ow’d!
line2: But ‘fore the Macedonians driven’

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: Fell India’s hardy sons,—
line2: Proud mountain oaks by thunders riven,—

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: That for their country’s freedom bled—
line2: And made on gore their glorious bed!

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: III
line2: But dauntlessly there stood

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: King Porus, towering ‘midst the foe’
line2: Like a Himala-peak

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: With its eternal crown of snow:
line2: And on his brow did shine

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: The jewell’d regal diadem.
line2: His milk-white elephant

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: Was deck’d with many a brilliant gem.
line2: He reck’d not of the phalanx

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: That ‘round him closed—but nobly fought’
line2: And like the angry winds that blow

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: And lofty mountain pines lay low,
line2: Amidst them dreadful havoc wrought,

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: And thinn’d his crown and country’s foe!
line2: The hardiest warriors, at his deeds,

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: Awe—struck quail’d like wind-shaken reeds:
line2: They dared not look upon his face,

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: They shrank before his burning gaze,
line2: For in his eye the hero shone

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: That feared not death;—but high—alone
line2: A being as if of lightning made,

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: That scorch’d all that is gazed upon—
line2: Trampling the living with the dead.

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: IV
line2: Th’ immortal Thund’rer’s son,

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: Astonish’d eyed the heroic king;
line2: He saw him bravely charge

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: Like his dread father,— fulmining:—
line2: Tho’ thousands’ round him closed,

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: He stood—as stand the ocean rock
line2: Amdist the lashing billows

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: Unmoved at their fierce thoundering shock.
line2: But when th’ Emathian conqueror

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: Saw that with gaping wounds he bled,
line2: ‘Desist—desist!’—he cried—

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: ‘Such noble blood should not be shed!’
line2: Then a herald was sent

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: Where bleeding and faint,
line2: Stood, ‘midst the dying’ and the dead,

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: King Porus,— boldly, undismayed:
line2: ‘Hail, brave and warlike prince!’

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: Thy generous rival bids thee cease—
line2: Behold! there flies the flag,

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: That lulls dread war, and wakens peace!’
line2: V

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: Like to a lion chain’d,
line2: That tho’ faint—bleeding—stands in pride—

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: With eyes, where unsubdued
line2: Yet flash’d the fire—looks that defied;

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: King Porus boldly went
line2: Where ‘midst the gay and flittering crowd’

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: Sat god-like Alexander;
line2: While ‘round’ Earth’s mightiest monarchs bow’d.

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: King Porus was no slave;
line2: he stooped not—bent not there his knee,—

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: But stood, as stands an oak,
line2: In Himalayan majesty.

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: ‘The mighty king of Macedon:’
line2: ‘Ev’n as a King,’ replied

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: In royal pride, Ind’s haughty son.
line2: The conqu’ror pleas’d,

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: Him forth releas’d:
line2: Thus India’s crown was lost and won.

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: VI
line2: But where, Oh! where is Porus now?

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: And where the noble hearts that bled
line2: For freedom—with the herioc glow

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: In patriot bosoms nourished—
line2: —Hearts, eagle-like that recked not death,

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: But shrank before foul Thraldom’s breath?
line2: And where art thou—fair Freedom!—thou

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: Once goodness of Ind’s sunny clime
line2: When glory’s halo round her brow

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: Shone radiant, and she rose sublime,
line2: Like her own towering Himalye

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: To kiss blue clouds thron’d on high!
line2: Clime of the sun!—How like a Dream—

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: How like bright sun-beams on a stream
line2: That melt beneath gray twilight’s eye—

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: That glory hath now flitted by!
line2: The crown that once did deck thy brow

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: Is tramped down—and thou sunk low;
line2: Thy pearl, thy diamond and thy mine

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: Of glistening gold no more is thine.
line2: Alas!—each conquering tyrant’s lust

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: Has robb’d thee of thy very dust!
line2: Thou standest like a lofty tree

author: Michael Madhusudan Dutt
line1: Shorn of fruits — blossoms — leaves and all—
line2: Of every gale the sport to be.

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: John Gilpin was a citizen
line2: Of credit and renown,

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: A train-band captain eke was he,
line2: Of famous London town.

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: John Gilpin’s spouse said to her dear,
line2: “Though wedded we have been

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: These twice ten tedious years, yet we
line2: No holiday have seen.

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: “To-morrow is our wedding-day,
line2: And we will then repair

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: Unto the ‘Bell’ at Edmonton,
line2: All in a chaise and pair.

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: “My sister, and my sister’s child,
line2: Myself, and children three,

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: Will fill the chaise; so you must ride
line2: On horseback after we.”

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: He soon replied, “I do admire
line2: Of womankind but one,

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: And you are she, my dearest dear,
line2: Therefore it shall be done.

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: “I am a linen-draper bold,
line2: As all the world doth know,

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: And my good friend the calender
line2: Will lend his horse to go.”

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: Quoth Mrs. Gilpin, “That’s well said;
line2: And for that wine is dear,

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: We will be furnished with our own,
line2: Which is both bright and clear.”

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: John Gilpin kissed his loving wife.
line2: O’erjoyed was he to find.

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: That though on pleasure she was bent,
line2: She had a frugal mind.

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: The morning came, the chaise was brought,
line2: But yet was not allowed

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: To drive up to the door, lest all
line2: Should say that she was proud.

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: So three doors off the chaise was stayed,
line2: Where they did all get in;

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: Six precious souls, and all agog
line2: To dash through thick and thin.

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: Smack went the whip, round went the wheels,
line2: Were never folks so glad!

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: The stones did rattle underneath,
line2: As if Cheapside were mad.

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: John Gilpin at his horse’s side
line2: Seized fast the flowing mane,

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: And up he got, in haste to ride,
line2: But soon came down again;

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: For saddletree scarce reached had he,
line2: His journey to begin,

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: When, turning round his head, he saw
line2: Three customers come in.

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: So down he came; for loss of time,
line2: Although it grieved him sore,

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: Yet loss of pence, full well he knew,
line2: Would trouble him much more.

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: ‘Twas long before the customers
line2: Were suited to their mind,

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: When Betty screaming came downstairs,
line2: “The wine is left behind!”

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: “Good lack!” quoth he, “yet bring it me,
line2: My leathern belt likewise,

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: In which I bear my trusty sword
line2: When I do exercise.”

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: Now Mistress Gilpin (careful soul!)
line2: Had two stone bottles found,

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: To hold the liquor that she loved,
line2: And keep it safe and sound.

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: Each bottle had a curling ear,
line2: Through which the belt he drew,

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: And hung a bottle on each side,
line2: To make his balance true.

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: Then over all, that he might be
line2: Equipped from top to toe,

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: His long red cloak, well brushed and neat,
line2: He manfully did throw.

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: Now see him mounted once again
line2: Upon his nimble steed,

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: Full slowly pacing o’er the stones,
line2: With caution and good heed.

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: But finding soon a smoother road
line2: Beneath his well-shod feet,

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: The snorting beast began to trot,
line2: Which galled him in his seat.

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: “So, fair and softly!” John he cried,
line2: But John he cried in vain;

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: That trot became a gallop soon,
line2: In spite of curb and rein.

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: So stooping down, as needs he must
line2: Who cannot sit upright,

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: He grasped the mane with both his hands,
line2: And eke with all his might.

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: His horse, who never in that sort
line2: Had handled been before,

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: What thing upon his back had got,
line2: Did wonder more and more.

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: Away went Gilpin, neck or nought,
line2: Away went hat and wig;

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: He little dreamt, when he set out,
line2: Of running such a rig.

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: The wind did blow, the cloak did fly
line2: Like streamer long and gay,

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: Till, loop and button failing both.
line2: At last it flew away.

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: Then might all people well discern
line2: The bottles he had slung;

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: A bottle swinging at each side,
line2: As hath been said or sung.

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: The dogs did bark, the children screamed,
line2: Up flew the windows all;

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: And every soul cried out, “Well done!”
line2: As loud as he could bawl.

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: Away went Gilpin—who but he?
line2: His fame soon spread around;

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: “He carries weight! he rides a race!
line2: ‘Tis for a thousand pound!”

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: And still as fast as he drew near,
line2: ‘Twas wonderful to view

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: How in a trice the turnpike-men
line2: Their gates wide open threw.

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: And now, as he went bowing down
line2: His reeking head full low,

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: The bottles twain behind his back
line2: Were shattered at a blow.

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: Down ran the wine into the road,
line2: Most piteous to be seen,

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: Which made the horse’s flanks to smoke,
line2: As they had basted been.

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: But still he seemed to carry weight.
line2: With leathern girdle braced;

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: For all might see the bottle-necks
line2: Still dangling at his waist.

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: Thus all through merry Islington
line2: These gambols he did play,

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: Until he came unto the Wash
line2: Of Edmonton so gay;

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: And there he threw the wash about
line2: On both sides of the way,

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: Just like unto a trundling mop,
line2: Or a wild goose at play.

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: At Edmonton his loving wife
line2: From the balcony spied

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: Her tender husband, wondering much
line2: To see how he did ride.

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: “Stop, stop, John Gilpin!—Here’s the house!”
line2: They all at once did cry;

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: “The dinner waits, and we are tired;”
line2: Said Gilpin—”So am I!”

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: But yet his horse was not a whit
line2: Inclined to tarry there;

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: For why?—his owner had a house
line2: Full ten miles off, at Ware.

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: So like an arrow swift he flew,
line2: Shot by an archer strong;

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: So did he fly—which brings me to
line2: The middle of my song.

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: Away went Gilpin, out of breath,
line2: And sore against his will,

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: Till at his friend the calender’s
