line2: His horse at last stood still.

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: The calender, amazed to see
line2: His neighbour in such trim,

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: Laid down his pipe, flew to the gate.
line2: And thus accosted him:

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: “What news? what news? your tidings tell;
line2: Tell me you must and shall—

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: Say why bareheaded you are come,
line2: Or why you come at all?”

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: Now Gilpin had a pleasant wit,
line2: And loved a timely joke;

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: And thus unto the calender
line2: In merry guise he spoke:

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: “I came because your horse would come;
line2: And, if I well forebode,

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: My hat and wig will soon be here,
line2: They are upon the road.”

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: The calender, right glad to find
line2: His friend in merry pin,

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: Returned him not a single word,
line2: But to the house went in;

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: Whence straight he came with hat and wig,
line2: A wig that flowed behind,

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: A hat not much the worse for wear,
line2: Each comely in its kind.

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: He held them up, and in his turn
line2: Thus showed his ready wit:

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: “My head is twice as big as yours,
line2: They therefore needs must fit.”

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: “But let me scrape the dirt away,
line2: That hangs upon your face;

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: And stop and eat, for well you may
line2: Be in a hungry case.”

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: Said John, “It is my wedding-day,
line2: And all the world would stare

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: If wife should dine at Edmonton,
line2: And I should dine at Ware.”

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: So turning to his horse, he said
line2: “I am in haste to dine;

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: ‘Twas for your pleasure you came here,
line2: You shall go back for mine.”

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: Ah! luckless speech, and bootless boast!
line2: For which he paid full dear;

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: For while he spake, a braying ass
line2: Did sing most loud and clear;

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: Whereat his horse did snort, as he
line2: Had heard a lion roar,

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: And galloped off with all his might,
line2: As he had done before.

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: Away went Gilpin, and away
line2: Went Gilpin’s hat and wig;

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: He lost them sooner than at first,
line2: For why?—they were too big.

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: Now Mistress Gilpin, when she saw
line2: Her husband posting down

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: Into the country far away,
line2: She pulled out half-a-crown;

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: And thus unto the youth she said
line2: That drove them to the “Bell,”

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: “This shall be yours when you bring back
line2: My husband safe and well.”

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: The youth did ride, and soon did meet
line2: John coming back amain;

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: Whom in a trice he tried to stop,
line2: By catching at his rein.

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: But not performing what he meant,
line2: And gladly would have done,

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: The frighted steed he frighted more,
line2: And made him faster run.

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: Away went Gilpin, and away
line2: Went postboy at his heels,

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: The postboy’s horse right glad to miss
line2: The lumbering of the wheels.

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: Six gentlemen upon the road,
line2: Thus seeing Gilpin fly,

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: With postboy scampering in the rear.
line2: They raised the hue and cry.

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: “Stop thief! stop thief! a highwayman!’”
line2: Not one of them was mute;

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: And all and each that passed that way
line2: Did join in the pursuit.

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: And now the turnpike-gates again
line2: Flew open in short space;

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: The toll-man thinking, as before,
line2: That Gilpin rode a race.

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: And so he did, and won it too,
line2: For he got first to town;

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: Nor stopped till where he had got up,
line2: He did again get down.

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: Now let us sing, Long live the King,
line2: And Gilpin, long live he;

poemTitle: The Diverting History of John Gilpin
author: William Cowper
line1: And when he next doth ride abroad.
line2: May I be there to see.

author: William Butler Yeats
line1: Turning and turning in the widening gyre
line2: The falcon cannot hear the falconer;

author: William Butler Yeats
line1: Things fall apart; the centre cannot hold;
line2: Mere anarchy is loosed upon the world,

author: William Butler Yeats
line1: The blood-dimmed tide is loosed, and everywhere
line2: The ceremony of innocence is drowned;

author: William Butler Yeats
line1: The best lack all conviction, while the worst
line2: Are full of passionate intensity.

author: William Butler Yeats
line1: Surely some revelation is at hand;
line2: Surely the Second Coming is at hand.

author: William Butler Yeats
line1: The Second Coming! Hardly are those words out
line2: When a vast image out of Spiritus Mundi

author: William Butler Yeats
line1: Troubles my sight: somewhere in sands of the desert
line2: A shape with lion body and the head of a man,

author: William Butler Yeats
line1: A gaze blank and pitiless as the sun,
line2: Is moving its slow thighs, while all about it

author: William Butler Yeats
line1: Reel shadows of the indignant desert birds.
line2: The darkness drops again; but now I know

author: William Butler Yeats
line1: That twenty centuries of stony sleep
line2: Were vexed to nightmare by a rocking cradle,

author: William Butler Yeats
line1: And what rough beast, its hour come round at last,
line2: Slouches towards Bethlehem to be born?

author: William Blake
line1: To see a World in a Grain of Sand
line2: And a Heaven in a Wild Flower

author: William Blake
line1: Hold Infinity in the palm of your hand
line2: And Eternity in an hour

author: William Blake
line1: A Robin Red breast in a Cage
line2: Puts all Heaven in a Rage

author: William Blake
line1: A Dove house filld with Doves & Pigeons
line2: Shudders Hell thr’ all its regions

author: William Blake
line1: A dog starvd at his Masters Gate
line2: Predicts the ruin of the State

author: William Blake
line1: A Horse misusd upon the Road
line2: Calls to Heaven for Human blood

author: William Blake
line1: Each outcry of the hunted Hare
line2: A fibre from the Brain does tear

author: William Blake
line1: A Skylark wounded in the wing
line2: A Cherubim does cease to sing

author: William Blake
line1: The Game Cock clipd & armd for fight
line2: Does the Rising Sun affright

author: William Blake
line1: Every Wolfs & Lions howl
line2: Raises from Hell a Human Soul

author: William Blake
line1: The wild deer, wandring here & there
line2: Keeps the Human Soul from Care

author: William Blake
line1: The Lamb misusd breeds Public Strife
line2: And yet forgives the Butchers knife

author: William Blake
line1: The Bat that flits at close of Eve
line2: Has left the Brain that wont Believe

author: William Blake
line1: The Owl that calls upon the Night
line2: Speaks the Unbelievers fright

author: William Blake
line1: He who shall hurt the little Wren
line2: Shall never be belovd by Men

author: William Blake
line1: He who the Ox to wrath has movd
line2: Shall never be by Woman lovd

author: William Blake
line1: The wanton Boy that kills the Fly
line2: Shall feel the Spiders enmity

author: William Blake
line1: He who torments the Chafers Sprite
line2: Weaves a Bower in endless Night

author: William Blake
line1: The Catterpiller on the Leaf
line2: Repeats to thee thy Mothers grief

author: William Blake
line1: Kill not the Moth nor Butterfly
line2: For the Last Judgment draweth nigh

author: William Blake
line1: He who shall train the Horse to War
line2: Shall never pass the Polar Bar

author: William Blake
line1: The Beggars Dog & Widows Cat
line2: Feed them & thou wilt grow fat

author: William Blake
line1: The Gnat that sings his Summers Song
line2: Poison gets from Slanders tongue

author: William Blake
line1: The poison of the Snake & Newt
line2: Is the sweat of Envys Foot

author: William Blake
line1: The poison of the Honey Bee
line2: Is the Artists Jealousy

author: William Blake
line1: The Princes Robes & Beggars Rags
line2: Are Toadstools on the Misers Bags

author: William Blake
line1: A Truth thats told with bad intent
line2: Beats all the Lies you can invent

author: William Blake
line1: It is right it should be so
line2: Man was made for Joy & Woe

author: William Blake
line1: And when this we rightly know
line2: Thro the World we safely go

author: William Blake
line1: Joy & Woe are woven fine
line2: A Clothing for the soul divine

author: William Blake
line1: Under every grief & pine
line2: Runs a joy with silken twine

author: William Blake
line1: The Babe is more than swadling Bands
line2: Throughout all these Human Lands

author: William Blake
line1: Tools were made & Born were hands
line2: Every Farmer Understands

author: William Blake
line1: Every Tear from Every Eye
line2: Becomes a Babe in Eternity

author: William Blake
line1: This is caught by Females bright
line2: And returnd to its own delight

author: William Blake
line1: The Bleat the Bark Bellow & Roar
line2: Are Waves that Beat on Heavens Shore

author: William Blake
line1: The Babe that weeps the Rod beneath
line2: Writes Revenge in realms of Death

author: William Blake
line1: The Beggars Rags fluttering in Air
line2: Does to Rags the Heavens tear

author: William Blake
line1: The Soldier armd with Sword & Gun
line2: Palsied strikes the Summers Sun

author: William Blake
line1: The poor Mans Farthing is worth more
line2: Than all the Gold on Africs Shore

author: William Blake
line1: One Mite wrung from the Labrers hands
line2: Shall buy & sell the Misers Lands

author: William Blake
line1: Or if protected from on high
line2: Does that whole Nation sell & buy

author: William Blake
line1: He who mocks the Infants Faith
line2: Shall be mockd in Age & Death

author: William Blake
line1: He who shall teach the Child to Doubt
line2: The rotting Grave shall neer get out

author: William Blake
line1: He who respects the Infants faith
line2: Triumphs over Hell & Death

author: William Blake
line1: The Childs Toys & the Old Mans Reasons
line2: Are the Fruits of the Two seasons

author: William Blake
line1: The Questioner who sits so sly
line2: Shall never know how to Reply

author: William Blake
line1: He who replies to words of Doubt
line2: Doth put the Light of Knowledge out

author: William Blake
line1: The Strongest Poison ever known
line2: Came from Caesars Laurel Crown

author: William Blake
line1: Nought can Deform the Human Race
line2: Like to the Armours iron brace

author: William Blake
line1: When Gold & Gems adorn the Plow
line2: To peaceful Arts shall Envy Bow

author: William Blake
line1: A Riddle or the Crickets Cry
line2: Is to Doubt a fit Reply

author: William Blake
line1: The Emmets Inch & Eagles Mile
line2: Make Lame Philosophy to smile

author: William Blake
line1: He who Doubts from what he sees
line2: Will neer Believe do what you Please

author: William Blake
line1: If the Sun & Moon should Doubt
line2: Theyd immediately Go out

author: William Blake
line1: To be in a Passion you Good may Do
line2: But no Good if a Passion is in you

line1: The Whore & Gambler by the State
line2: Licencd build that Nations Fate

author: William Blake
line1: The Harlots cry from Street to Street
line2: Shall weave Old Englands winding Sheet

line1: The Winners Shout the Losers Curse
line2: Dance before dead Englands Hearse

line1: Every Night & every Morn
line2: Some to Misery are Born

author: William Blake

line2: Some are Born to sweet delight

author: Edgar Allan Poe
line1: Once upon a midnight dreary, while I pondered, weak and weary,
line2: Over many a quaint and curious volume of forgotten lore—

author: Edgar Allan Poe
line1: While I nodded, nearly napping, suddenly there came a tapping,
line2: As of some one gently rapping, rapping at my chamber door.

author: Edgar Allan Poe
line1: “’Tis some visitor,” I muttered, “tapping at my chamber door—
line2: Only this and nothing more.”

author: Edgar Allan Poe
line1: Ah, distinctly I remember it was in the bleak December;
line2: And each separate dying ember wrought its ghost upon the floor.

author: Edgar Allan Poe
line1: Eagerly I wished the morrow;—vainly I had sought to borrow
line2: From my books surcease of sorrow—sorrow for the lost Lenore—

author: Edgar Allan Poe
line1: For the rare and radiant maiden whom the angels name Lenore—
line2: Nameless here for evermore.

author: Edgar Allan Poe
line1: And the silken, sad, uncertain rustling of each purple curtain
line2: Thrilled me—filled me with fantastic terrors never felt before;

author: Edgar Allan Poe
line1: So that now, to still the beating of my heart, I stood repeating
line2: “’Tis some visitor entreating entrance at my chamber door—

author: Edgar Allan Poe
line1: Some late visitor entreating entrance at my chamber door;—
line2: This it is and nothing more.”

author: Edgar Allan Poe
line1: Presently my soul grew stronger; hesitating then no longer,
line2: “Sir,” said I, “or Madam, truly your forgiveness I implore;

author: Edgar Allan Poe
line1: But the fact is I was napping, and so gently you came rapping,
line2: And so faintly you came tapping, tapping at my chamber door,

author: Edgar Allan Poe
line1: That I scarce was sure I heard you”—here I opened wide the door;—
line2: Darkness there and nothing more.

author: Edgar Allan Poe
line1: Deep into that darkness peering, long I stood there wondering,
line2: fearing,

author: Edgar Allan Poe
line1: Doubting, dreaming dreams no mortal ever dared to dream before;
line2: But the silence was unbroken, and the stillness gave no token,

author: Edgar Allan Poe
line1: And the only word there spoken was the whispered word,
line2: “Lenore?”

author: Edgar Allan Poe
line1: This I whispered, and an echo murmured back the word, “Lenore!”—
line2: Merely this and nothing more.

author: Edgar Allan Poe
line1: Back into the chamber turning, all my soul within me burning,
line2: Soon again I heard a tapping somewhat louder than before.

author: Edgar Allan Poe
line1: “Surely,” said I, “surely that is something at my window lattice;
line2: Let me see, then, what thereat is, and this mystery explore—

author: Edgar Allan Poe
line1: Let my heart be still a moment and this mystery explore;—
line2: ’Tis the wind and nothing more!”

author: Edgar Allan Poe
line1: Open here I flung the shutter, when, with many a flirt and flutter,
line2: In there stepped a stately Raven of the saintly days of yore;

author: Edgar Allan Poe
line1: Not the least obeisance made he; not a minute stopped or stayed
line2: he;

author: Edgar Allan Poe
line1: But, with mien of lord or lady, perched above my chamber door—
line2: Perched upon a bust of Pallas just above my chamber door—

author: Edgar Allan Poe
line1: Perched, and sat, and nothing more.
line2: Then this ebony bird beguiling my sad fancy into smiling,

author: Edgar Allan Poe
line1: By the grave and stern decorum of the countenance it wore,
line2: “Though thy crest be shorn and shaven, thou,” I said, “art sure no

author: Edgar Allan Poe
line1: craven,
line2: Ghastly grim and ancient Raven wandering from the Nightly shore—

author: Edgar Allan Poe
line1: Tell me what thy lordly name is on the Night’s Plutonian shore!”
line2: Quoth the Raven “Nevermore.”

author: Edgar Allan Poe
line1: Much I marvelled this ungainly fowl to hear discourse so plainly,
line2: Though its answer little meaning—little relevancy bore;

author: Edgar Allan Poe
line1: For we cannot help agreeing that no living human being
line2: Ever yet was blessed with seeing bird above his chamber door—

author: Edgar Allan Poe
line1: Bird or beast upon the sculptured bust above his chamber door,
line2: With such name as “Nevermore.”

author: Edgar Allan Poe
line1: But the Raven, sitting lonely on the placid bust, spoke only
line2: That one word, as if his soul in that one word he did outpour.

author: Edgar Allan Poe
line1: Nothing farther then he uttered—not a feather then he fluttered—
line2: Till I scarcely more than muttered “Other friends have flown before

author: Edgar Allan Poe
line2: On the morrow he will leave me, as my Hopes have flown before.”

author: Edgar Allan Poe
line1: Then the bird said “Nevermore.”
line2: Startled at the stillness broken by reply so aptly spoken,

author: Edgar Allan Poe
line1: “Doubtless,” said I, “what it utters is its only stock and store
line2: Caught from some unhappy master whom unmerciful Disaster

author: Edgar Allan Poe
line1: Followed fast and followed faster till his songs one burden bore—
line2: Till the dirges of his Hope that melancholy burden bore

author: Edgar Allan Poe
line1: Of ‘Never—nevermore’.”
line2: But the Raven still beguiling all my fancy into smiling,

author: Edgar Allan Poe
line1: Straight I wheeled a cushioned seat in front of bird, and bust and
line2: door;

author: Edgar Allan Poe
line1: Then, upon the velvet sinking, I betook myself to linking
line2: Fancy unto fancy, thinking what this ominous bird of yore—

author: Edgar Allan Poe
line1: What this grim, ungainly, ghastly, gaunt, and ominous bird of yore
line2: Meant in croaking “Nevermore.”

author: Edgar Allan Poe
line1: This I sat engaged in guessing, but no syllable expressing
line2: To the fowl whose fiery eyes now burned into my bosom’s core;

author: Edgar Allan Poe
line1: This and more I sat divining, with my head at ease reclining
line2: On the cushion’s velvet lining that the lamp-light gloated o’er,

author: Edgar Allan Poe
line1: But whose velvet-violet lining with the lamp-light gloating o’er,
line2: She shall press, ah, nevermore!

author: Edgar Allan Poe
line1: Then, methought, the air grew denser, perfumed from an unseen
line2: censer

author: Edgar Allan Poe

author: Edgar Allan Poe

author: John Keats

author: John Keats

author: John Keats

author: John Keats

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: PART 1.
line2: A Sensitive Plant in a garden grew,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: And the young winds fed it with silver dew,
line2: And it opened its fan-like leaves to the light.

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: And closed them beneath the kisses of Night.
line2: And the Spring arose on the garden fair,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Like the Spirit of Love felt everywhere;
line2: And each flower and herb on Earth’s dark breast

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Rose from the dreams of its wintry rest.
line2: But none ever trembled and panted with bliss

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: In the garden, the field, or the wilderness,
line2: Like a doe in the noontide with love’s sweet want,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: As the companionless Sensitive Plant.
line2: The snowdrop, and then the violet,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Arose from the ground with warm rain wet,
line2: And their breath was mixed with fresh odour, sent

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: From the turf, like the voice and the instrument.
line2: Then the pied wind-flowers and the tulip tall,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: And narcissi, the fairest among them all,
line2: Who gaze on their eyes in the stream’s recess,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Till they die of their own dear loveliness;
line2: And the Naiad-like lily of the vale,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Whom youth makes so fair and passion so pale
line2: That the light of its tremulous bells is seen

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Through their pavilions of tender green;
line2: And the hyacinth purple, and white, and blue,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Which flung from its bells a sweet peal anew
line2: Of music so delicate, soft, and intense,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: It was felt like an odour within the sense;
line2: And the rose like a nymph to the bath addressed,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Which unveiled the depth of her glowing breast,
line2: Till, fold after fold, to the fainting air

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: The soul of her beauty and love lay bare:
line2: And the wand-like lily, which lifted up,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: As a Maenad, its moonlight-coloured cup,
line2: Till the fiery star, which is its eye,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Gazed through clear dew on the tender sky;
line2: And the jessamine faint, and the sweet tuberose,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: The sweetest flower for scent that blows;
line2: And all rare blossoms from every clime

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Grew in that garden in perfect prime.
line2: And on the stream whose inconstant bosom

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Was pranked, under boughs of embowering blossom,
line2: With golden and green light, slanting through

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Their heaven of many a tangled hue,
line2: Broad water-lilies lay tremulously,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: And starry river-buds glimmered by,
line2: And around them the soft stream did glide and dance

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: With a motion of sweet sound and radiance.
line2: And the sinuous paths of lawn and of moss,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Which led through the garden along and across,
line2: Some open at once to the sun and the breeze,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Some lost among bowers of blossoming trees,
line2: Were all paved with daisies and delicate bells

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: As fair as the fabulous asphodels,
line2: And flow’rets which, drooping as day drooped too,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Fell into pavilions, white, purple, and blue,
line2: To roof the glow-worm from the evening dew.

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: And from this undefiled Paradise
line2: The flowers (as an infant’s awakening eyes

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Smile on its mother, whose singing sweet
line2: Can first lull, and at last must awaken it),

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: When Heaven’s blithe winds had unfolded them,
line2: As mine-lamps enkindle a hidden gem,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Shone smiling to Heaven, and every one
line2: Shared joy in the light of the gentle sun;

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: For each one was interpenetrated
line2: With the light and the odour its neighbour shed,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Like young lovers whom youth and love make dear
line2: Wrapped and filled by their mutual atmosphere.

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: But the Sensitive Plant which could give small fruit
line2: Of the love which it felt from the leaf to the root,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Received more than all, it loved more than ever,
line2: Where none wanted but it, could belong to the giver,—

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: For the Sensitive Plant has no bright flower;
line2: Radiance and odour are not its dower;

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: It loves, even like Love, its deep heart is full,
line2: It desires what it has not, the Beautiful!

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: The light winds which from unsustaining wings
line2: Shed the music of many murmurings;

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: The beams which dart from many a star
line2: Of the flowers whose hues they bear afar;

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: The plumed insects swift and free,
line2: Like golden boats on a sunny sea,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Laden with light and odour, which pass
line2: Over the gleam of the living grass;

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: The unseen clouds of the dew, which lie
line2: Like fire in the flowers till the sun rides high,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Then wander like spirits among the spheres,
line2: Each cloud faint with the fragrance it bears;

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: The quivering vapours of dim noontide,
line2: Which like a sea o’er the warm earth glide,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: In which every sound, and odour, and beam,
line2: Move, as reeds in a single stream;

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Each and all like ministering angels were
line2: For the Sensitive Plant sweet joy to bear,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Whilst the lagging hours of the day went by
line2: Like windless clouds o’er a tender sky.

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: And when evening descended from Heaven above,
line2: And the Earth was all rest, and the air was all love,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: And delight, though less bright, was far more deep,
line2: And the day’s veil fell from the world of sleep,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: And the beasts, and the birds, and the insects were drowned
line2: In an ocean of dreams without a sound;

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Whose waves never mark, though they ever impress
line2: The light sand which paves it, consciousness;

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: (Only overhead the sweet nightingale
line2: Ever sang more sweet as the day might fail,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: And snatches of its Elysian chant
line2: Were mixed with the dreams of the Sensitive Plant);–

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: The Sensitive Plant was the earliest
line2: Upgathered into the bosom of rest;

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: A sweet child weary of its delight,
line2: The feeblest and yet the favourite,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Cradled within the embrace of Night.
line2: PART 2.

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: There was a Power in this sweet place,
line2: An Eve in this Eden; a ruling Grace

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Which to the flowers, did they waken or dream,
line2: Was as God is to the starry scheme.

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: A Lady, the wonder of her kind,
line2: Whose form was upborne by a lovely mind

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Which, dilating, had moulded her mien and motion
line2: Like a sea-flower unfolded beneath the ocean,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Tended the garden from morn to even:
line2: And the meteors of that sublunar Heaven,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Like the lamps of the air when Night walks forth,
line2: Laughed round her footsteps up from the Earth!

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: She had no companion of mortal race,
line2: But her tremulous breath and her flushing face

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Told, whilst the morn kissed the sleep from her eyes,
line2: That her dreams were less slumber than Paradise:

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: As if some bright Spirit for her sweet sake
line2: Had deserted Heaven while the stars were awake,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: As if yet around her he lingering were,
line2: Though the veil of daylight concealed him from her.

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Her step seemed to pity the grass it pressed;
line2: You might hear by the heaving of her breast,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: That the coming and going of the wind
line2: Brought pleasure there and left passion behind.

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: And wherever her aery footstep trod,
line2: Her trailing hair from the grassy sod

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Erased its light vestige, with shadowy sweep,
line2: Like a sunny storm o’er the dark green deep.

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: I doubt not the flowers of that garden sweet
line2: Rejoiced in the sound of her gentle feet;

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: I doubt not they felt the spirit that came
line2: From her glowing fingers through all their frame.

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: She sprinkled bright water from the stream
line2: On those that were faint with the sunny beam;

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: And out of the cups of the heavy flowers
line2: She emptied the rain of the thunder-showers.

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: She lifted their heads with her tender hands,
line2: And sustained them with rods and osier-bands;

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: If the flowers had been her own infants, she
line2: Could never have nursed them more tenderly.

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: And all killing insects and gnawing worms,
line2: And things of obscene and unlovely forms,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: She bore, in a basket of Indian woof,
line2: Into the rough woods far aloof,–

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: In a basket, of grasses and wild-flowers full,
line2: The freshest her gentle hands could pull

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: For the poor banished insects, whose intent,
line2: Although they did ill, was innocent.

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: But the bee and the beamlike ephemeris
line2: Whose path is the lightning’s, and soft moths that kiss

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: The sweet lips of the flowers, and harm not, did she
line2: Make her attendant angels be.

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: And many an antenatal tomb,
line2: Where butterflies dream of the life to come,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: She left clinging round the smooth and dark
line2: Edge of the odorous cedar bark.

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: This fairest creature from earliest Spring
line2: Thus moved through the garden ministering

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Mi the sweet season of Summertide,
line2: And ere the first leaf looked brown—she died!

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: She floats up through the smoke of Vesuvius.

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Felt the sound of the funeral chant,
line2: And the steps of the bearers, heavy and slow,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: And the sobs of the mourners, deep and low;
line2: The weary sound and the heavy breath,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: And the silent motions of passing death,
line2: And the smell, cold, oppressive, and dank,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Sent through the pores of the coffin-plank;
line2: The dark grass, and the flowers among the grass,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Were bright with tears as the crowd did pass;
line2: From their sighs the wind caught a mournful tone,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: And sate in the pines, and gave groan for groan.
line2: The garden, once fair, became cold and foul,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Like the corpse of her who had been its soul,
line2: Which at first was lovely as if in sleep,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Then slowly changed, till it grew a heap
line2: To make men tremble who never weep.

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Swift Summer into the Autumn flowed,
line2: And frost in the mist of the morning rode,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Though the noonday sun looked clear and bright,
line2: Mocking the spoil of the secret night.

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: The rose-leaves, like flakes of crimson snow,
line2: Paved the turf and the moss below.

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: The lilies were drooping, and white, and wan,
line2: Like the head and the skin of a dying man.

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: And Indian plants, of scent and hue
line2: The sweetest that ever were fed on dew,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Leaf by leaf, day after day,
line2: Were massed into the common clay.

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: And the leaves, brown, yellow, and gray, and red,
line2: And white with the whiteness of what is dead,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Like troops of ghosts on the dry wind passed;
line2: Their whistling noise made the birds aghast.

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: And the gusty winds waked the winged seeds,
line2: Out of their birthplace of ugly weeds,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Till they clung round many a sweet flower’s stem,
line2: Which rotted into the earth with them.

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: The water-blooms under the rivulet
line2: Fell from the stalks on which they were set;

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: And the eddies drove them here and there,
line2: As the winds did those of the upper air.

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Then the rain came down, and the broken stalks
line2: Were bent and tangled across the walks;

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: And the leafless network of parasite bowers
line2: Massed into ruin; and all sweet flowers.

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Between the time of the wind and the snow
line2: All loathliest weeds began to grow,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Whose coarse leaves were splashed with many a speck,
line2: Like the water-snake’s belly and the toad’s back.

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: And thistles, and nettles, and darnels rank,
line2: And the dock, and henbane, and hemlock dank,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Stretched out its long and hollow shank,
line2: And stifled the air till the dead wind stank.

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: And plants, at whose names the verse feels loath,
line2: Filled the place with a monstrous undergrowth,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Prickly, and pulpous, and blistering, and blue,
line2: Livid, and starred with a lurid dew.

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: And agarics, and fungi, with mildew and mould
line2: Started like mist from the wet ground cold;

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Pale, fleshy, as if the decaying dead
line2: With a spirit of growth had been animated!

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Spawn, weeds, and filth, a leprous scum,
line2: Made the running rivulet thick and dumb,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: And at its outlet flags huge as stakes
line2: Dammed it up with roots knotted like water-snakes.

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: And hour by hour, when the air was still,
line2: The vapours arose which have strength to kill;

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: At morn they were seen, at noon they were felt,
line2: At night they were darkness no star could melt.

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: And unctuous meteors from spray to spray
line2: Crept and flitted in broad noonday

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Unseen; every branch on which they alit
line2: By a venomous blight was burned and bit.

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: The Sensitive Plant, like one forbid,
line2: Wept, and the tears within each lid

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Of its folded leaves, which together grew,
line2: Were changed to a blight of frozen glue.

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: For the leaves soon fell, and the branches soon
line2: By the heavy axe of the blast were hewn;

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: The sap shrank to the root through every pore
line2: As blood to a heart that will beat no more.

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: For Winter came: the wind was his whip:
line2: One choppy finger was on his lip:

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: He had torn the cataracts from the hills
line2: And they clanked at his girdle like manacles;

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: His breath was a chain which without a sound
line2: The earth, and the air, and the water bound;

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: He came, fiercely driven, in his chariot-throne
line2: By the tenfold blasts of the Arctic zone.

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Then the weeds which were forms of living death
line2: Fled from the frost to the earth beneath.

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Their decay and sudden flight from frost
line2: Was but like the vanishing of a ghost!

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: And under the roots of the Sensitive Plant
line2: The moles and the dormice died for want:

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: The birds dropped stiff from the frozen air
line2: And were caught in the branches naked and bare.

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: First there came down a thawing rain
line2: And its dull drops froze on the boughs again;

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Then there steamed up a freezing dew
line2: Which to the drops of the thaw-rain grew;

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: And a northern whirlwind, wandering about
line2: Like a wolf that had smelt a dead child out,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Shook the boughs thus laden, and heavy, and stiff,
line2: And snapped them off with his rigid griff.

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: When Winter had gone and Spring came back
line2: The Sensitive Plant was a leafless wreck;

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: But the mandrakes, and toadstools, and docks, and darnels,
line2: Rose like the dead from their ruined charnels.

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: CONCLUSION.
line2: Whether the Sensitive Plant, or that

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Which within its boughs like a Spirit sat,
line2: Ere its outward form had known decay,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Now felt this change, I cannot say.
line2: Whether that Lady’s gentle mind,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: No longer with the form combined
line2: Which scattered love, as stars do light,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Found sadness, where it left delight,
line2: I dare not guess; but in this life

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Of error, ignorance, and strife,
line2: Where nothing is, but all things seem,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: And we the shadows of the dream,
line2: It is a modest creed, and yet

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Pleasant if one considers it,
line2: To own that death itself must be,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Like all the rest, a mockery.
line2: That garden sweet, that lady fair,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: And all sweet shapes and odours there,
line2: In truth have never passed away:

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: ’Tis we, ’tis ours, are changed; not they.
line2: For love, and beauty, and delight,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: There is no death nor change: their might
line2: Exceeds our organs, which endure

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: I shot an arrow into the air,
line2: It fell to earth, I knew not where;

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: For, so swiftly it flew, the sight
line2: Could not follow it in its flight.

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: I breathed a song into the air,
line2: It fell to earth, I knew not where;

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: For who has sight so keen and strong,
line2: That it can follow the flight of song?

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: Long, long afterward, in an oak
line2: I found the arrow, still unbroke;

author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
line1: And the song, from beginning to end,
line2: I found again in the heart of a friend.

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: I
line2: O wild West Wind, thou breath of Autumn’s being,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Thou, from whose unseen presence the leaves dead
line2: Are driven, like ghosts from an enchanter fleeing,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Yellow, and black, and pale, and hectic red,
line2: Pestilence-stricken multitudes: O thou,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Who chariotest to their dark wintry bed
line2: The winged seeds, where they lie cold and low,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Each like a corpse within its grave, until
line2: Thine azure sister of the Spring shall blow

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Her clarion o’er the dreaming earth, and fill
line2: (Driving sweet buds like flocks to feed in air)

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: With living hues and odours plain and hill:
line2: Wild Spirit, which art moving everywhere;

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Destroyer and preserver; hear, oh hear!
line2: II

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Thou on whose stream, mid the steep sky’s commotion,
line2: Loose clouds like earth’s decaying leaves are shed,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Shook from the tangled boughs of Heaven and Ocean,
line2: Angels of rain and lightning: there are spread

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: On the blue surface of thine aëry surge,
line2: Like the bright hair uplifted from the head

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Of some fierce Maenad, even from the dim verge
line2: Of the horizon to the zenith’s height,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: The locks of the approaching storm. Thou dirge
line2: Of the dying year, to which this closing night

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Will be the dome of a vast sepulchre,
line2: Vaulted with all thy congregated might

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Of vapours, from whose solid atmosphere
line2: Black rain, and fire, and hail will burst: oh hear!

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: III
line2: Thou who didst waken from his summer dreams

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: The blue Mediterranean, where he lay,
line2: Lull’d by the coil of his crystalline streams,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Beside a pumice isle in Baiae’s bay,
line2: And saw in sleep old palaces and towers

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Quivering within the wave’s intenser day,
line2: All overgrown with azure moss and flowers

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: So sweet, the sense faints picturing them! Thou
line2: For whose path the Atlantic’s level powers

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Cleave themselves into chasms, while far below
line2: The sea-blooms and the oozy woods which wear

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: The sapless foliage of the ocean, know
line2: Thy voice, and suddenly grow gray with fear,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: And tremble and despoil themselves: oh hear!
line2: IV

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: If I were a dead leaf thou mightest bear;
line2: If I were a swift cloud to fly with thee;

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: A wave to pant beneath thy power, and share
line2: The impulse of thy strength, only less free

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Than thou, O uncontrollable! If even
line2: I were as in my boyhood, and could be

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: The comrade of thy wanderings over Heaven,
line2: As then, when to outstrip thy skiey speed

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Scarce seem’d a vision; I would ne’er have striven
line2: As thus with thee in prayer in my sore need.

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Oh, lift me as a wave, a leaf, a cloud!
line2: I fall upon the thorns of life! I bleed!

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: A heavy weight of hours has chain’d and bow’d
line2: One too like thee: tameless, and swift, and proud.

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: V
line2: Make me thy lyre, even as the forest is:

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: What if my leaves are falling like its own!
line2: The tumult of thy mighty harmonies

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Will take from both a deep, autumnal tone,
line2: Sweet though in sadness. Be thou, Spirit fierce,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: My spirit! Be thou me, impetuous one!
line2: Drive my dead thoughts over the universe

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Like wither’d leaves to quicken a new birth!
line2: And, by the incantation of this verse,

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Scatter, as from an unextinguish’d hearth
line2: Ashes and sparks, my words among mankind!

author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
line1: Be through my lips to unawaken’d earth
line2: The trumpet of a prophecy! O Wind,

author: Rudyard Kipling
line1: When the Himalayan peasant meets the he-bear in his pride,
line2: He shouts to scare the monster, who will often turn aside.

author: Rudyard Kipling
line1: But the she-bear thus accosted rends the peasant tooth and nail,
line2: For the female of the species is more deadly than the male.

author: Rudyard Kipling
line1: When Nag the basking cobra hears the careless foot of man,

author: Rudyard Kipling

line2: trail -

line1: For the female of the species is more deadly than the male.

author: Rudyard Kipling

author: Rudyard Kipling
line1: Man’s timid heart is bursting with the things he must not say,

author: Rudyard Kipling
line1: But when hunter meets with husband, each confirms the others tale -
line2: The female of the species is more deadly than the male.

author: Rudyard Kipling
line1: Man, a bear in most relations, worm and savage otherwise,
line2: Man propounds negotiations, Man accepts the compromise;

author: Rudyard Kipling
line1: Very rarely will he squarely push the logic of a fact
line2: To its ultimate conclusion in unmitigated act.

author: Rudyard Kipling
line1: Fear, or foolishness, impels him, ere he lay the wicked low,
line2: To concede some form of trial even to his fiercest foe.

author: Rudyard Kipling
line1: Mirth obscene diverts his anger; Doubt and Pity oft perplex
line2: Him in dealing with an issue - to the scandal of the Sex!

author: Rudyard Kipling
line1: But the Woman that God gave him, every fibre of her frame
line2: Proves her launched for one sole issue, armed and engined for the

author: Rudyard Kipling
line1: same,
line2: And to serve that single issue, lest the generations fail,

author: Rudyard Kipling
line1: The female of the species must be deadlier than the male.
line2: She who faces Death by torture for each life beneath her breast

author: Rudyard Kipling
line1: May not deal in doubt or pity - must not swerve for fact or jest.
line2: These be purely male diversions - not in these her honor dwells -

author: Rudyard Kipling
line1: She, the Other Law we live by, is that Law and nothing else!
line2: She can bring no more to living than the powers that make her great

author: Rudyard Kipling
line1: As the Mother of the Infant and the Mistress of the Mate;

author: Rudyard Kipling
line1: claim
line2: Her right as femme (and baron), her equipment is the same.

author: Rudyard Kipling
line1: She is wedded to convictions - in default of grosser ties;
line2: Her contentions are her children, Heaven help him, who denies!

author: Rudyard Kipling
line1: He will meet no cool discussion, but the instant, white-hot wild
line2: Wakened female of the species warring as for spouse and child.

author: Rudyard Kipling
line1: Unprovoked and awful charges - even so the she-bear fights;
line2: Speech that drips, corrodes and poisons - even so the cobra bites;

author: Rudyard Kipling
line1: Scientific vivisection of one nerve till it is raw,
line2: And the victim writhes with anguish - like the Jesuit with the squaw!

author: Rudyard Kipling
line1: So it comes that Man, the coward, when he gathers to confer

author: Rudyard Kipling
line1: Where, at war with Life and Conscience, he uplifts his erring hands

author: Rudyard Kipling
line1: High hopes faint on a warm hearth-stone–

author: Rudyard Kipling

line2: Falls by himself, with himself to blame;

author: Rudyard Kipling
